A former Japanese school principal named Yuhei Takashima, 64, is facing criminal charges after Tokyo police arrested him for allegedly photographing obscene acts involving a minor and producing pornography in Manila. According to police, Takashima confessed to paying for sex with over 12,000 women, some as young as 14, during repeated visits to the Philippines over more than 25 years, with investigators seizing 147,600 photos documenting his activities. Takashima told police he began buying sex in 1988 while working at a Japanese school in Manila and continued the practice frequently afterward, citing cheap prices and relief from work pressure as reasons for his actions.

A Brazilian online football store has published three alleged new Chelsea kits featuring the incoming 'Yokohama' sponsor on the front, though notably without the Adidas emblem—the club's official kit manufacturer. Most notably, the website is selling the new Chelsea kit with 'Bale 9' on the back, fueling speculation about Gareth Bale's potential move from Real Madrid to Chelsea, while versions without Bale's name remain available for purchase. The kits are listed despite the unofficial nature of the website and uncertainties surrounding several players linked to the club.

Clarence Taylor, 44, is on the run after police in Akron, Ohio charged him with faking his own kidnapping in a bizarre incident last fall, allegedly by duct taping his mouth and binding himself to a tree. On November 1, Taylor's 45-year-old girlfriend called police to report him missing, but he was soon found tied to the tree by a friend; Taylor claimed he had been abducted by armed men and robbed of $2,500, though police found his story suspicious due to his lack of injuries or signs of dehydration. Taylor has been charged with first-degree misdemeanor falsification, and a warrant was issued for his arrest, but he currently remains at large.

Rita Wilson and an ensemble of actresses star in a satirical video created by the Make it Fair Project that uses irony to promote gender equality, sarcastically proclaiming "It's only fair that men should have it all" while highlighting statistics showing that males hold 70 to 95 percent of the most influential positions in Hollywood and other industries. The PSA presents eye-opening data about how men completely dominate film directing, writing, and leading roles, as well as government and Fortune 500 companies, emphasizing that women comprise nearly 60 percent of college graduates yet earn significantly less—a message Wilson underscored weeks after revealing her own battle with cancer. Created entirely by an all-female crew, the video demonstrates the abundance of talented women in the industry whose potential often remains untapped.

# Summary

Spanish researchers have found evidence that a warming climate around 1.4 million years ago enabled our distant human ancestors, known as hominins, to migrate from Africa into Western Europe for the first time, with the Barranco León site in southeast Spain revealing Oldowan tools used by these earliest known Europeans. The study suggests that colder and drier conditions had previously prevented hominin settlement in the region, but when temperatures warmed to 13°C (55°F) and humidity increased, these early ancestors were able to spread across Europe and scavenge carcasses using simple stone tools. The research demonstrates that climate change significantly impacted the availability of resources and environmental conditions, directly constraining early human migration patterns rather than cultural or geographical factors.

Former NFL running back and Nebraska standout Lawrence Phillips, imprisoned since 2005 for choking his girlfriend and hitting three teens with his car, is suspected of killing his cellmate Damion Soward at Kern Valley State Prison in Central California, prison officials revealed. Soward, a 37-year-old inmate serving 82 years to life for murder, was found lifeless in the cell he shared with Phillips on Saturday morning, according to prison spokesman Lt. Marshall Denning. Prison officials are investigating the death along with the Kern County Coroner and District Attorney, and a second cellmate killing at the same facility is also under investigation.

Liverpool's FA Cup semi-final defeat to Aston Villa, sealed by Fabian Delph's second-half goal, has cast doubt on the club's ambitions to become Champions League regulars and compete for the Barclays Premier League title, with manager Brendan Rodgers facing criticism for his tactical indecision throughout the match. The loss highlighted Liverpool's lack of character and consistency, as Rodgers repeatedly changed formations without establishing rhythm, while only three of 17 expensive signings over two summers have delivered results alongside ongoing contract disputes including a new deal standoff with Raheem Sterling. With Steven Gerrard departing and a squad in upheaval, Liverpool faces a pivotal summer requiring significant rebuilding to avoid mediocrity.

Google has combined Google Maps with Pac-Man for an impish April Fools' Day prank, rolling out Tuesday to allow users to play the classic game on real-world maps. Simply pulling up Google Maps on desktop and clicking the Pac-Man icon transforms any location into a playable Pac-Man course, with users already enjoying the feature in Manhattan, London, and San Francisco. This playful addition continues Google's tradition of creative April Fools' pranks, following previous Map-based games like a Pokemon Challenge and treasure map.

Rangers boss Stuart McCall has been reassured that the club's de-listing from the London Stock Exchange's AIM market—with a potential switch to ISDX under consideration—will not impact the team's promotion push, despite the interim chairman blaming the previous board for failing to secure a new nominated advisor and the threat of legal action from former football board chairman Sandy Easdale. McCall stated he is focusing solely on football matters rather than the club's mounting financial pressures, including six-month losses of £2.6 million and a surprise £500,000 payment owed to Newcastle United if Rangers win promotion through their loan agreement. The Rangers manager remains concentrated on Sunday's match against Hearts, where his side will pay tribute to the newly-crowned Scottish Championship winners.

ICANN, the US body regulating Internet addresses, is cracking down on Canadian company Vox Populi Registry Ltd. for allegedly running a "shakedown scheme" by charging celebrities and major brands like Kevin Spacey and Taylor Swift exorbitant fees (around $2,000-$2,499 annually) to remove their names from '.sucks' domains—which include Youtube.sucks, Bing.sucks, and other major brand registrations since March 30—or from offensive extensions like '.porn' that have targeted celebrities in the past. The advisory panel claims Vox Populi's business model is coercive, threatening to offer consumers these domains for just $10 per year starting in September, thereby pressuring companies to pay premium prices to protect their reputations. ICANN has referred the matter to U.S. and Canadian regulators to determine if Vox Populi's actions violate laws, and could declare the company in breach of contract if illegal activity is found.

Manchester United's slim chances of signing Uruguayan striker Edinson Cavani to fill their looming Old Trafford void may well have evaporated after his slumbering Champions League performance saw PSG lose 2-0 to Barcelona on Tuesday night. Manager Louis van Gaal, already burned by Radamel Falcao's failed loan from Monaco, prefers younger talent and appears further deterred by Cavani's inconsistent Ligue 1 form since his £55m move from Napoli. Cavani has since declared his intention to remain at PSG and honor his contract, leaving him few alternatives elsewhere.

With seven points separating 15th-place Sunderland from bottom-dweller Leicester, the fight for Premier League survival remains extremely competitive, featuring five key fixtures between bottom-six sides over their remaining seven games. While Sunderland and Aston Villa are predicted to survive, Steve Bruce's Hull face the hardest run-in and could finish safely only on goal difference, with Burnley and Leicester predicted to be relegated despite Leicester's advantage of five home games remaining. The outcome will likely hinge on how teams capitalize against opponents with little left to play for, such as Southampton and Everton, where early points could prove decisive.

Real Madrid star Karim Benzema posted a video showcasing his rapping skills while singing along to Tupac's classic track "Check Out Time" from his Bugatti Veyron as the Los Blancos prepare for their La Liga clash against Granada at the Bernabeu. The France forward, who has scored 20 goals this season, boldly stated earlier this week that he believes he can win the Ballon d'Or award in the future if he continues to perform at Real Madrid and international level, despite the dominance of his Real team-mate Cristiano Ronaldo and Barcelona's Lionel Messi in recent years.

Pope Francis, the 78-year-old Catholic leader, has been advised by Vatican doctors to lose weight and reduce his pasta intake to two servings a week, as his considerable weight gain since taking office two years ago is aggravating his sciatica, a nerve condition causing back pain. The doctors have recommended he take daily walks like his predecessor Pope Benedict XVI did to address his sedentary lifestyle, though the Pope has largely ignored their warnings and continues to eat pasta daily. Despite having suggested his papacy would not last long, Francis jokingly refers to himself as "the undisciplined one" and shows little commitment to following the medical advice.

Google has released an update to its Android software that allows phone owners to unlock their secured devices using voice recognition through a "Trusted Voice" feature accessible via smart lock settings that activates when users say "OK, Google," though the company warns this method is less secure than traditional passwords. Additionally, Google offers Face Unlock, available on Android phones running Ice Cream Sandwich or higher through the Screen Lock settings, which uses the device's front-facing camera to capture and store an encrypted facial image that is cross-referenced each time a user attempts to unlock their phone. Both features provide alternatives to traditional PIN and pattern locks, though they require backup security measures due to their limitations in poor lighting conditions or security vulnerabilities.

Robert Bates' lawyer released training documents on Saturday claiming the 73-year-old Oklahoma reserve deputy met all required qualifications, including one Taser training class and documents showing Bates scoring high enough on firearms qualifications from 2009 to 2014 to carry a handgun. However, the records have raised questions, including missing firearms qualification entries from four dates and an unusual concentration of 14-20 training classes taken on single days in 2013 and 2014—which Wood said Bates may have completed as computer-based courses finished quickly. The documents contradict allegations from Eric Harris's family lawyer that Bates received preferential treatment and wasn't properly qualified, though the Tulsa World stands by its reporting that the Tulsa County Sheriff's Office requires 480 hours of field training for an "advanced" reserve deputy.

Peter Tait, headmaster of Sherborne Preparatory School, has claimed that parents have become "dervishes ready to battle with anyone and anything on behalf of their child" and should instead trust schools and teachers, warning that such interference could hinder natural development. A survey by teachers' union NASUWT of 1,500 teachers revealed that abuse has more than doubled in the past year to 60 percent, with families increasingly using social media to post threats and obscenities targeting staff. Education leaders, also attacking parents for putting children before all others despite their natural desire for the best outcomes, warn that this interference and intimidation undermines teachers' ability to do their jobs effectively.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu criticized the nuclear deal struck by six world powers to limit Iran's nuclear program, telling CNN's Jim Acosta on "State of the Union" Sunday that he believes there is a better alternative than accepting "this bad deal or war" and that standing firm could lead to improved terms. Netanyahu argued that the deal fails to roll back Iran's nuclear infrastructure, keeps thousands of centrifuges operational, and cannot be trusted given Iran's complete lack of trustworthiness, prompting Democratic Senator Dianne Feinstein to say he needs to "contain himself." While President Obama and Senator Feinstein defended the framework—which would reduce Iran's centrifuges from 19,000 to 5,060 and extend the timeline to build a nuclear bomb—Republican leaders like Senator Lindsey Graham countered that maintaining current sanctions and negotiating with a new president could yield better results.

Catalans Dragons winger Vincent Duport has been ruled out for the rest of the season after suffering a ruptured tendon in his shoulder during their defeat to Hull FC on March 20, requiring surgery. The France international, who had scored three tries in the first six rounds of Super League XX, dealt a hugely disappointing blow to the sixth-placed Dragons. Head coach Laurent Frayssinous acknowledged the setback but expressed confidence that Duport, who has bounced back from big injuries before, will recover again with the team's full support.

Samantha Fleming, a young Indiana woman, was reported missing weeks ago after a woman posing as a Department of Child Services official told Fleming she needed to attend a hearing and left with her three-week-old daughter Serenity from their Anderson home. Police found Fleming's body wrapped in plastic and doused with bleach in a storage bin at a residence in Gary, 180 miles away, after tracing her cell phone signal to the city. The newborn daughter was discovered unharmed at the same home, and a woman believed to be the alleged kidnapper is currently a person of interest being held at a hospital in Texas.

Geologist Dr Danny Hilman believes that Gunung Padang, a megalithic site in West Java, Indonesia, is an ancient pyramid built between 9,000 and 20,000 years ago—potentially making it four times older than Egypt's oldest pyramid, the step pyramid of Djoser built around 2,700 BC, and far predating even the Brazilian pyramids from around 3,000 BC. If authenticated, the discovery would prove the existence of an advanced ancient civilization in Java and rewrite prehistoric history, though his controversial findings are disputed by 34 Indonesian archaeologists and geologists who question his methods and the site's interpretation. The site, nestled among volcanoes and tea plantations, is covered with volcanic rubble and stepped terraces that Dr Hilman suggests may have functioned as an ancient temple for worship or astronomy, with some rocks carbon-dated to around 7,000 BC and man-made structures detected up to 49 feet underground.

Two separate stabbing attacks on bouncers at Melbourne nightclubs bracketed the Easter long weekend, with police expressing disgust over the violence. Late Thursday night, a 29-year-old North Melbourne security guard was stabbed in the back by a man he hadn't ejected from the club, suffering non-life threatening injuries requiring hospitalization. Early Good Friday, another bouncer was slashed in the leg after refusing entry to a 24-year-old man, leading to a confrontation at Collins Street where the attacker sustained serious injuries.

# Summary

Carlisle United manager Keith Curle launched a scathing attack on his players following their dismal 3-1 defeat to Accrington Stanley, suggesting some "don't deserve to be professionals" and criticizing the lack of spirit and character in the changing room. The Cumbrians, who were relegated from League One last season, are now just two places and three points above the relegation zone in League Two with five games remaining. Curle even suggested staging an open forum with supporters to confront the players about their lack of commitment to the shirt.

Victoria's Secret has launched its new Dream Angels lingerie campaign featuring supermodels Candice Swanepoel, Elsa Hosk, and Jasmine Tookes posing in see-through macs with the brand's romantic collection of blush pink and white lingerie, complete with complimentary pink striped umbrellas for customers who purchase two bras. Candice leads the glamorous shoot showcasing the Dream Angels push-up bras and lace-trimmed styles available at retailers like Topshop and DKNY Intimates as everyday essentials you can get now. In addition to the lingerie campaign, Candice recently appeared as Marilyn Monroe in Max Factor's beauty campaign, continuing her successful week in the fashion and beauty industries.

Wannabe singer Kristina Karo is suing actress Mila Kunis for $5,000, claiming that Kunis stole her pet chicken named "Doggie" 25 years ago when they were children in Ukraine, an incident Karo states traumatized her and forced her into therapy. According to Karo's allegations, the envious young Kunis confessed to taking the hen from her whole chicken farm and offered her another bird instead. The lawsuit appears questionable, as records show the two girls lived more than 100 miles apart in Ukraine, and Karo has only recently moved to Los Angeles near Kunis, which she claims has revived the painful memories.

Richard Howarth, a 35-year-old medical recruitment consultant from Milton Keynes, claims he experiences severe physical reactions—sweating, shaking, and feeling physically sick—whenever he sees Ukip leader Nigel Farage on television, a response he initially developed while trying to educate himself on the party's policies. His wife Catherine confirms that hearing Farage's voice makes her husband anxious and irritable with trembling hands, though the symptoms typically ease once he changes the channel. A psychologist explains that Mr. Howarth's reactions are likely indicative of a phobia rather than a true allergy, possibly rooted in his fundamental disagreement with Ukip and concerns about their impact on the UK.

# Summary

A drunk 17-year-old boy named Rahul Kumar climbed over the enclosure fence at Kamla Nehru Zoological Park in Ahmedabad, India, and ran toward the lions shouting that he would "kill a lion or a lion kills me." Fortunately, Kumar fell into a moat while running toward the animals, allowing zoo security to rescue him before he reached the lions, which were asleep at the time. Kumar later explained he was intoxicated and thought he could fight the predators, and he has been handed over to police for psychiatric evaluation.

A nutritionist warns that popular Easter eggs contain excessive sugar—for example, one Crème egg contains nearly seven teaspoons of sugar and takes 19 minutes of skipping to burn off its 177 calories, while a Kit Kat Chunky Easter egg requires three 45-minute spin classes burning 300 calories each to work off its 836 calories, and a 90g packet of Cadbury's mini eggs demands 50 minutes of Zumba to burn 444 calories. Many Easter eggs exceed the WHO's recommended daily sugar intake of 50g, and consuming too much sugar could trigger insulin resistance, leading to obesity, type 2 diabetes, nutrient deficiencies, and behavioral issues. Dr. Sam Christie recommends children eat smaller solid chocolate eggs rather than ones with sugary fillings, and maintain a healthy diet year-round to protect against the damaging effects of excess sugar consumption.

The secretive Havasupai tribe, America's smallest Indian nation with over 600 inhabitants, operates the hidden village of Supai deep within Havasu Canyon in the Grand Canyon National Park, which they claimed from National Park rangers in 1975 after a long legal battle. Situated eight miles from the nearest road and accessible only by foot, helicopter, or mule, this remote canyon settlement relies heavily on tourism income from approximately 20,000 annual visitors drawn to experience the tribe's culture and the spectacular Havasu Falls. The Havasupai people also sustain themselves through traditional practices such as farming and weaving, while maintaining their sacred connection to the Grand Canyon landscape.

Scientists have developed RTS,S, a new jab and the first malaria vaccine to reach advanced trials after 30 years of development, which showed it could reduce clinical malaria cases by 36 percent in vaccinated children receiving a booster dose over four years. Trials involving over 15,000 children in sub-Saharan Africa demonstrated that the vaccine could prevent millions of cases of the world's deadliest disease, though its effectiveness declined over time and was less effective in younger children. If approved by the European Medicines Agency, RTS,S could help address the estimated 198 million annual malaria cases and potentially save millions of lives, particularly among African children who currently account for most malaria deaths.

Chilean pickpockets have emerged as the most effective thieves operating in central London, according to undercover police teams working to reduce theft in the area, with gangs of three to six spotted targeting wealthy tourists—particularly rich Arab visitors in Park Lane and Mayfair—as they follow shoppers through London's busy commercial districts. One example saw Metropolitan Police stop two Chilean women wearing burkas carrying €130,000, £20,000, and jewelry, illustrating how these organized criminals steal cash and belongings through their intelligence-led approach. While Romanians and Bulgarians comprise the majority of arrested pickpockets, Chilean gangs distinguish themselves through their ability to blend in as families or businessmen, making them particularly difficult to trace as they operate across international cities.

After nearly 90 years since Britain's first electricity pylon was erected in 1928, the UK's National Grid has unveiled the T-pylon, a new tower design that is about a third shorter than the traditional steel lattice pylons while maintaining the same 400,000-volt power capacity. The simpler design, composed of just eight components, allows new T-pylons to be erected in a single day compared to a week for older pylons, and the National Grid plans to use the first operational T-pylons with the Hinkley Point C nuclear power station in Somerset. Rather than replacing the existing 88,000 lattice pylons currently in use across the UK, the T-pylon will serve as an alternative option for new transmission routes where its shorter height and sleeker appearance offer advantages.

# Summary

Holly Nicole Solomon, 31, pleaded guilty to aggravated assault after hitting her husband Daniel with their SUV in a Mesa parking lot because he failed to vote in the 2012 presidential election. Solomon, who was six months pregnant at the time, claimed she accidentally accelerated instead of braking while trying to scare her husband, but Daniel suffered critical injuries including a shattered pelvis and torn artery. Under her plea deal, Solomon will serve three and a half years in prison rather than face up to 15 years if convicted on the original charges.

Student Drew Hollinshead, 21, was issued a £70 parking ticket by a traffic warden in Bournemouth after he stopped in a disabled bay to help an elderly woman who had collapsed on the pavement. Although Hollinshead parked in the nearest available space and spent less than a minute assisting the pensioner, the warden placed the fine on his windscreen, which Hollinshead initially tore up and discarded despite the warden apparently witnessing the good Samaritan act. The Bournemouth University marketing student challenged the decision as unreasonable, arguing that common sense should prevail in such circumstances, and Bournemouth Council's Parking Services subsequently offered him the opportunity to appeal.

In Pingjiang County, Hunan province, China, builders are constructing a mountain road thousands of feet above the ground with minimal safety equipment, spending their days hauling heavy planks and wheelbarrows full of concrete across a rickety wooden walkway only a few feet wide. The workers assemble scaffolding poles along the cliff face to extend the pathway through the Mufu mountain range that dominates the region, which officials hope will attract tourists to the area. Despite the extremely dangerous conditions and lack of safety harnesses or ropes, the construction continues as part of a broader effort to develop scenic mountain attractions.

An independent investigation has uncovered significant failures at two NHS hospitals—Royal Oldham and North Manchester General—where seven babies and three mothers died within eight months, including Lisa Parkisson who died 48 hours after giving birth and newborn Thomas Beaty who died after doctors forced him back into the womb. The review of Royal Oldham's newly opened maternity unit and North Manchester General found a notable absence of clinical leadership, inadequate care planning, and failure to follow national guidelines for treating obese mothers. North Pennine Acute Hospitals NHS Trust, which controls both hospitals, is now developing an action plan to strengthen clinical leadership, improve risk management, and ensure adequate staffing levels.

Following months of turmoil after unarmed black teenager Michael Brown was killed by police in August, Ferguson held its first municipal election since the tragedy, resulting in significant changes to the beleaguered city's government. With a higher-than-normal 30% turnout, two African-American candidates—Ella Jones and Wesley Bell—won their wards Tuesday night, making the six-member City Council 50% black, which better reflects Ferguson's population that is 70% black. The new council members prioritize police reform, with Bell advocating that Ferguson's police officers be judged by community relationships rather than ticket quotas and revenue generation.

Aaron Finch, Australia's World Cup-winning batsman and captain of the Twenty20 team, underwent surgery on his left hamstring after sustaining a high-grade injury while playing for the Mumbai Indians in the Indian Premier League, with Cricket Australia physiotherapist Alex Kountouris confirming the operation went well. The 28-year-old faces up to three months of rehabilitation and will miss his scheduled stint with English county side Yorkshire. Finch, who has never played Test cricket, was already excluded from Australia's upcoming Test series in the West Indies and the Ashes tour.

AP McCoy secured his second win at his final Aintree meeting by riding Don Cossack to a dominant 26-length victory in the Melling Chase, trained by Irishman Gordon Elliott. McCoy will ride Shutthefrontdoor in Saturday's Grand National instead of Elliott's Cause of Causes, which will be ridden by Paul Carberry. Elliott praised Don Cossack's impressive performance and indicated the horse is destined for the Gold Cup next season.

Vince Cable, the Business Secretary and self-proclaimed Tory bogeyman in the Coalition Cabinet—once regarded by David Cameron and George Osborne as a closet Labour politician—has announced his ambition to remain in government for another five years and potentially replace George Osborne as Chancellor, despite his lifelong opposition to the Conservatives. While some Labour MPs fantasize that boyish Ed Miliband could have recruited Cable to help Labour walk the election by lending credibility to the party's spending plans and preventing voters from worrying would-be PM Red Ed might do "something barmy," Cable instead criticizes Miliband's policies and insists he can work pragmatically with the Tories, whom he describes as "highly intelligent guys." Despite Conservative canvassers being sent to his Twickenham constituency to try to "decapitate" him, Cable's warm words toward Cameron and Osborne suggest his calculated willingness to continue collaborating with the party he has spent his political career fighting.

A survey of 2,000 British office workers found that the average British worker will sit through 6,239 meetings in their career, with 60 per cent finding most meetings "pretty pointless" and 70 per cent regularly zoning out after just 20 minutes. One in five workers confessed to making significant mistakes due to inattention, and many believe lengthy offsite and internal meetings waste company money on travel expenses when conference calls would be equally effective. The study also revealed that workers employ various distraction tactics during meetings, from doodling to daydreaming about dinner and evening plans, with some attending solely for the complimentary coffee and biscuits.

A logic puzzle about fictional Cheryl's birthday from the Singapore and Asian Schools Math Olympiad challenged thousands of people worldwide to determine when her birthday falls. In the puzzle, Cheryl gives Albert the month and tells Bernard the day separately, then makes statements that allow solvers to deduce the birthday cannot possibly fall in May or June—since Bernard might have already known if given those months' unique dates—ultimately narrowing the answer to July 16. Set as one of 25 questions in the SASMO exam for 14-year-olds, this difficult problem was designed to identify the most intelligent students among the 28,000 participants from countries including Singapore, China, and the UK.

Army Major John Jackson and his wife Carolyn are on trial for abusing their three foster children over a period of years between 2005 and 2010, forcing some to consume hot sauce and subjecting them to physical violence, food and water deprivation that resulted in broken bones, skull fractures, and severe malnutrition while their three biological children showed no signs of abuse. This is the couple's second trial after their first ended in a mistrial when a prosecutor inadvertently mentioned the death of one of their foster children, which was not permitted as evidence. The Jacksons' defense attorneys argue that while their parenting methods were objectionable, they do not constitute criminal abuse, claiming the parents acted in good faith.

Spanish newspapers Marca and AS report that Real Madrid will be without French striker Karim Benzema for their Champions League quarter-final second leg clash against Atletico Madrid on Wednesday, a knee injury sustained during the 3-1 victory against Malaga that contradicts manager Carlo Ancelotti's earlier assurances to reporters about the striker's availability. With Benzema, Gareth Bale, and Luka Modric all likely sidelined, Marca reports that Ancelotti is struggling to decide between a 4-4-2 or 4-3-3 formation for the crucial Champions League encounter. Additionally, Barcelona coach Luis Enrique has been praised for achieving the best statistical record in his first 50 games as manager in the club's history, surpassing legendary coaches Helenio Herrera, Pep Guardiola, and Tata Martino.

The culprits behind the £60m Hatton Garden raid in London may have carried out a similarly audacious burglary two years earlier at the Volksbank in Berlin, where thieves targeted 294 security vaults and escaped with £8.3m in diamonds, gold, and silver. Both heists share strikingly similar traits, including the use of heavy-duty drills to penetrate concrete walls, burglars disguised as construction workers, timing raids over bank holidays when staff were absent, and security alarms being ignored before discovery—with the London gang raiding 71 security boxes alone. The parallel methods and expertise demonstrated in both the Berlin and London raids—along with suspected insider involvement—suggest the same criminal gang may have been responsible for both audacious bank robberies.

# Summary

A father named Barles Charkley from Baltimore, Maryland captured a heartwarming video of his toddler daughter Viktoria's emotional reaction while watching Disney's The Lion King for the first time, specifically during the tear-jerking scene where the evil brother Scar murders Mufasa, leaving Simba fatherless. The video, which garnered over four million views, shows little Viktoria becoming visibly upset as she witnesses this pivotal moment, with her bottom lip trembling and tears filling her eyes before she seeks comfort from her father. The Mufasa death scene is widely considered one of the most haunting and tear-jerking moments in Disney history.

HSBC was found guilty of age discrimination by the Financial Ombudsman Service, which determined the bank had relied on untested assumptions and stereotypes about age when rejecting a £250,000 interest-only mortgage application from a couple in their 40s—unusual victims of such discrimination—because the husband would exceed 65 when the 18-year loan matured. Despite the husband's substantial pension and the couple's significant home equity, HSBC refused to proceed based on its strict age policies that cap interest-only lending at 65 (with a 75-year ceiling for repayment mortgages). The ombudsman ordered the bank to pay £500 compensation and reconsider the application, marking the first formal finding of age discrimination in mortgage lending and offering hope to borrowers facing similar age-based lending restrictions.

Dr. Kristen Lindsey, a Texas veterinarian, allegedly shot an arrow through an orange tabby cat's head and posted a boastful Facebook photo dangling the slain cat by the arrow's shaft, calling it her "first bow kill" and claiming "the only good feral tomcat is one with an arrow through it's head." After animal rescuers identified the cat as a missing pet belonging to an elderly couple, public outrage led to Lindsey being fired from Washington County Animal Clinic, and she now faces potential criminal charges as the sheriff's office investigates. Animal rights activists like Becky Robinson are pushing for prosecution and offering a $7,500 reward for evidence leading to Lindsey's arrest and conviction.

Professor Ninian Peckitt, a pioneering facial surgeon, faces being struck off the medical register after being accused of repeatedly punching a patient in the face during a closed reduction procedure to correct a fracture caused by an industrial accident at Ipswich Hospital in February 2012. The world-renowned specialist in titanium implants claimed he had simply "digitally manipulated" the patient's face as part of hospital treatment, but witnesses disputed this account, and the tribunal heard he treated Patient A without seeking colleague advice. The hearing also examined allegations that he failed to check medical records for Patient B, leading to an unnecessary procedure, and treated a third patient for complex facial deformity without proper surgical consultation.

Actor Ben Powers, best known for playing Thelma's husband Keith during the sixth and final season of the long-running sitcom Good Times, died on April 6 at the age of 64 at his home in New Bedford, Massachusetts, though his family has not revealed the cause. His on-screen wife Bern Nadette Stanis, who played main character Thelma, posted an emotional tribute on Facebook mourning the actor's death and calling him a great friend gone too soon. In addition to Good Times, Powers had roles in other television shows and films, and is survived by his mother and two sisters.

Darren Bent scored in stoppage time to secure a 1-1 draw for Derby against Brentford, rescuing a result the Rams desperately needed to keep their slim promotion hopes alive. Brentford dominated and took the lead through Alex Pritchard's brilliant strike, celebrated with his team-mates after a 28th-minute finish past David Button's left post, but squandered chances throughout—including in the 51st minute when Andre Gray fired wide—allowing Derby to snatch an unlikely equalizer when Bent pounced on a rebound in the 92nd minute. Button made several key saves diving to keep Brentford in front, but his efforts proved insufficient as Derby stole a draw they scarcely deserved.

A gang of four armed robbers used pink umbrellas to conceal their identities while carrying out a two-month crime spree, targeting 13 shops including supermarkets and convenience stores across the West Midlands and stealing approximately £145,000 in cash and goods along with £2,000 worth of cigarettes and vehicles valued at £30,000. The brolly-clad thieves threatened shop workers with hammers, knives, golf clubs, and screwdrivers, and used stolen cars as getaway vehicles. Following forensic evidence discovered on a weapon used in an Asda robbery, police arrested all four suspects—Ashleigh Evans, Liam Bell, Trea Richardson, and Marcus Morgan—who were sentenced to between four and ten years in prison after pleading guilty to conspiracy to rob.

Italian explorer Alex Bellini plans to undertake a daring project called Adrift, in which he will live atop an iceberg in Greenland for an entire year inside a ball-shaped capsule to raise awareness about global warming and melting icebergs. This cabin fever-inducing stunt will expose him to extreme isolation as the iceberg's center of mass shifts and potentially flips, throwing the capsule into the air, but Bellini's plan includes sensors to detect tremors and a rotating interior mounted on roller balls to protect him from sudden movements. Through this extreme feat of endurance, Mr. Bellini aims to highlight the devastating effects of climate change and inspire people worldwide to live more sustainably.

A photograph circulating on Snapchat, allegedly taken by a student and shared among peers, showed principal Andrew Barr of elite private school Geelong College in Melbourne watching pornography in his office, with graphic images visible on his computer screen. After the Geelong College Council became aware of the image and confirmed it breached the school's standards, Mr. Barr resigned from his position, though the investigation found he had not broken any law. The council has begun searching for a replacement principal.

30-year-old Francis Pusok was beaten by San Bernardino County Sheriff's deputies—kicked and punched more than 80 times on Thursday after he fled his southern California home when they arrived to arrest him on identity theft charges, with helicopter footage capturing the violent assault as video shows Pusok falling to the ground after being tasered and then attacked by the first officers, who continued the beating even after he was handcuffed. Pusok, who was released from jail on Sunday, claims he was beaten while lying helpless and that a deputy threatened him during the attack, concerns that deeply worry his pregnant girlfriend Jolene Bindner, who fears retaliation against their family. The incident has sparked an FBI investigation into excessive force amid a recent wave of high-profile cases involving police violence, including the murder of Walter Scott in South Carolina, with 10 officers now placed on paid administrative leave.

# Summary

Hizb ut-Tahrir (HT), an extremist Islamist group with significant UK support, has reportedly joined forces with al-Qaeda's Nusra Front in Syria and attacked civil activists in Aleppo, while several British men linked to ISIS, including "Jihadi John" Mohammed Emwazi, are known to have contacted the group at UK universities. Liberal Democrat candidate Maajid Nawaz, who spent 13 years in HT before renouncing his views, has faced controversy after footage emerged showing him attempting to touch a lap dancer at an east London strip club during Ramadan, contradicting his public persona as a feminist and family man. The revelations have prompted calls for Nawaz to resign from his parliamentary candidacy, with critics questioning his hypocrisy between his stated values and personal behavior.

Marks & Spencer is now stocking the Bouea macrophylla, a hybrid fruit nicknamed the "plango," which crosses mango and plum varieties developed by specialist growers in Thailand. The latest tropical fruit features a bright orange edible skin that delivers all the flavor of a mango without the preparation hassle, as you can eat the skin whole. According to M&S fruit expert Shazad Rehman, customers seeking new and interesting produce will find it on shelves this weekend.

South Africa's oldest university, the University of Cape Town, has voted to remove a bronze statue of British colonialist Cecil Rhodes after protests from black students who felt uncomfortable with the monument symbolizing historical white oppression. While the government backed the removal—emphasizing they do not support violent removal of statues—as a positive step in addressing the country's colonial past, the decision has sparked counter-protests from white Afrikaner solidarity groups who argue their heritage is being unfairly targeted and have demanded protection for their historical monuments. The defaced statue of Rhodes, the 19th-century imperialist who founded Rhodesia and helped lay the groundwork for apartheid, will likely be moved to a museum as South African campuses grapple with how to reckon with colonial symbols.

Hannah Bluck, an 18-year-old Welsh international footballer, has had her promising career halted after being diagnosed with avascular necrosis, an ultra-rare condition causing her shin bone to die due to poor blood supply, with doctors warning that eventual collapse could leave her leg unable to support her weight. Forced to sit out for two years after suffering a freak ankle dislocation during training in March 2013, Bluck remains determined to return to professional football, with her mother Kerstin expressing pride in her daughter's bravery and resilience as she pursues specialist treatment in London.

Paris Saint-Germain forward Edinson Cavani, the former Napoli striker, has dismissed reports of a breakdown in his relationship with team-mate Zlatan Ibrahimovic, insisting they respect one another despite not necessarily being friends. Cavani admits frustration at being played out of position rather than as a striker alongside Ibrahimovic, as he was originally recruited to do, though he argues his concerns extend only to tactical deployment and not interpersonal conflict. Despite being linked with a summer exit following PSG's 3-2 win over Marseilles, where he challenged midfielder Mario Lemina during the clash, Cavani stated he intends to honor his three-year contract while remaining focused on the club's current campaign.

England coach John Peacock has announced his squad for next month's UEFA European Under-17 Championship in Bulgaria, with the Young Lions aiming to defend their title after beating Holland on penalties last year and progressing with a perfect qualifying record. The squad includes talented players from top Premier League clubs such as Chelsea's Jay Dasilva and Ike Ugbo, Arsenal duo Chris Willock and Stephy Mavididi, and Liverpool pair Herbie Kane and Trent Arnold, who have earned recalls after missing recent qualifiers. England will compete in Group D alongside Holland, Italy, and the Republic of Ireland, opening their campaign against Italy in Burgas on May 7, followed by matches against Holland on May 10 and Ireland on May 13.

Fireball Cinnamon Whisky, the rebranded successor to McGillicuddy's Fireball Whisky that arrived in its current form a decade ago, has become the fastest-growing liquor brand in America, with retail sales rocketing from under $2 million in 2011 to $130 million in 2014, largely through social media campaigns and bartender outreach—including brand ambassadors who shared their "Fireballing exploits" with #FireballFriday hashtags—turning the yellow-labeled bottle with its fire-breathing demon (inspired by Atomic Fireball hard candy) into the sixth-most popular liquor brand and dethroning Jagermeister as America's party shot of choice. The brand's phenomenal appeal to young drinkers and women who appreciate its sweet cinnamon taste and lack of throat burn has made it so popular that bars like Clayton's in South Padre Island go through three or four bottles nightly, though Fireball also endured a European recall over propylene glycol concerns and now faces competition from copycat products like Jack Daniels Tennessee Fire. Despite these headwinds and signs that explosive growth has slowed in recent months, Fireball remains wildly popular in bars nationwide, leaving industry observers uncertain whether it will become a cornerstone brand or fade as a consumer fad.

Researchers at the University of Montana found that eating fast food after a workout may be just as beneficial as dietary supplements for recovery during resting periods between exercise. In their study published in the International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism, 11 male athletes performed a 90-minute endurance workout, then consumed either fast food or sports supplements before their second meal and a subsequent 12-mile stationary bike ride; performance and glycogen levels remained similar between both groups, with the fast food group showing slightly higher recovery markers. However, the researchers acknowledged the small sample size and noted that the long-term effects of using fast food as recovery food compared to healthier alternatives remain unknown.

Gael Monfils delivered a performance that showcased just how good the Frenchman could be, defeating former world No. 1 Roger Federer 6-4, 7-6 in a thrilling last-16 match at the Monte Carlo Masters in Monaco, with 22 winners to Federer's 17 and a dominant 31-shot rally that produced three break points in the opening set. Monfils' exceptional athleticism and shotmaking, paired with the focus and commitment that can sometimes elude the world No. 18, sent the 33-year-old out of the clay court tournament and advanced him to his first Monte Carlo quarter-final against Grigor Dimitrov.

Reverend Al Sharpton appeared in North Charleston, South Carolina on Sunday to preach at Charity Missionary Baptist Church and address the fatal shooting of Walter Scott by police officer Michael Slager, drawing North Charleston Mayor Keith Summey and South Carolina Senator Marlon Kimpson to hear him speak on national policing reform. During his sermon, Sharpton praised the mayor's decision to fire Slager and urged the community to set a new standard for policing nationwide. Sharpton also attended a vigil near the shooting scene on Sunday afternoon, while Scott's funeral took place on Saturday in nearby Summerville.

Stewart Brand, president of the Long Now Foundation and editor of the Whole Earth Catalogue, argues that Earth's biodiversity is actually increasing despite warnings about widespread extinctions, citing evidence that new species are being discovered at a rate of 18,000 per year—outpacing the current extinction rate and contrasting sharply with the catastrophic die-offs of past mass extinction events like the Ordovician-Silurian, Late Devonian, and Triassic-Jurassic extinctions. He contends that focusing on extinction threats is counterproductive and points to successful conservation efforts such as removing invasive rats from islands and the recovery of previously endangered species like the black rhino and kakapo, as well as historical data showing that biodiversity has been at its highest level in 200 million years. Brand also suggests that biotechnology could help reintroduce extinct species and that while 23,214 species are currently deemed threatened, climate change is unlikely to significantly increase extinction rates as many species will adapt and evolve.

ISIS presents itself as a functioning government operating under Islamic law, as evidenced by official documents relating to birth certificates, vaccination schedules, and rent disputes in territories where ISIS fighters swept through Iraqi cities like Mosul, which the group overran and subsequently controlled. While ISIS enforces its medieval interpretation of Sharia law through barbaric punishments and destruction of pre-Islamic artifacts, it also attempts to portray itself as just and fair by providing services such as hospitals through its Health Department, reopening universities with altered curricula, and implementing rent control policies to win over populations. This bureaucratic approach distinguishes ISIS from groups like the Taliban, as it allows certain modern services like vaccinations and segregated girls' education while justifying all policies through Islamic jurisprudence to establish moral authority over its followers.

In Milwaukee, Wisconsin, a tragic incident unfolded Sunday evening when a 2-year-old ran into the street and was struck and killed by a van driver, later identified as Archie Brown Jr. Milwaukee police say an unidentified gunman shot and killed Brown at the scene in what authorities suspect may have been a revenge killing, and also fatally shot a 15-year-old boy who died at the hospital. Brown's father, Archie Brown Sr., confirmed his son was a father-of-four real estate entrepreneur with no known enemies, as police continue searching for the shooter.

Betting odds on the new royal baby being a girl have shortened significantly after 90 percent of bets placed with Ladbrokes favored a female child, with punters across the UK convinced the Duchess of Cambridge will give birth to a princess—a sentiment Prince George may not share. The most popular predicted birth dates are April 18-20, despite the official due date being April 25th, and Alice and Victoria are the leading name choices, both drawing on royal heritage including Queen Victoria, whose 12 granddaughters bore the name Victoria, as well as Princess Alice Maud Marie, whose descendants include Sweden's Crown Princess Victoria. While bookmakers hope for a boy, popular male names include James—also the name of the Duchess's brother—and Arthur, which has roots in Celtic mythology.

Presenters Holly Willoughby and Davina McCall have joined forces for their first campaign together with Garnier Nutrisse, with the This Morning presenter celebrating finding their perfect hair shades—Holly chose her ideal multi-tonal blonde shade 10.01 while Davina selected Garnier Nutrisse 4.3. Meanwhile, Holly has been keeping busy designing a vintage-inspired baby clothing range for BHS, while Davina renewed her wedding vows with husband Matthew Robertson in the desert gambling haven of Las Vegas last weekend, with their three children writing the ceremony vows.

# Summary

A New York judge ruled that a Brooklyn woman, Ellanora Arthur Baidoo, can serve her husband Victor Sena Blood-Dzraku with divorce papers via Facebook after her attorney Andrew Spinnell filed an application for service by alternate means, since they have been unable to locate him through traditional methods. Justice Matthew Cooper approved the use of social media, stating that Facebook and Twitter are the "next frontier" for delivering legal summons. Baidoo's marriage began to unravel shortly after they married in 2009 when her husband refused to participate in a Ghanaian wedding ceremony, and they never lived together.

The Turin Shroud, a 14-foot-long linen cloth believed by devotees to wrap Jesus' body as his burial cloth, went on public display in Turin's cathedral on Sunday for the first time in five years, with over one million people already booking slots to see it until June 24. The cloth, imprinted with the image of a man who appears to have been crucified, has been dated to the 13th century and remains scientifically mysterious, as researchers have been unable to determine exactly how the image was created. While the Church does not officially confirm the relic's authenticity, it values the shroud's symbolic significance in representing Christ's suffering, and the exhibition is expected to boost the economy of newly-industrialized Turin while marking the 200th anniversary of St. John Bosco's birth.

A newly-built £25million mansion on central London's Farm Street in Mayfair has gone on the market, standing on the site of a former milking parlour that operated 150 years ago when London's milking parlours—dating back to Tudor times—served as hubs of agricultural trade. The four-bedroom plush mansion, constructed in 2011 after developers demolished the original structure, features modern luxury amenities including an indoor swimming pool, gym, cinema room, private lift, roof terrace, and a dining table that seats 14. Estate agents describe the recently built house as an "outstanding example of contemporary living in historic Mayfair," with flowing open spaces and generous ceiling heights rarely found in the historic area.

BBC period drama Poldark has been commissioned for a second series as the final episodes approach, with heartthrob Aidan Turner set to reprise his role as broody Ross Poldark alongside Eleanor Tomlinson as his wife Demelza. The new phenomenon helped BBC1 deliver its strongest start to a year for a decade, attracting an average of 8.1 million viewers per episode as audiences followed Ross Poldark's journey. The second series will adapt the third and fourth books from Winston Graham's 12-book Poldark series, continuing the story of the swoon-worthy Captain Ross Poldark.

A gastrointestinal illness has affected 100 people aboard the cruise ship Celebrity Infinity, including 95 of the ship's 2,117 passengers and 5 crew members, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. The CDC has yet to determine the cause of the vomiting, diarrhea, and other symptoms, though Celebrity Cruises has increased cleaning procedures and is collecting specimens from the afflicted for testing. Two CDC staffers are scheduled to meet the ship in San Diego on Monday, marking the third such outbreak on the Infinity, with previous incidents occurring in 2006 and 2013.

UltraHaptics is a technology company that creates tactile three-dimensional shapes using ultrasound, allowing users to feel virtual interfaces without physical contact. By combining this haptic technology with virtual reality goggles, users could not only see a virtual world projected before their eyes but also touch it, addressing virtual reality's current limitation of bypassing the sense of touch—while several existing devices already support gesture control, UltraHaptics adds tactile feedback that transforms both traditional and popular electronic devices from car dashboards to smartphones and kitchen appliances. The company is already working with 15 to 20 clients across consumer electronics, automotive, and gaming industries to incorporate this tactile ultrasound technology into their products.

Jodie Barden, 28, is forced to plan funerals for her two daughters, eight-year-old Ella and one-year-old Chloe, who both suffer from Cockayne Syndrome, a rare progeria-like genetic disorder inherited from both parents that causes premature aging with life expectancy under 12 years. Despite the devastating diagnosis, Barden and her husband—an HGV driver who, like her, carries the mutated gene—have committed to making their daughters' lives memorable through special experiences while raising approximately £14,000 for Cockayne Syndrome charities. The family continues to create meaningful moments as CS patients face challenges including vision and hearing loss, demonstrating that even with this rare disease affecting one in 500,000 children, joy and connection remain possible.

Damon Muller, an 11-year-old boy from Queensland, was diagnosed with Juvenile Dermatomyositis (JDM), a rare autoimmune disease in November that has robbed him of his active lifestyle while nearly doubling his size through severe swelling and bloating. The condition limits muscle movement throughout his body and forces him to avoid sun exposure due to risk of severe rashes and throat swelling that could close his airway. Damon's family is fundraising to attend an American conference for the Cure JM Foundation to gain support and information, as few resources exist for the disease in Australia.

Prince Harry's former girlfriend Chelsy Davy, a 29-year-old who seemed in high spirits, debuted a sophisticated summer style at the launch party for London restaurant The Ivy Chelsea Garden, wearing a white layered chiffon top paired with navy trousers and tan ankle-strap heels. The Zimbabwean socialite circulated the party with friend Irene Forte, showcasing a more grown-up and glamorous approach to fashion as she approaches her thirties. Davy is currently single following her January split from jeweller Charles Goode and remains on good terms with Prince Harry, whom she dated for six years until 2010.

Hundreds of mourners attended the funeral of nine-year-old Chloe at St Peter's Church in Calais, northern France, where her mother Isabelle Hyart broke down in tears as she lovingly touched her daughter's small white coffin. Chloe was abducted from a playground, raped, and murdered by Polish migrant Zbigniew Huminski on April 15, with her lifeless body discovered in nearby woodland just hours later. The tragic killing sparked a national outpouring of grief in France, with over 6,000 people participating in a memorial march to honor the young schoolgirl.

Low iron levels, particularly low ferritin (stored iron), can trigger hair loss by reducing oxygen delivery to hair follicles, and while experts like Dr. Vicky Jolliffe at the Royal London Hospital debate whether to take iron pills for treatment, research shows women with increased hair-shedding respond well to iron supplements combined with l-lysine, an amino acid thought to play a role in hair loss by aiding iron absorption. Experts emphasize that eating adequate protein at every meal is vital for healthy hair since hair consists of protein, though dietary changes typically take six months to show visible results. Other supplements like Viviscal and caffeine-based products show promise in laboratory studies, but lack sufficient clinical trial data to definitively prove their effectiveness in treating hair loss.

Suma, a 36-year-old orangutan at Melbourne Zoo, underwent a comprehensive health examination to assess the progression of her arthritis, a painful condition she was first diagnosed with in 2013. During the procedure, surgeons anaesthetised Suma and performed X-rays on her hips and ankles, while orthopaedic surgeon Associate Professor Marino Pirpiris and senior vet Dr Helen McCracken conducted a full check-up of her ears, teeth, and eyes, with Pirpiris administering a steroid injection to reduce her pain for months or even years. The examination allowed the medical team to move beyond the daily pain medication that Melbourne Zoo keepers had been giving Suma to manage her condition, which is not uncommon in middle-aged orangutans.

New pictures and Snapchat videos have emerged showing Louis Tomlinson from One Direction partying with five girls at London's Soho Hotel on Wednesday night, where he appeared to be rolling a joint in his hotel room next to a suspicious substance. The footage captures Louis leaving the Cirque Le Soir nightclub at 4am and continuing the celebration at his hotel until at least 5:48am. This incident comes just one month after bandmate Zayn Malik quit the group and follows a previous controversial video of the pair appearing to smoke marijuana during a tour in Peru.

Century-old photographs from 1900 to 1911 during China's Qing dynasty offer a rare historical glimpse of Beijing's famous landmarks and popular tourist attractions—including the Temple of Heaven, Western Qing Tombs, Summer Palace, and Yonghe Temple—when they were virtually devoid of visitors. These black and white images show locals and foreigners casually posing next to giant stone statues of lions, camels, and horses in a serene Beijing untouched by modern crowds, smog, and commercial tourism. The striking contrast reveals how dramatically the Chinese capital has transformed from a quiet imperial city into one of the world's most populous destinations, now attracting millions of visitors annually to these same UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

A CoreLogic Pain and Gain report reveals that many Australian capital city homes under the hammer are experiencing record-breaking profits at auctions, with only 8.6 percent of home resales recording a gross loss and 32.3 percent of properties selling for more than double their original purchase price. The total value of profit-making resales exceeded $17 billion with an average profit of $251,696 per successful property, while Sydney, Perth, and Melbourne saw extremely low resale losses of 2.4 percent, 5.5 percent, and 5.6 percent respectively—a trend driven partly by owners rebuilding original homes rather than selling existing structures. However, this positive market shift has not extended to regional areas or the Western Australia market, and Australian homes are now typically owned for over ten years before being resold, with Melbourne properties held the longest at nearly 12 years.

West Bromwich Albion manager Tony Pulis delivered a typical performance to beat his former employers Crystal Palace 2-0, with James Morrison scoring within two minutes and Craig Gardner adding a stunning 30-yard strike in the second half, though Alan Pardew's Palace side dominated much of the match. The victory virtually guaranteed West Brom's Premier League safety with 36 points, showcasing Pulis's gritty, resilient approach that remains unchanged despite the sport's prominence and television cameras. True to form at every game, Pulis wore his iconic cap, tracksuit, and white trainers on the touchline, insisting he has no reason to alter the ritual that works.

Crystal Palace co-chairman Steve Parish celebrated his club's impressive 2-1 victory over champions Manchester City by posting a photo from the Palace boardroom on Instagram with manager Alan Pardew, England boss Roy Hodgson, and Bill Wyman, an original Rolling Stones member and avid Palace fan. Wyman, who has supported Palace for 68 years since attending his first game at age 10, was given special permission to skip the boardroom's strict dress code due to his rock-star status. The victory was marked by seven English players in Palace's lineup, including goal scorer Jason Puncheon, while England's Joe Hart played for the defeated Manchester City side.

The Solar Impulse 2, an experimental plane attempting to fly around the world without fuel, has been grounded by poor weather in the southwestern city of Chongqing for two and a half weeks, far longer than the planned overnight pit stop to its next destination in Nanjing. The aircraft, which stores energy from over 17,000 solar cells to power its motors, requires near perfect weather conditions with cross winds of less than 4 knots to operate, and pilots say the mission aims to prove the power of renewable energy. Though the delay is testing the team's patience, pilot Bertrand Piccard notes that adventure means working with nature's forces, and despite the setback, the plane is technically still on schedule ahead of the critical Pacific crossing to Hawaii.

Jordan Spieth visited the Empire State Building after his Masters triumph, where the Augusta record-equalling champion proudly wore his green jacket following a four-shot victory and an 18-under total that equalled Tiger Woods's scoring record. His success hinged on redemption at pivotal holes—where his approach shots and birdie on the eighth contrasted sharply with last year's bogeys against Bubba Watson's birdie, and a crucial par-saving putt on the 16th that Justin Rose praised as among the best of the day. At 21, Spieth now ranks No. 2 globally and has joined Woods, Tom Creavy, and Gene Sarazen as the only players to win three PGA Tour titles including a major before turning 22, positioning him to eventually reach Rory McIlroy at the top of world rankings.

This article presents etiquette expert William Hanson's humorous guide to determining social class based on household items, sparked by scrutiny of current Prime Minister David Cameron's home. According to Hanson, truly upmarket stock know that essential markers include a cafetière, an Aga, proper tea cups with saucers, cloth napkins ensured even for casual suppers, plain white toilet roll rather than quilted alternatives, bar soap, and fabric seating—the only proper choice for sofas—alongside bathrooms featuring two separate taps instead of modern mono fixtures. The scoring system ranges from upper class (18-14 points) to lower-middle class (4-1 points), reflecting Britain's enduring preoccupation with class distinctions and what constitutes a genuinely posh home.

Andy Murray tied the knot with Kim Sears on Saturday at Dunblane Cathedral in his native town, with thousands of fans gathering outside to celebrate the couple. The wedding ceremony took place at around 4:00pm at the 12th-century cathedral with family and close friends, before Murray shared his excitement that morning through emojis on Twitter. The reception followed at Cromlix House Hotel, as the local community turned out in force to support their hometown tennis hero.

Alastair Cook has been working with former England batting coach Graham Gooch on a new, more open stance, but appears to have overdone it during the first Test against the West Indies, positioning his front foot too far open which forces him to play shots with his hands away from his body—a vulnerability highlighted by pictures from training in Grenada on Monday where he and England head coach Peter Moores were observed, as well as footage from the match itself. The article compares Cook's stance from a 2007 match at Lord's—when he was playing well with a near-perfect stance—to these recent images, showing how his exaggerated footwork leaves him susceptible to nicking the ball, evident when he kicked out in frustration after being dismissed for just 13 runs. Despite the technical issues, the author notes Cook's mental strength as his greatest asset and suggests that once he settles in with a few boundaries, his natural batting ability should return.

Dani Alves is set to leave Barcelona this summer after rejecting the club's final contract offer of a three-year extension conditional on playing 60 percent of matches, as confirmed by his agent at a press conference Thursday. The 31-year-old Brazilian right-back, who has spent seven successful years at the Nou Camp winning four Spanish titles and two Champions League trophies, has been unable to reach an agreement with the Catalan club on renewing his contract. Alves is now expected to attract interest from several top clubs including Manchester United, Manchester City, and Paris St Germain, with his representative stating he already has two three-year offers on the table.

Robin Ellis, the original star of Poldark now 73 years old, was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes in 1999 at age 58 despite being slim and fit throughout his life, a condition that occurs when the body's cells fail to react to insulin causing blood sugar levels to soar and can lead to serious complications like blindness and heart disease. Unlike his mother who died from type 1 diabetes complications, Robin's type 2 diabetes likely stems from hidden visceral fat around his organs rather than visible obesity—a reminder that excess fat cells release chemicals disrupting insulin response, and BMI alone doesn't reveal the full picture of diabetes risk. Through strict lifestyle changes including eliminating refined carbohydrates and walking 150 minutes weekly, Robin managed to control his blood glucose levels without medication for ten years, and he now shares his knowledge through cookbooks and workshops to help others manage the condition.

Former New England Patriots tight end Aaron Hernandez was moved to the maximum-security Souza-Baranowski Correctional Center in Shirley, Massachusetts one week after a state prisons official confirmed he was convicted on April 15 of first-degree murder for killing 27-year-old Odin Lloyd, who was dating the sister of Hernandez's fiancée, and sentenced to life in prison. Hernandez, who had initially been held at Cedar Junction prison in Walpole, is now at the state's most advanced facility, which houses over 1,000 inmates serving life sentences and features 366 cameras for constant surveillance. It remains unknown which cell block Hernandez will be assigned to, though options include solitary confinement, general population, the "lifers block" for those serving life sentences, or the "kitchen block" for kitchen workers.

Jamie Robbins, a 35-year-old robber, handed himself into police a year after stealing £1,600 from a Birmingham convenience store with an accomplice, saying he preferred life in prison. He was jailed for four-and-a-half years at Birmingham Crown Court after pleading guilty to robbery and possession of a bladed article, having used a machete during the planned attack. However, the day before his court appearance, Robbins discovered his girlfriend was pregnant, which gave him newfound purpose and changed his desire to remain incarcerated.

The world's first cloned camel, Injaz, a mother-to-be created from the ovarian cells of a slaughtered camel in 2009, is now pregnant at six years old and expected to give birth later this year, which scientists say will prove that cloned camels are fertile and can reproduce like naturally produced camels. The article also discusses recent developments in genetically modifying human embryos, including scientists in China who have reportedly introduced DNA changes to reproductive cells for the first time, a development that has sparked ethical concerns despite potential benefits for eliminating inherited diseases. This advancement in cloning and genetic modification technology represents significant milestones since Dolly the sheep became the world's first cloned mammal in Edinburgh 19 years ago.

# Summary

Former Manchester United manager Sir Alex Ferguson spoke with 1991 World Snooker Champion John Parrott and presenter Hazel Irvine at the BBC's coverage of the World Championship at the Crucible, where he revealed why he rates Cristiano Ronaldo above Lionel Messi. Ferguson believes Ronaldo is superior because he could score for any team due to his versatility with both feet, speed, aerial ability, and bravery, whereas he considers Messi to be primarily a Barcelona player. The 73-year-old, who has been a keen snooker fan since childhood, also shared anecdotes about beating former player Paul Ince at snooker and discussed what he misses most since retiring from football in 2013.

On Saturday night, a Fenerbahce team bus carrying players and coaching staff was attacked by gunfire near Trabzon, with Fenerbahce TV maintaining that a bullet struck the driver in the head. The injured bus driver was hospitalized with a head wound, though none of the players or officials on board were hurt. In a separate incident, Caykur Rizespor midfielder Ludovic Obraniak was also taken to hospital with a suspected heart attack, though preliminary tests showed he was not in immediate danger.

Nicola Ferguson Sturgeon, Scotland's First Minister and leader of the Scottish National Party (SNP), impressed a Britain-wide television audience during a political debate, with 28 percent of viewers rating her as the best performer—earning praise from Judy Murray as "Game, Set and Match"—as she pivoted from the SNP's referendum loss to champion Scottish independence beyond Scotland's borders. Having transformed herself from a dowdy figure into a glamorous and powerful leader who commands widespread devotion (12,000 supporters paid to hear her speak after the SNP lost), Sturgeon deployed the debate as a charm offensive, greeting young fans on the campaign trail while positioning herself as a more conciliatory figure than her predecessor. With polls suggesting the SNP could win 45 of Scotland's 59 seats, Sturgeon is poised to wield significant influence over Westminster's next government formation—a prospect that alarms critics concerned her left-wing agenda could destabilize British politics.

# Summary

Ulster and Ireland prop Declan Fitzpatrick, 31, has retired from rugby on medical grounds after experiencing multiple concussive episodes in recent seasons, with symptoms progressively slower to resolve. Following neurological tests and medical advice, Ulster Rugby determined that retirement was in Fitzpatrick's long-term interests. The 31-year-old, who won seven Ireland caps and made 98 Ulster appearances during nine seasons with the province, stated that while his symptoms continue to improve, he accepts the medical recommendation for his long-term health.

# Summary

Oregon-based defense contractor FLIR Systems Inc. agreed to pay $9.5 million to settle Securities and Exchange Commission bribery charges involving expensive trips and luxury gifts given to Middle Eastern government officials, with the thermal-imaging company earning over $7 million in profits from sales influenced by these gifts. Two former employees in FLIR's Dubai office gave luxury watches to Saudi officials and arranged lavish international travel, including a 20-night trip across multiple countries, despite having received Foreign Corrupt Practices Act training that explicitly prohibited such gifts. FLIR's deficient financial controls failed to detect the misconduct, and the company must now report to the SEC on its compliance efforts for the next two years.

# Summary

American television network Showtime has released a promotional video showing Floyd Mayweather Jr. preparing for his highly anticipated $300 million mega fight against Manny Pacquiao on May 2 at the MGM Grand in Las Vegas. The short film takes viewers inside Mayweather's training camp and gym, where the undefeated American is intensely focused on winning the WBA, WBO, and WBC welterweight titles, explaining that he has "never wanted to win a fight so bad" in his life. Mayweather is currently within three-and-a-half pounds of the welterweight limit, and both fighters have been documenting their training camps on social media as they prepare for what many are calling the "Fight of the Century."

A 58-year-old mother named Diwalinen Vankar heroically saved her 19-year-old daughter Kanta from a 13-foot mugger crocodile that grabbed her leg and dragged her into the murky waters of the Vishwamitri River in west India. When the crocodile's powerful jaws clasped around Kanta with razor-sharp teeth—threatening to drown her as it tried to roll deeper into the river—Vankar fought the creature for 10 minutes using a wooden washing paddle, repeatedly striking its head until it finally released her daughter. Kanta suffered a leg injury but has since been discharged from the hospital, while wildlife officials warn that the river harbors over 200 ferocious crocodiles and remains extremely dangerous.

Sportsmail counts down the 20 greatest shots ever seen at Augusta National Golf Club, featuring memorable moments from Masters history including Mark O'Meara's clutch 20-foot putt to win in 1998 after shooting two birdies in three holes, Billy Joe Patton's near-miss coming within a stroke of victory with an amateur hole-in-one in 1954 before a double bogey dashed his hopes, and Byron Nelson's remarkable comeback from six shots down in 1937. The countdown also highlights Seve's second Masters victory marked by spectacular shotmaking, Charl Schwartzel's incredible chipped-in birdie from 100 feet in 2011, Phil Mickelson's pressure-packed 18-footer to win his first major in 2004, and Louis Oosthuizen's perfectly executed 253-yard approach for an albatross in 2012.

In tonight's episode of Married at First Sight, all three couples experience serious issues during their honeymoons, as revealed in web exclusive clips. Jaclyn Methuen, who nearly left husband Ryan Ranellone at the altar, continues to put him in the friend zone during their Puerto Rico trip and admits she wanted to be a runaway bride, leaving Ryan feeling disappointed. Meanwhile, Jessica Castro and Ryan DeNiro argue during a romantic dinner in Key Largo, and Davina Kullar and Sean Varricchio struggle with relationship issues during their Colorado honeymoon, with Davina upset about feeling disconnected from her new husband.

The Taliban recently published an 11-page biography of Mullah Mohammed Omar on their website to dispel rumors about his death and reaffirm his leadership of their self-declared Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan, particularly as ISIS leader Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi gains prominence among militants. The biography chronicles Omar's early years in Afghanistan's Kandahar province, his religious education and subsequent role commanding mujahedeen against Soviet forces, and his founding of the Taliban movement in 1994, which captured Kabul two years later to impose Islamic law across the country. However, Omar has remained a reclusive figure during the Taliban's six-year rule and beyond, and despite claims he continues leading from hiding, years without video or audio recordings have fueled speculation that Omar may have died.

The NFL launched an investigation led by attorney Ted Wells to determine whether the New England Patriots intentionally used deflated footballs during the AFC Championship game to gain a competitive advantage, with Patriots quarterback Tom Brady calling the accusation "ridiculous" and head coach Bill Belichick denying any wrongdoing. The investigation, which has lasted over three months with no announced completion date, could result in fines and/or loss of draft picks for the Patriots, mirroring penalties imposed on other teams like the Browns for rule violations in games and the Falcons for recent infractions. The case echoes the 2007 "Spygate" scandal when the Patriots illegally videotaped the New York Jets' defensive signals, for which the league stripped them of a first-round draft pick and imposed significant fines on the home club and coach.

# Summary

Bianca Gascoigne, daughter of former England midfielder Paul Gascoigne, was mugged by a gang of eight on bikes in central London on Tuesday afternoon while walking in Marylebone. The attackers grabbed her phone, which reportedly contained private texts from her father, and attempted to snatch her bag before fleeing when she fought back. Bianca took to Twitter following the theft, branding the muggers "lowlifes on bikes," and was reportedly left devastated over the loss of the private messages stored on her device.

Aberdeen striker Adam Rooney, who has netted 27 goals this season and made the PFA Scotland Player of the Year shortlist, admits that watching Celtic celebrate title glory inside Pittodrie would be painful, and expects Aberdeen to be fully motivated to prevent that outcome when Celtic visit on May 10. Despite trailing by eight points and losing all three meetings with Celtic this season, Rooney remains optimistic that Aberdeen can narrow the gap in coming years with new signings and continued stability under manager Derek McInnes. The 27-year-old, just three goals shy of 30 for the season, credited his prolific form to his teammates' creativity and emphasized that Aberdeen's sole focus is securing victory in their final clash against the champions.

Former rap mogul Marion "Suge" Knight was ordered to stand trial for one count of murder, one count of attempted murder, and one count of hit-and-run stemming from a deadly hit-and-run confrontation on a movie set in January 2015, with Judge Ronald Coen lowering his bail from $25 million to $10 million.
