Knight's defense attorney argued that Knight was acting in self-defense against aggressor Cle Sloan, claiming Knight was the victim trying to survive, while prosecutors countered that Knight deliberately used his vehicle as a deadly weapon, running over two men and killing Terry Carter. Knight, who suffers from diabetes and blood clots, faces up to life in prison if convicted, compounding his troubled legal history that includes previous prison time for assault and weapons convictions following Tupac Shakur's death in 1996, when Knight was driving the vehicle in which the rapper was fatally shot.

Hillary Clinton's astroturf candidacy continued its carefully orchestrated approach during her brief Iowa campaign swing, with a Tuesday coffee shop roundtable in LeClaire staged by campaign staffer Troy Price, who recruited Democratic Party insiders including Austin Bird—a former Vice President Biden chauffeur—to pose as ordinary Iowans. Bird told Daily Mail Online that Price called him and two other political operatives in Davenport, driving them to the event after a vetting session, misleading news outlets into depicting them as typical voters. The staged encounter exemplifies Clinton's broader strategy to project spontaneity and populism while countering perceptions that she is calculating and disconnected from everyday Americans.

The BioSure HIV Self Test, a 99.7 percent accurate testing kit now available for sale online in the UK, enables people to discreetly test themselves using a small finger-prick blood sample to detect HIV antibodies within 15 minutes, though positive results require confirmation by a healthcare professional. With an estimated 26,000 people in the UK unaware of their HIV status and 40 percent of those living with HIV remaining undiagnosed for at least four years, knowing your HIV status is critical as late diagnosis can significantly impact health and life expectancy, particularly for those in high-risk groups who should be tested every three months. This self-test represents a major advancement compared to the only previous alternative, "home sampling," which requires a blood sample 160 times larger and takes five days for results, while treatments now make HIV a manageable disease when diagnosed early.

California's approximately 162,000 inmates, particularly the most dangerous housed in Level IV maximum-security facilities like Calipatria State Prison where up to 97% are gang-affiliated, face starkly different conditions depending on their gang status. Since 1999, the California Department of Corrections has established several Sensitive Needs Housing Units where former gang members from organizations like Nuestra Familia and Mexican Mafia can safely renounce gang life, allowing inmates to participate in joint venture programs and interact peacefully in the prison yard. In contrast, validated gang leaders remain segregated in Administrative Segregation Housing Units under 22-hour solitary confinement, confined to bare holding cells while continuing to rule life behind the cement walls through violence and drug trafficking.

Direct Assist, a personal injury claims company based in Bolton, collapsed after being fined £80,000 by the Information Commissioner's Office for making nuisance calls to households registered with the Telephone Preference Service, even telling one complainant they would be called for three years until making a claim. The company received 801 complaints between January 2013 and July 2014, deliberately instructing staff to use phone numbers from TPS lists despite lacking permission, leaving some elderly and vulnerable customers afraid to answer their phones and demonstrating a blatant disregard for the law. The ICO's enforcement action signals a firm stance against cold calling violations, with new regulations coming into effect that will make it easier for regulators to fine companies engaging in this irresponsible marketing behavior.

Jose Mourinho has established himself as the best defensive manager in Premier League history, with an astounding 101 clean sheets from 189 matches—a 53.4 per cent clean sheet rate that beats Rafa Benitez and all other Premier League managers. His defensive prowess has twice helped Chelsea win the league, and he also heads the list with the lowest average of goals conceded per game at 0.63. While other notable managers like Sir Alex Ferguson, Carlo Ancelotti, and Arsene Wenger have built impressive defensive records, Mourinho's statistics demonstrate his unmatched ability to shut out opponents and maintain defensive discipline.

A 15-year-old girl from Cape Town, South Africa, was detained at Cape Town International Airport while attempting to join ISIS, joining cases like a Virginia teen accused of being an ISIS recruiter and Australia teens suspected of attempting similar activities. The girl had been using social media to interact with unknown people and consume material related to the terrorist group, though authorities are still investigating how she was recruited and how her airfare was arranged. After being debriefed along with her family, the girl was released into her family's care as officials work to address the growing concern of youth radicalization and recruitment to terrorist organizations globally.

# Summary

Ben Powers, who played Keith Anderson, the husband of Thelma on the final season of the classic CBS sitcom "Good Times" (1978-1979), has died at age 64 on April 6 in New Bedford, Massachusetts. Throughout his career, Powers appeared in various television shows including a regular role on the CBS detective drama "Mickey Spillane's Mike Hammer" and guest spots on popular sitcoms, after being discovered performing stand-up comedy at Playboy clubs and on the revived "Laugh-In" in 1977. Powers attended the Rhode Island School of Design before pursuing his acting career and is survived by his mother and sisters Yvonne and Maya.

# Summary

A 39-year-old man, known on Facebook as Karl Kay, was shot multiple times while sitting in a car in his mother's Altona Meadows driveway in Melbourne's southwest at approximately 2am on Sunday, leaving behind six children. The shooting victim died shortly after paramedics arrived at the scene, and police believe the attack was targeted, though no arrests have been made. Neighbors reported hearing screeching tires after the shots were fired, but no eyewitnesses have come forward to assist the homicide investigation.

The author, an amateur photographer, enjoyed a luxury safari at Ulusaba Game Reserve in South Africa's Sabi Sands, Sir Richard Branson's private reserve, where he photographed a 4,500lb horned rhino and a crash of three fearless rhinos from an open-sided Land Rover alongside lions, elephants, and handsome antelope. With 1,000 rhinos killed by poachers annually, experienced guides Danny and Trevor helped him pursue elusive cheetah across the African plains before discovering leopard cubs frolicking in a tree. The trip offered magical wildlife encounters that rewarded his graduation and fulfilled his ambition to swap photographing Ferraris for the raw beauty of the African bush.

After neighbors blocked George Lucas from building a Lucasfilm Ltd studio complex on Lucas Valley Road in the Bay Area, the Star Wars director dropped those plans and pivoted to create the largest affordable housing development in the area instead. The $200 million project would provide homes for 224 low-income families and seniors on 52 acres of his Grady Ranch estate in Marin County, California. While Lucas's representatives claim the shift is not retaliatory, the move demonstrates how the filmmaker chose to benefit the community when his original film studio plans were rejected by local residents.

Photographer Marc Latremouille captured a bird's eye view of a great grey owl with a five-foot wingspan swooping down to attack a tiny field mouse in snowy fields near Ontario, Canada. The predatory bird stalked its prey from snowy treetops before launching its attack, making an inelegant landing in the snow to scoop up the rodent with its claws. The great grey owl's soft feathers enable it to glide silently, allowing it to strike its prey by surprise with deadly precision.

Author Martin Fletcher, a survivor of the Valley Parade fire that killed 56 people at Bradford City Football Club on May 11, 1985, has claimed in a new book that the devastating fire was not an accident and has linked then-Bradford chairman Stafford Heginbotham to eight other blazes at businesses he owned or managed over an 18-year period. Although an official inquiry concluded the Bradford fire was caused by a discarded cigarette in the wooden stand, Fletcher's research suggests Heginbotham's history with fires that resulted in significant insurance claims warranted further investigation. Despite the new allegations, former sports minister Gerry Sutcliffe and the original inquiry judge Sir Olive Popplewell maintain that the fire was an accident, though Popplewell acknowledged that the previous blazes would have demanded investigation had they been known at the time.

Bacary Sagna, whose acrimonious departure from Arsenal last summer dismayed many Gunners fans, insists he has no regrets about joining Manchester City despite the club's underwhelming season as Premier League holders now languishing in fourth place while Arsenal sits second. The 32-year-old defender, who has made only 16 appearances for City this season, stated he would make the same decision again, believing Manuel Pellegrini's side will return to winning ways and finish the season well while fending off threats to their Champions League qualifying places. Though Sagna could end the campaign trophyless and lower than Arsenal in the Premier League, he remains philosophical about both clubs' diverging fortunes and confident in City's ability to secure European football.

Nigel Farage suggested that Prime Minister David Cameron has been secretly using hair dye to turn back the years, claiming after shaking his hand at last week's leaders debate that he is "jealous" of how Cameron has reversed the greying process while in office. Cameron has previously been accused of visiting stylists and trichologists to keep his hair looking youthful, though Downing Street denied in 2011 that he dyed his hair, stating the Prime Minister had other things to think about. A Tory party spokesman refused to comment on whether Cameron actually dyes his hair, despite observations that his grey hairs have looked less prominent over time.

Australian woman Evita Nicole Sarmonikas, 29, a receptionist at LJ Hooker in Surfer's Paradise, died during a Brazilian butt lift procedure performed by Dr. Victor Ramirez at a Mexican clinic in March, with her family flying to Sydney after retrieving her body following pressure from the clinic to have her cremated before an autopsy. The surgeon had lost another patient, Roseann Falcon Ornelas, the previous year, and an independent investigation revealed discrepancies in the clinic's medical records and unsafe conditions. The tragic incident has prompted warnings from the Australian Medical Association about the dangers of undergoing cosmetic surgery in developing countries, and Evita's family has urged women to accept their natural bodies rather than risk their lives pursuing surgical perfection.

Help for Heroes has launched a fundraising campaign to help 96-year-old World War II veteran Robert Clark remain in his own home in London after Brent Council—which had contributed £350 weekly toward his care—refused to continue funding his live-in carer, threatening instead to move him into a care home. Mr. Clark, one of Britain's oldest surviving prisoners of war who endured Hitler's brutal 1,000-mile death march and camps like Campo PG70, has already spent his £50,000 life savings on care costs and now faces extreme anxiety at the prospect of institutional living, which he says would remind him of his traumatic wartime captivity. Despite a petition signed by over 165,000 people and an emergency grant from Help for Heroes, the council insists it cannot afford the £960-weekly care package and has offered only £451 weekly, forcing Mr. Clark to either enter a care home or pay the shortfall himself.

# Summary

FC Chernomorets Odessa, a Ukrainian Premier League club struggling financially due to severe funding and sponsorship shortages, is offering their players luxury houses valued at approximately £67,000 near the sea to cover salary debts. The club's general manager Sergey Kernitskiy revealed this novel solution as the team faces the threat of UEFA disqualification and relegation if they cannot settle their outstanding debts to current and former players. Some players have had their monthly salaries slashed from over £7,000 to £2,000 or less as a result of the club's dire financial situation.

Kestutis Martuzevicius, a Lithuanian gangster who arrived in Britain in 1996 and is wanted for 22 crimes including two murders, cost British taxpayers hundreds of thousands of pounds by repeatedly delaying his extradition through human rights appeals, claiming depression and mental illness while held in Belmarsh Prison and Broadmoor Hospital. After nearly five years of legal battles, the European Court of Human Rights ruled his case "manifestly ill-founded" and ordered his deportation back to his home country. The case has reignited concerns about foreign criminals exploiting human rights laws to avoid facing justice in their homeland.

Police are investigating a gang of vigilante paedophile hunters in the North-West who pose as underage girls online to lure men into meetings, then attack and beat them while filming the assaults for blackmail purposes. A 19-year-old teenager told Greater Manchester Police he was beaten by the gang in a church car park in Atherton after being tricked into believing he was meeting a 14-year-old girl, while a second victim, Andrew O'Neill, 25, was assaulted in Wigan's Market Place on Saturday and admitted to grooming an underage girl. Police have warned that vigilante justice is unacceptable and are urging the public to report any information about the gang's activities.

Real Madrid's fringe players trained at the Valdebebas complex on Wednesday while Champions League starters rested following their 0-0 draw against rivals Atletico on Tuesday, with Pepe climbing highest as the squad worked through drills and kept the ball moving. Players including Isco and Sami Khedira (linked with a Premier League move) participated in Wednesday's session ahead of Real Madrid's La Liga match against Malaga on Saturday. The team will face Atletico again in next Wednesday's second leg, with manager Carlo Ancelotti aiming to secure their first win against their neighbors this season.

David Hibbitt, a 33-year-old bowel cancer patient told he had 18 months to five years to live, claims cannabis oil cured his advanced cancer after he stopped chemotherapy and continued taking the drug instead. Following an all-clear scan in January, Hibbitt credits the cannabis oil—which he felt was less damaging than chemotherapy that was killing his energy—with his recovery, though Cancer Research UK stresses there is no solid evidence from clinical trials proving cannabis extracts safely and effectively treat cancer in patients.

President Barack Obama visited Everglades National Park on Earth Day, accompanied by Bill Nye 'the Science Guy,' to highlight how climate change is threatening America's natural treasures and the economies of surrounding communities. During his tour of the Anhinga Trail in South Florida, Obama emphasized that rising sea levels and salt water intrusion are already damaging the Everglades and other iconic parks like Joshua Tree National Park, and he called for immediate action to reduce carbon emissions and ensure natural monuments remain the birthright of all Americans for generations to come. However, the day trip drew criticism for its environmental cost, as Air Force One consumed 9,000 gallons of fuel for the round-trip flight to Florida.

Real Madrid failed to activate their option to buy Manchester United striker Javier Hernandez outright before the midnight Thursday deadline, when the exclusivity period from last summer's deal—which allowed Real Madrid to first loan Hernandez before purchasing him—expired without Madrid making a formal offer. The 26-year-old has recently impressed during training with his Real Madrid team-mates, delivering five goals in his last six games, including a decisive strike against Atletico Madrid and two goals in Madrid's 4-2 win at Celta Vigo. With the door now open to other suitors, West Ham, Southampton, Newcastle, and Lazio are positioned to bid for the Mexican international, whom United values at around £10 million.

Bonnie Doll, Australia's answer to Lara Croft in the gaming world, has turned her passion for Call of Duty into a lucrative career through live streaming on Twitch, earning enough to leave her previous hospitality job. Originally from Sweden, Bonnie now has over 52,000 global followers on the streaming platform, with her channel accumulating nearly one million views through subscriber fees, ad revenue, and donations. While she enjoys the financial success and flexibility of working from home, Bonnie admitted that dealing with online trolls, gender-based attacks, and invasive fans remains the most challenging aspect of her streaming career.

YouTube has announced its first ad-free, subscription-based service to YouTube Partners through updated Partner Program Terms, which will offer creators 55% of net revenues from subscription fees—matching their advertising revenue split. The company is announcing YouTube Music Key beta, a subscription music service that rivals Spotify by offering ad-free listening, offline viewing, and music videos for £9.99 per month, currently available by invite only. Additionally, YouTube has introduced a new Music Tab to help users more easily discover music videos, recommended playlists, and full albums from their favorite artists.

Marion "Suge" Knight, the former rap music mogul, was ordered to stand trial Thursday on murder and attempted-murder charges after a judge concluded he intentionally struck two men with his pickup truck in January, killing Terry Carter and seriously injuring Cle 'Bone' Sloan, then rushed to the hospital following the ruling. Knight's defense claims he was ambushed and attempting to escape Sloan's attack when he hit the men, while prosecutors contend the incident was deliberate, with the case complicated by Sloan's reluctance to testify against Knight due to concerns about being a "snitch." Knight faces up to life in prison if convicted and had his bail reduced from $25 million to $10 million.

Bailey Murrill, a young patient from Texas who had been inexplicably paralyzed for 11 days, shocked her favorite nurse by suddenly getting up and walking towards her in the hospital, prompting the emotional caregiver to scream with joy and burst into tears. The heartwarming moment was captured on video by Bailey's mother, Becky Murrill, and shared online where it went viral with over 1 million views. Though the cause of Bailey's unexpected paralysis remains unknown, she has made a full recovery and regained the ability to walk.

WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange, who has been sheltering in the Ecuadorian Embassy in London since Ecuador granted him political asylum in 2012, has agreed to be interviewed by Swedish prosecutors at the embassy after prosecutors previously refused to travel. Swedish prosecutors and Ecuadorian authorities must now coordinate the interview, though prosecutors acknowledge that conducting it in London would lower the quality of the investigation, yet time is running out as some alleged crimes face an August 2015 statute of limitations deadline. Assange has denied the 2010 allegations of rape and sexual molestation and fears Sweden would extradite him to the United States, where he could face the death penalty for publishing government secrets through WikiLeaks.

Big Brother winner Pete Bennett, who won the show in 2006, revealed on Jeremy Kyle today that he is now homeless and living off friends' charity after his life spiraled downhill following his fame. Despite receiving £100,000 in prize money and publishing a successful autobiography, Pete struggled with addiction to the Class B drug ketamine, which he believes was triggered by the pressures of reality TV fame, and has also been affected by the deaths of seven friends from drug-related incidents. After seeking help from a drugs counselor and overcoming his addiction—during which his behavior became noticeably more controlled—Pete started rebuilding his life and pursuing acting, having also made significant progress in managing his Tourette's Syndrome.

Borussia Dortmund returned to winning ways with a comfortable 3-0 victory over relegation-threatened Paderborn at Signal Iduna Park, with Henrikh Mkhitaryan opening the scoring before Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang doubled the lead—celebrating with his trademark front flip—and Shinji Kagawa sealed the win, as goalkeeper Lukas Kruse proved unable to keep Mkhitaryan's header out at the near post. The victory moved Dortmund to eighth place and within three points of sixth-place Augsburg, giving them a realistic chance of qualifying for European football. The triumph came three days after manager Jurgen Klopp announced his departure at season's end, with home fans holding up signs to honor their soon-to-be-departing manager's achievements over seven years.

Lee Min-bok, a North Korean defector, launches thousands of copies of the Hollywood film "The Interview" across the border from South to North Korea via helium-filled balloons, alongside DVDs, dollar bills, and political leaflets, believing the movie's portrayal of Kim Jong Un as a vulnerable man rather than the god-like figure promoted by Pyongyang's state media can inform ordinary North Koreans and advance reunification. Despite opposition from South Korea's local residents who fear retaliation after Pyongyang fired on similar balloons last October, Lee makes nightly launches under military protection, determined to tell truths that would prove fatal if spoken in North Korea. Though Pyongyang condemns the campaign as "an act of terrorism," the regime's fierce reaction suggests some information is successfully reaching North Korean territory.

Israeli developers have created Optimal, a thermal protection case that prevents smartphones from overheating by using micro-fans to automatically cool the device, while resistance coils provide gentle heating in freezing conditions, with temperature sensors continuously monitoring the phone's temperature via Bluetooth connectivity. The case also includes an app that tracks temperature changes over time and identifies which apps have the highest thermal impact on the device, allowing users to set custom temperature zones and alarm thresholds to maintain optimal phone performance.

# Summary

A Russian commercial fishing vessel called the Dalniy Vostok freezer trawler sank off the Kamchatka Peninsula in Russia's Far East, resulting in at least 54 deaths and 15 people still missing out of the 132 people on board. The trawler went down in the Sea of Okhotsk within 15 minutes after encountering difficulties, likely caused by a collision that damaged the hull and was exacerbated by the weight of approximately 80 tons of fish on deck. More than 60 people were rescued from the chilly waters, and over 20 fishing vessels are actively searching for the remaining 15 missing individuals.

The food industry has seen an explosion of apps designed to help with meal planning and dining decisions, with developers releasing new options almost daily. This article highlights ten essential apps every foodie should have, including Where Chefs Eat featuring chef recommendations, British Street Food with live GPS maps showing where vendors are trading, Berry's Wine List for wine connoisseurs, and Fromage for cheese enthusiasts, alongside free options like Jamie Oliver's Recipes, Love Food Hate Waste for meal planning, and OpenTable for restaurant reservations. Whether you need help planning your evening meal, managing your budget, or discovering the best places to eat dinner, these apps offer solutions for every aspect of food shopping, cooking, and dining out.

Frances Clarkson, the 53-year-old estranged wife of Jeremy Clarkson whom she married in 1993, enjoyed a family holiday in Barbados over Easter, where she appeared relaxed in a white skimpy swimsuit with a black palm tree print and a Help For Heroes wristband, displaying her holiday tan. While Frances vacationed in the Caribbean, her husband continued to deal with the fallout from being sacked from Top Gear after punching a producer, with reports indicating the couple have been living apart for years due to his alleged affairs, including a long-standing relationship with events organizer Phillipa Sage. The article also details Clarkson's various controversies throughout his career, including racist remarks he used during Top Gear episodes.

# Summary

Discount supermarket Aldi is under fire for failing to notify customers of a 0.5 percent surcharge on credit card transactions and tap-and-go payments, six months after promising the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) it would implement proper signage and disclosure measures. Despite committing to display signs at store entrances and registers and have cashiers inform customers of the charges, many Aldi stores still lack adequate signage or display it in tiny print, leaving customers unaware of the additional fees. Aldi defends the surcharge as necessary to cover credit card acceptance costs and maintains it has always charged these fees, but the company has been criticized for not adequately disclosing the charges on both credit and debit card tap-and-go transactions.

During World War II, the HMS Ulster Queen, a former Irish Sea ferry converted into a battle cruiser, carried Russian bullion loaded onto her in Murmansk and arrived in the River Clyde in November 1942 after surviving an Arctic convoy where two ships were sunk by German U-boats. During the transfer of the precious cargo, one case of gold and silver bars slipped and fell overboard into the muddy waters—an incident that remained secret for decades until revealed through a diary kept by Leonard H. Thomas, one of the Ulster Queen's engineers who had previously served on the research ship RRS Discovery II and participated in four convoys to Russia. The fate of the lost bullion remains unknown, and it may still lie at the bottom of the Clyde.

Luis Suarez's wife Sofia revealed to Canal 10 that the Barcelona star initially denied biting Italy defender Giorgio Chiellini during the 2014 World Cup in Brazil, though the Uruguay striker's shoulder bite was captured on television. After 10 days of media scrutiny, Suarez admitted the truth to his wife, and the Uruguayan subsequently received a four-month ban for the incident. Despite the controversy, he completed his £75 million move to Barcelona where he has since thrived alongside Messi and Neymar.

A man named Ranulfo Perez, 48, who dresses as the Cookie Monster in Times Square, was arrested for allegedly groping a 16-year-old girl's breasts, but was later released by the Manhattan District Attorney due to insufficient evidence, as the teenager lost sight of her attacker and could not confirm it was him. The incident exemplifies a persistent problem with costumed characters in Times Square, where nearly 20 such performers have been arrested over recent years for sexual assault, harassment, and disorderly conduct—offenses ranging from an Elmo impersonator's aggressive panhandling to a Woody costumed character accused of sexual assault. Authorities have launched "Operation Sesame Street" to regulate the performers and are considering requiring licenses costing $175.

The son of Canadian Consul General Roxanne Dubé, 17-year-old Jean Wabafiyebazu, was shot dead during a gunfight on Monday in Miami when he and his brother Marc attempted to rob drug dealers during a marijuana purchase, leaving another victim, Joshua Wright, also killed and a third man wounded. Marc, 15, and Anthony Rodriguez were arrested and charged with felony murder, while police investigate how the young men obtained their weapons. The incident occurred at a home in the Coral Way area when the brothers allegedly posed as buyers for two pounds of marijuana valued at $5,000 before attempting the robbery.

Dr Omar Saadi Al-Atraqchi, a 42-year-old family doctor, faces being struck off the medical register after admitting to secretly filming men in gym showers by cutting holes in Lynx shampoo bottles and concealing his iPhone inside the device to record footage. He was caught twice at Pure Gym in Holloway when gym users noticed the bottles and camera lens in their shower cubicles—once when a man spotted him repositioning the bottle and again when staff wrenched open his cubicle as he fled with the shower gel bottle—leading to him being banned from all Pure Gyms for life and fined £500. The Medical Practitioners Tribunal Service found him guilty of misconduct and voyeurism, determining that his poor insight and impulsive behavior pose a risk of reoffending and undermine public confidence in the medical profession.

John T. Booker Jr., a former U.S. Army enlistee who posted on Facebook about "the adrenaline rush" of dying in jihad, was arrested Friday near Fort Riley military base in Kansas while attempting to detonate a car bomb that had been constructed with inert materials by FBI confidential informants. Booker said he had enlisted planning to commit an insider attack and later became inspired by ISIS, eventually deciding that nearby Fort Riley would be an ideal target for a truck bombing to kill American soldiers. A second man, Alexander E. Blair, was charged with failing to report the felony after learning of Booker's bomb plot and loaning him money for storage space, though Fort Riley's security was never breached and no one was in danger.

Swedish scientists analyzed over 10,000 families and found that children experiencing stressful events such as death, illness, or family separation during childhood are three times more likely to develop type 1 diabetes, a condition where the immune system kills beta cells that produce insulin. The researchers suggest that serious life events may affect these beta cells and raise insulin demands to cope with elevated stress hormones, though genetic predisposition remains the most important factor—particularly compared to type 2 diabetes, which is associated with being overweight. Among the families studied, 58 children were subsequently diagnosed with type 1 diabetes, with many experiencing damaging events in their formative years.

Fifteen-year-old African American Jamar Nicholson is suing Los Angeles for $20 million after being shot in the back by LAPD officer Miguel Gutierrez, who approached with guns drawn and mistook a toy gun with an orange tip held by Nicholson's friend for a real weapon. According to Nicholson's attorneys, the officers "shot first and asked questions later," contradicting the LAPD's claim that they ordered the teenager holding the replica gun to drop it multiple times. Nicholson survived but suffered injuries and trauma, and he now feels nervous around police officers.

A man was discovered lying in the foetal position on the baggage reclaim conveyor at Domodedovo Airport in Russia, taking an unusual snooze while passengers waited to collect their luggage. While the dozing man circled around on the belt, most passengers appeared focused on claiming their own luggage rather than concerned about his choice of resting spot. This incident echoes similar cases at other airports and train stations, where inebriated passengers have fallen asleep on luggage belts and conveyors.

Cadbury's is releasing a special edition seven-row Dairy Milk Spectacular for the first time in its history, featuring seven different fillings including classic Dairy Milk, Caramel, Fruit & Nut, Whole Nut, Oreo, Daim, and Turkish varieties in one super bar designed to get chocolate lovers to try new flavors. However, only 50 of these limited-edition bars have been created for a competition in partnership with food artist Prudence Staite. The brand developed this super bar containing seven different fillings based on research showing that Britons are more likely to experiment with new tastes when recommended by loved ones.

Muhammadu Buhari, a 72-year-old former military leader who once ruled Nigeria with strict discipline, won the nation's presidential election in a historic peaceful transition of power, running on an anti-corruption platform after ordinary Nigerians grew tired of elite venality and a system of crony capitalism—epitomized by backroom deals at venues like the Abuja Hilton Hotel—that left the country's poor victimized despite enormous oil wealth. Buhari's victory is particularly significant because a Muslim candidate won in Christian areas, challenging Western assumptions about Islam and governance, and he promises to address the intertwined problems of corruption and the Boko Haram terrorist insurgency that has plagued the nation, particularly Muslim population centers in the north, where previous governments' dysfunction rather than any strategic ingenuity by the militants allowed the violence to persist. The author argues that Buhari's win reflects Nigerians' exhaustion with years of government misrule, and hopes that his administration will restore military discipline and effectively combat both corruption and terrorism.

Disgraced comedian Daniel O'Reilly has resurrected his controversial "Dapper Laughs" persona—the character that originally sprang to fame on Vine—during a trip to Sydney, documenting his journey via Doha and Melbourne in videos featuring inappropriate comments toward air stewardesses and airport workers. This comeback follows the cancellation of O'Reilly's ITV show "Dapper Laughs: On The Pull" after over 60,000 people signed a petition and 44 comedians published an open letter condemning his sexist and degrading content. Despite declaring the character dead on BBC Newsnight and admitting he had gotten "carried away" with his social media success, O'Reilly resurrected the persona on Christmas Day, signaling the controversial work "isn't finished yet."

Nine British Muslims, including Waheed Ahmed and other members of Labour councillor Shakil Ahmed's family, were arrested in Turkey while attempting to cross into ISIS-controlled Syria and are expected to return to the UK this week. Outspoken Rochdale candidate Simon Danczuk said the family would not be welcome back in Rochdale and called it "unacceptable" that people prepared to join ISIS should be allowed to return to Britain without facing terror charges. Mr. Danczuk also criticized the Government's anti-terror strategy, arguing that more needs to be done to prevent radicalization as over 600 Britons are now thought to have fled to join ISIS.

Avril Lavigne, the 30-year-old singer, revealed that she has been suffering from Lyme disease, a debilitating bacterial infection transmitted through tick bites whose symptoms range from headaches and joint pain to severe fatigue and paralysis, which left her bedridden for five months feeling like she was dying. Uncertain of when the bites occurred or where she contracted the infection, Lavigne was initially dismissed by doctors before a specialist finally diagnosed her several months later, during which time she was fully suffering the effects of the disease's complete weakness and inability to breathe, speak, or move. Supported by her husband Chad Kroeger from Nickelback and her mother during her recovery at home in Ontario, Lavigne has now recovered approximately 80 percent and wants to raise awareness about the condition, which affects thousands of people annually according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

An Egyptian court has sentenced 71 people to life in prison for their role in the August 2013 burning of the Virgin Mary Church in Kafr Hakim, Giza province, which was part of coordinated attacks on at least 42 churches across Egypt that month following the military's removal of President Mohamed Morsy. The mob that torched and looted the church chanted against Coptic Christians and called for Egypt to become an "Islamic state," with other targeted churches including St. George Church in Sohag and Prince Tadros Church in Fayoum. Most defendants—52 of the 73—were tried in absentia and are getting life sentences, while two minors received 10-year sentences, according to Egypt's official news reports.

Australian police arrested five young men in Melbourne on Saturday in connection with an ISIS-inspired terror plot targeting Anzac Day ceremonies on April 25, with Australia's Federal Police confirming the suspects planned to commit acts in preparation for terrorist attacks against police officers. Two suspects, including 18-year-old Sevdet Besim, have been charged with conspiring to commit these acts, though authorities deliberately avoided the term ISIS, instead referring to the group as the "Daesh death cult." Police emphasized that the individuals were acting alone and expressed concern about the young age of those involved in the plot.

Aston Villa wide man Carles N'Zogbia turned up for training at the club's Bodymoor Heath complex on Tuesday in an outrageously flamboyant flowery ensemble and sunglasses, earning scathing criticism from goalkeeper Shay Given who called it "the worst ever" on Twitter. Tim Sherwood, the Aston Villa boss known for his own sartorial quirks—particularly his penchant for a gilet during his Tottenham managerial stint—has regularly featured N'Zogbia since his February appointment. Despite the fashion misstep, Villa continues to thrive, moving six points clear of the Premier League relegation zone after defeating Tottenham on Saturday.

British photographer Laura Stevens, left heartbroken following a painful breakup, created a therapeutic photography project called "Another November" in which she directed friends and women she met to portray the emotional stages of grief after losing love. The photographic narrative features intimate images of women isolated in domestic Parisian spaces, capturing feelings of pain, confusion, loneliness, and loss as they enact the evolving chapters of heartbreak. By constructing these visual narratives, Stevens gained perspective on her own emotional journey while allowing the women posing to process their own stories of lost love and transformation.

# Wedding Rehearsal Summary

Andy Murray, the British No. 1 tennis player, attended his wedding rehearsal at Dunblane Cathedral in his Scottish hometown on Friday ahead of his Saturday wedding to long-term girlfriend Kim Sears. The couple, who have been together for 10 years after meeting at the 2005 US Open, ran through the wedding service with family and friends at the cathedral. Murray appeared slightly nervous during the rehearsal, though he and Sears left the cathedral together ahead of their last night of being single, while the local community of Dunblane decorated the streets to celebrate their returning hero's special occasion.

Hummelstown police officer Lisa Mearkle was charged with criminal homicide last month for fatally shooting unarmed motorist David Kassick twice in the back on February 2 as he lay face-down on the ground. Video footage recorded by Mearkle's stun gun captured the shooting and has been called the strongest evidence in the case by prosecutors, though her defense attorneys are attempting to prevent its release. Mearkle claims she fired in self-defense believing Kassick was reaching for a weapon, while the arrest affidavit states the video clearly shows Kassick was unarmed and posed no threat when she shot him.

Mick Schumacher, the 16-year-old son of seven-time Formula 1 champion Michael Schumacher, made his Formula 4 pre-season test debut at the Oschersleben circuit in Germany with Van Amersfoort Racing, just weeks after suffering a 100mph crash at Lausitzring. During the training session, Mick was pictured accidentally driving through gravel, though he walked away from the previous crash uninjured. After seven years of karting and finishing second in the world, European, and German championships last season, the young driver is now working to establish himself in racing under his team manager's guidance.

Bruce Jenner will chronicle his transition into a woman in an upcoming eight-episode docu-series on E! starting July 27, following his widely-viewed Diane Sawyer 20/20 special, though his children—including sons Burt, Brandon, and Brody, eldest daughter Casey, and stepdaughter Kim Kardashian—worry the show will diminish his accomplishments and have urged him to keep the rest of his journey private. Despite their concerns, Bruce remains committed to the docu-series, believing it will educate people and create empathy, as his family members have expressed overall support for his transformation.

The RSPCA is reconsidering its controversial policy of pursuing fox hunting prosecutions after the collapse of its latest case against William Bryer, joint master of the renowned Cattistock Hunt in Dorset, leaving the animal charity with no remaining legal actions under the Hunting Act. The charity has faced criticism for spending £22.5 million on prosecutions and has a history of dropped cases—including against the Cheshire Forest Hunt—where defendants claimed insufficient evidence or withheld information, with hunting offences often pursued against individuals rather than official hunts that increasingly use trail hunting to remain within the law. Following recommendations to leave prosecutions to the Crown Prosecution Service, the RSPCA is now reviewing whether to abandon these cases and refocus on its core mission of animal rescue and rehabilitation rather than pursuing what critics describe as pointless court battles.

Darren Stanton, a former police officer and the UK's top human lie detector and body expert, appeared on ITV's This Morning to reveal that contrary to popular belief, liars often stare rather than avoid eye contact. Stanton demonstrated his expertise by catching both Amanda Holden and Philip Schofield fibbing through micro-expressions like mouth flicks and raised eyebrows. The expert shared various techniques for detecting deception, including observing physical barriers, noticing hesitation and brief answers, and spotting involuntary twitches that commonly occur when people are being untruthful.

This article explores Holy Week and the Easter Triduum—the three sacred days beginning with Good Friday (commemorating Jesus's crucifixion and death), proceeding through Holy Saturday (a day of anxious transition), and culminating in Easter Sunday with the resurrection, a celebration that calls mostly to those who approach it with serious intent. The author emphasizes that while Jesus celebrated the Last Supper on Maundy Thursday to establish the foundation of this sacred weekend, Christians repeatedly experience this spiritual pattern of suffering, suspension, and transformation throughout their lives, as resurrection implies a total transformation beyond the physical realm. The article argues that the ritual enactment of these Easter season days serves as a powerful reminder that we should expect to move through darkness into light, offering a spiritual progression that resonates with the universal human experience of death, loss, and renewal.

Camoji is a new app that allows users to easily create and share animated GIFs with Instagram-style filters through Facebook Messenger, enabling seamless back-and-forth communication that may soon become commonplace in messaging. Facebook has opened up its Messenger platform to third-party developers, allowing them to create innovative apps and services for its 600 million users, marking the company's effort to transform Messenger into an independent messaging service that competes with email and other platforms. This integration represents Facebook's strategy shift from being a single app to a family of apps, giving users more tools for expression and connection.

Labour leader Ed Miliband admitted that his party made past mistakes on immigration and pledged new measures to crack down on the illegal exploitation of migrant workers, arguing that such exploitation drives low-skilled migration and suppresses British wages. He insisted migrants must learn to speak English to live together harmoniously in British communities, particularly those in public-facing NHS roles. However, Conservative immigration minister James Brokenshire criticized Miliband's proposals as ineffective for controlling net migration at 298,000 annually, warning that a Labour government would risk undoing the party's immigration enforcement gains.

Four terrified puppies strapped in a cage on top of a van were rescued by police in Akron, Ohio after dozens of concerned drivers called 911 during Wendell Steiner's 300-mile journey from Nova, Ohio to Pennsylvania. Sergeant Kris Beitzel found the wind-battered puppies huddled together and traumatized, though uninjured, and discovered the Mennonite family treated the animals as livestock rather than pets—a cultural misunderstanding that resulted in only a warning. The incident recalled former presidential candidate Mitt Romney's 1983 controversy when he drove 12 hours with his dog secured on top of his car, which had dogged his political campaigns for years.

Louis Smith will represent Great Britain at the European Championships in Montpellier for the first time since his double Olympic medal-winning performance at the 2012 London Games, where he won pommel horse silver and team bronze, following his competitive return at last year's Commonwealth Games. Smith was selected after winning gold on the pommels at the recent British Championships, joining other Olympic team-mates including reigning pommel horse champion Max Whitlock, Kristian Thomas, Sam Oldham, and Daniel Purvis on the six-man squad. The women's team features four-time Commonwealth Games champion Claudia Fragapane, alongside experienced competitors Becky Downie, Ellie Downie, and Amy Tinkler, with the championships taking place between April 15-19.

# Summary

New York taxi driver Mohammed Dahbi was ordered to pay a lesbian couple, actress Kassie Thornton and TV executive Christy Spitzer, $10,000 in damages after he ordered them to stop kissing in his cab and told them to "keep that behavior for the bedroom." When they refused to pay the fare and got out, Dahbi called them derogatory names, and a judge found him guilty of discrimination, also requiring him to attend anti-discrimination training and pay an additional $5,000 civil penalty to the city. Dahbi's legal team plans to appeal the decision, arguing the fine is unfair given the minor nature of the incident.

Jonas Gutierrez was dropped from Newcastle's squad for Sunday's 3-1 defeat to Tottenham following a heated row with head coach John Carver, who cited "selection reasons" while the midfielder tweeted his frustration about the club's treatment of him. The 31-year-old Argentinian, who has twice battled testicular cancer, will not be offered a new contract despite seven years at the club, as his deal expires in the summer—a decision rooted in Newcastle's controversial directive for him to find a new club during his initial cancer recovery in 2013. Gutierrez has spoken openly about his disappointment at how the club handled his illness, stating he won't forgive the manner in which he was treated during his battle with the disease.

ISIS militants entered the Palestinian-run Yarmouk camp in Damascus—under government siege for nearly two years with thousands of civilians trapped and dependent on U.N. food parcels—in a coordinated attack with the Al Qaeda-affiliated Nusra Front, marking their deepest infiltration into the Syrian capital, while Jordan simultaneously closed its only functioning border crossing with Syria due to heavy clashes between rebels and government forces. The jihadis, who already control large swaths of territory in northern Syria, clashed with Palestinian faction members and could potentially use the camp as a strategic base to threaten Assad's power. Additionally, gruesome images emerged showing ISIS militants stoning a blindfolded couple to death in Iraq for alleged fornication, with the execution carried out by the group's elderly Mosul-based executioner-in-chief in front of a large crowd.

Amanda Peake Glover, a South Carolina mother of four and co-owner of a fitness center, died on Saturday after collapsing moments after completing the Palmetto Half Marathon, leaving her husband Benji and close friends like Ashley Blocker—who met Glover at their gym and knew her as an devoted fitness enthusiast—shocked by her sudden death. Despite being an avid runner who had completed the Myrtle Beach Marathon just a month prior with her brother Josh, the exact cause of her death remained unclear. She is survived by her husband Benjamin and their four children: Hope, Reagan, Caroline, and Walker.

Ed Balls, Labour's shadow chancellor and husband of shadow home secretary Yvette Cooper, was left briefly stumped when asked what seven times six equals during a Q&A session following a speech in Leeds, taking ten seconds before correctly answering 42. This incident comes after Balls recently revealed on Good Morning Britain that he relies on his mother-in-law, a maths teacher, for help with mathematics. Balls joked that answering maths questions is "a very dangerous thing for politicians," stating he would not answer any more such questions.

Alastair Cook and Jonathan Trott have become the sixth English pair to score more than 100 runs together on 10 occasions in Test cricket, following legendary partnerships including Cook with both Strauss and Pietersen. Their opening-stand century marked England's first in over two years, while West Indies bowler Shannon Gabriel troubled England's batsmen with speeds reaching 94.3mph, striking Blackwood twice with Cook shots before dismissing both Cook and Bell. Meanwhile, New Zealand all-rounder Corey Anderson is doubtful for next month's Test series in England after breaking a finger, and England continues working with Adil Rashid to develop him into a top-class bowler.

Manhattan-based Skin Laundry's popular Light and Laser procedure promises to tighten skin, improve complexion, and leave clients' skin glowing, though the treatment requires signing an unusual eight-page waiver that releases personal information to a funeral director and coroner—details the New York Post highlighted that haven't deterred skin-obsessed New Yorkers from flocking to the clinic. Despite the ominous legal language, the Manhattan location has become so popular that Skin Laundry extended its hours to accommodate bookings, with many clients becoming regular customers who pay $100 per treatment or purchase discounted monthly memberships. While some clients report visible improvements in acne scars and skin appearance, skeptics like plastic surgeon Dr. Norman Rowe question the treatment's effectiveness given how frequently clients must return.

Tickets for Floyd Mayweather and Manny Pacquiao's eagerly-anticipated weigh-in on May 1 sold out within minutes on Friday evening when 10,000 tickets went on sale at MGM property box offices in Las Vegas, with fight fans queuing around the block and the MGM Resorts Box Office overwhelmed by calls for #MayweatherPacquiao weigh-tickets. The sale came just a day after Thursday night's rapid-fire ticket sale for the main fight itself sold out in under a minute, with secondary market prices soaring to over $128,000. The two fighters, who struck their poses together at a news conference last month, continue to drive unprecedented demand for what promises to be boxing's biggest spectacle.

Victoria's Secret Angel Alessandra Ambrosio was treated at UCLA Hospital's Ronald Reagan Medical Center in Los Angeles on Tuesday for constant headaches, despite appearing in perfect health while partying at the Coachella festival in California just days earlier on Sunday. The Brazilian supermodel had reportedly scheduled the procedure two weeks in advance and is currently being monitored at the facility, where her spokesperson confirmed she remains in good health. Ambrosio was attending Coachella with her fiancé Jamie Mazur and other supermodels before undergoing the medical procedure.

The Oxford University Women's Boat Race team were rescued by the Chiswick RNLI station on Wednesday after their boat was overcome by choppy waters on the Thames caused by strong winds against the tide. The crew and cox were safely returned to Putney, and their boat was recovered, just days before the women's and men's races taking place on Saturday, April 11, with the BNY Mellon Boat Race preceded by the RNLI Alternative Boat Race fundraising event on April 10. The RNLI, which has rescued over 3,600 people since 2002, praised the Oxford rowers for handling the situation calmly and safely.

Paris Saint-Germain playmaker Lucas Moura has praised fellow South American Lionel Messi ahead of PSG's Champions League clash against Barcelona on Wednesday night at Parc des Princes, revealing he is "obsessed" with the way Messi plays football and considers him the best player in the world. Despite the traditional rivalry between Brazilians and Argentines, however Moura acknowledged that stopping Messi will be very difficult and PSG will need to work hard to secure victory. PSG enter the encounter on the back of winning the French League Cup before they face Barcelona again at the Nou Camp on April 21.

Following Zayn Malik's departure from One Direction, the 22-year-old has faced multiple cheating accusations against his fiancée, Little Mix singer Perrie Edwards, with Australian waitress Courtney Webb claiming she bedded him, while model Lauren Richardson and Swedish model Martina Olsson have also come forward with allegations—marking the first time Zayn has been publicly confronted with such repeated infidelity claims. Relationship expert Tracey Cox warned that his level of fame at such a young age may be contributing to his pattern of behavior, yet Perrie, who was pictured boarding a private jet with him earlier this week, has remained by his side despite the ongoing accusations.

The article argues that businesses, including Marriott International—founded by J.W. Marriott and now advocating for LGBTQ+ travelers through its "#Love Travels" campaign—are increasingly rejecting anti-gay bias because supporting discrimination threatens their bottom line, particularly as younger consumers and millennials—72% of whom support same-sex marriage—use social media to mobilize boycotts against discriminatory laws in states like Arkansas and Indiana. Marriott's leadership pointed to the Bible's message of unconditional love while acknowledging the need to separate personal faith from business practices, recognizing that the LGBT travel market represents over $200 billion in global spending and that discrimination wherever it occurs now risks brand loyalty among younger generations. This shift reflects a new American reality where demographic and generational changes have made diversity-friendly policies not just a moral imperative—with 90% of youth believing they should help tackle bias—but also a sound business strategy for companies competing for consumer support.

TOWIE star and footballer Jake Hall scored Boston's second goal for non-league side Boston United on Monday during their Conference North match against Tamworth, impressing his on-screen girlfriend Chloe Lewis in the stands. Hall netted with his first touch just moments after coming off the bench to secure a 2-0 victory, with his co-star James 'Arg' Argent also present and excitedly celebrating from the sidelines. The goal was significant for the club's push toward the play-offs and demonstrated Hall's continued commitment to Boston United despite his rising fame on the popular Essex-based ITV show.

The U.S. military and foreign volunteers are supporting forces fighting ISIS in Iraq and Syria through training and military assistance, with active U.S. personnel like Lt. Colonel Schwemmer training Iraqi soldiers while organizations like 1st NAEF provide support to Kurdish Peshmerga forces in northern Iraq. Though some Western volunteers have joined Kurdish militia groups to fight ISIS, the Kurdish Regional Government and coalition forces don't need foreign fighters on the front lines, preferring instead logistical and training support as a more sustainable long-term solution. This approach also helps avoid playing into ISIS's divisive sectarian rhetoric by limiting non-Arab forces on the battlefield, while maintaining Kurdish pride in defending their own region.

Seventy years after British troops liberated Bergen-Belsen concentration camp in 1945, the story of Sergeant Norman Turgel and Polish survivor Gena Turgel exemplifies hope amid unimaginable horror—their romance, kindled when Norman spotted beauty in the starving inmate, was preserved by Major Leonard Berney's compassion as the camp transformed into a displaced persons center. The camp, where approximately 50,000 people including Dutch diarist Anne Frank perished from typhus and starvation at a rate of 500 per day, claimed its final victims even after liberation, with 13,000 unburied corpses discovered when soldiers arrived and later gave evidence at the Belsen war crimes trials. Norman and Gena married and built a life together in London with three children until his 1995 death, while Berney's memoir "Liberating Belsen Concentration Camp" and Gena's recollections continue to honor the lessons of Belsen's liberation.

Following Nepal's devastating earthquake, a CNN correspondent traveled six hours from Kathmandu to the town of Gorkha to document the humanitarian crisis unfolding near the epicenter, where a district hospital—ill-equipped for serious injuries and functioning primarily as a transit point—was overwhelmed with casualties from remote mountainous villages difficult to reach. Despite Gorkha appearing relatively intact, the hospital's emergency room and makeshift tarpaulin shelters were filled with injured patients suffering severe trauma, including 10-year-old Mita, while Nepalese and international medical teams treated them in far-from-ideal circumstances, transporting the most critical cases via helicopters and old buses to larger facilities. The dispatch demonstrated both the magnitude of the disaster and the resilience of Nepalese people alongside committed global humanitarians working to aid Nepal's suffering population.

Two Australian drug smugglers, Andrew Chan and Myuran Sukumaran, part of the "Bali Nine," face execution by firing squad in Indonesia after a court rejected their clemency appeal, marking a decade on death row for their involvement in a failed heroin smuggling plot. Several other members of the group, including 37-year-old Renae Lawrence and others arrested at Denpasar International Airport with heroin strapped to their bodies, received life sentences or reduced 20-year sentences despite claims Chan threatened them—allegations he has denied—while other members say they were coerced into the operation. Despite Australia's repeated calls for clemency citing the men's rehabilitation, President Joko Widodo has maintained Indonesia's tough stance on drug smugglers since taking office.

A new study from Oxford scientists proposes that young Earth may have been struck by a Mercury-like body about 4.5 billion years ago, providing the heat source that powers the planet's core and magnetic field. This collision would also account for the unusual abundance of rare-earth elements like samarium and neodymium in Earth's mantle that don't match the composition of meteorites. Additionally, this Mars-sized but Mercury-like impactor could be the same body responsible for creating the moon.

Red Bull's dominance in Formula One has ended, and the team has struggled since their dominant four years at the top, falling behind Mercedes and Ferrari this season. Daniel Ricciardo, the Red Bull driver, took instructions from a martial artist at Guyi Garden in Shanghai as the Formula One circus arrived for the Chinese Grand Prix, practicing stances in hopes of gaining mental discipline to help turn around the team's disappointing start to 2015. Though the martial arts training may not provide the performance gains needed to bring Red Bull back to the front of the grid, the experience could help Ricciardo cope with what appears to be a frustrating year ahead.

An unidentified drone carrying traces of radioactive material landed on the rooftop of Japan's Prime Minister's official residence in Tokyo, likely as a protest against the government's nuclear energy plans. The four-propeller, 50-centimeter wide device contained a camera and a plastic bottle with caesium, which Tokyo Metropolitan Police determined to be harmless to humans, prompting authorities to launch investigations and consider new drone regulations. The incident reflects growing public concern over Japan's nuclear power plants following the 2011 Fukushima Daiichi disaster, as Prime Minister Shinzo Abe pushes to restart reactors to reduce dependence on imported fossil fuels.

According to the article, ISIS is both a religious and political organization driven by power, with its military core composed of former Baathist officers from Saddam Hussein's army, as revealed in an internal ISIS report. While ISIS presents itself as a global organization, it has thrived primarily due to local Sunni rebellion in Iraq and Syria against Shiite rule, though people living under its brutal, theocratic governance—like those under the Taliban—grow increasingly disenchanted, yet the group slaughters its way to control without popular consent. Despite tactical military setbacks, the threat ISIS poses remains difficult to assess, as the organization functions primarily as a messaging and recruitment machine that spreads murky propaganda through viral videos to attract alienated young men worldwide.

Following a leaders' debate, internet users created numerous memes referencing the politicians involved, with Ed Miliband featured in several images including one where he was superimposed over London rapper Plan B issuing a "come at me bruv" call to Prime Minister David Cameron. David Cameron and Nick Clegg, who did not participate in the debate, were humorously superimposed onto the Channel 4 series Gogglebox sofa, while other memes showed the Labour leader doing a Kate Winslet in Titanic and images displayed Scottish National Party leader Nicola Sturgeon posing with a sceptical expression. The five participating party leaders were also reimagined as the Mighty Morphin Power Rangers, with Nigel Farage notably isolated after being shunned by the other politicians during the debate.

Several Manchester City fans were ejected from Old Tattoo on Sunday after allegedly mocking the 1958 Munich Air Disaster by making airplane wing gestures to taunt home supporters during Manchester United's 4-2 derby victory. The Munich tragedy killed 23 people, including former Manchester City goalkeeper Frank Swift who was covering the match as a journalist, when Matt Busby's talented young Manchester United side crashed during a takeoff attempt from Munich after refueling following a European Cup clash against Red Star Belgrade. Despite the incident and initial concerns about the late 4pm kick-off time potentially increasing disorder, police in Greater Manchester praised the overwhelming majority of fans' behavior, with only eight arrests made during the derby match.

Manchester City manager Manuel Pellegrini has admitted that the club's academy players are not ready for the first team, suggesting City may instead invest in established English talent from rival clubs to meet Premier League regulations requiring a homegrown quota. With Liverpool's Raheem Sterling reportedly unwilling to sign a new contract at Anfield and Everton's Ross Barkley also linked with City, Pellegrini's comments indicate the defending champions could target top young players from other Premier League clubs, including Arsenal's Jack Wilshere, particularly as City's current homegrown group will be reduced when Frank Lampard departs and James Milner may leave for Liverpool. Facing eight crucial matches to close a gap behind Chelsea, Pellegrini acknowledged the importance of the academy while expressing confidence that future talent will emerge, but made clear that bringing in the best available players remains the priority for a club of City's ambitions.

A 28-year-old heavily pregnant Jehovah's Witness in Sydney died along with her baby after refusing a blood transfusion necessary for treating her leukaemia, diagnosed seven months into her pregnancy. Though doctors offered her a caesarean section and chemotherapy, she refused both procedures on religious grounds, prioritizing her unborn child's life over her own; her baby died in utero three days after the caesarean was offered, and she died 13 days after diagnosis from a stroke and multi-organ failure in August 2009. Her family defended her decision as a selfless sacrifice, though her brother blamed the religion and medical staff questioned whether both deaths could have been prevented had she accepted the life-saving transfusion.

Mohammad Liaqat, 34, launched a racist tirade and assaulted staff at Mount Carmel Roman Catholic High School in Accrington after two 14-year-old Muslim pupils were initially banned from growing beards, a uniform policy that headteacher Xavier Bowers enforced but Mount Carmel school later reversed by allowing pupils to keep facial hair upon joining a mosque program. Despite the issue being resolved, Liaqat stormed the school and racially abused white teachers in front of parents and children, then moved to a nearby primary school where he assaulted that headteacher as well. He was found guilty of one count of assault and five counts of racially or religiously aggravated behavior, and was remanded in custody pending sentencing in June.

During a six-month nationwide anti-drug campaign called "ban drugs in hundreds of cities," Chinese police arrested more than 133,000 people, seized 43.3 tons of illegal narcotics, handled 115,000 drug-related crimes including robbery, and processed 606,000 drug use cases while forcefully regulating drug users, with drug trafficking groups suffering "a heavy blow" according to Assistant Minister Liu Yuejin—figures nearly double those from a year earlier. Despite these significant achievements, the campaign came at a cost with nine police officers killed and 657 wounded, including 76 severely, while Liu emphasized that China's drug problems remain severe, particularly with the rising threat of online drug trafficking and drug-related Internet crimes. The crackdown, which included the arrests of entertainment industry figures, has prompted Beijing authorities to implement stricter regulations banning drug use in the entertainment industry.

A new species of glass frog named Hyalinobatrachium dianae has been discovered in the mountains of eastern Costa Rica, distinguished by its translucent underside skin that allows its heart, liver, and gastrointestinal tract to be easily visible. The frog, which measures just one inch long, is identifiable from other glass frogs by its distinctive black and white forward-facing eyes and unique metallic whistle-like call that sounds more like an insect than a typical frog. Scientists were surprised to find this species given that a thorough survey of the region was conducted in the late 20th century—remarkable considering the first glass frog wasn't discovered until 1872—and it was named by discoverer Brian Kubicki after his mother to honor her support of his scientific career.

Prince William and Kate have moved back to Kensington Palace in London to prepare for the imminent birth of their second child, due Saturday, after spending the weekend at her family home in Berkshire. Kensington Palace revealed William has completed his air ambulance pilot training earlier than expected and will take approximately six weeks of paternity leave around the birth, mirroring his approach after son George's arrival in 2013, with his final training stage now scheduled to resume on June 1st. Prince Harry, currently on deployment in Australia, will return to the UK this Sunday to present medals at the London Marathon and potentially meet his new niece or nephew before returning to Australia on Monday.

Maurice Thibaux, a 67-year-old French neighbour of the Carriageworks in Sydney's inner city, stormed the catwalk during Sunday night's opening show of Sydney's Mercedes-Benz Fashion Week to protest the excessive noise, which he compared to a jumbo jet taking off. The disruption occurred as Australian fashion designer Kym Ellery was taking her bow, with Thibaux claiming the heavy bass rumbling was so intense his house shook, and other neighbours reported the noise disturbing their sleep. Despite the interruption, Ellery remained unfazed and continued her lap of honour as security escorted Thibaux from the stage.

FBI Chief James Comey has introduced compulsory annual fitness tests for all 135,000 FBI agents due to concerns about expanding waistlines and declining fitness levels, with those who fail facing marks on their performance reviews and potential reassignment from physically demanding roles. The shift toward desk-based cyber crime and intelligence gathering work following 9/11 has contributed to agents becoming less active, prompting standards requiring agents under 30 to run a mile in 12.5 minutes and perform 24 push-ups, with adjusted benchmarks for older agents. This initiative addresses America's broader obesity problem and reflects Comey's conviction that FBI agents must maintain fitness to "run, fight and shoot" while embodying the professional image the public expects of the agency.

Volcano Calbuco in southern Chile, one of the country's 90 active volcanoes, suddenly erupted for the first time in over 40 years at around 6pm local time, forcing approximately 1,500 residents from the nearby town of Ensenada to flee their homes. The eruption sent a massive plume of ash and gas high into the sky that was visible from up to 100 miles away, prompting authorities to establish a 12.5-mile exclusion zone around the volcano. Calbuco's last major eruption occurred in 1961, with a minor eruption following in 1972, though the volcano had not been under special observation before this unexpected event, reminding residents that volcanoes occur where tectonic plates meet and eruptions happen when magma forces its way through weak points in the Earth's crust.

David Cameron defended his Coalition government's austerity and welfare policies against criticism from Church of England bishops in an Easter message, insisting the government has spent five years "trying to lift people up" by making work pay and encouraging responsibility. The Prime Minister's intervention came after the Church provoked a row by publishing a guide for Britain's 30 million Christians on approaching the General Election, which was widely interpreted as indirect criticism of Coalition welfare policies, while senior Tory Michael Gove claimed Christians in Britain face condescension and dismissal. Meanwhile, UKIP argued that 16 and 17-year-olds should be excluded from any EU referendum, as Cameron visited Marston's Brewery in Wolverhampton to highlight his government's economic recovery efforts rooted in Christian values of hard work and responsibility.

Protests erupted in Baltimore after Freddie Gray died from a severe spinal cord injury allegedly suffered while in police custody on April 12, when officers spotted and chased him, arresting him after finding a knife in his pocket. Protesters rallied at Baltimore City Hall demanding justice, with Gray's family's attorney arguing police lacked probable cause to pursue him, while the Baltimore Police Department said Wednesday that all six officers involved have cooperated with investigators and been suspended with pay. Baltimore City Council President Jack Young acknowledged the broader mistrust between police and the community, noting the city has paid over $5.7 million since 2011 for more than 100 cases involving police wrongdoing allegations, as Baltimore Police officials say they plan to hand over investigation details to the State Attorney's Office next week.

Brandon Afoa, a 33-year-old airport worker at Seattle-Tacoma International Airport, was awarded $40 million in compensation after a freak runway accident more than seven years ago left him paralyzed from the waist down and unable to use his right arm. The incident occurred when the brakes and steering on the tug he was operating to push aircraft failed, causing it to crash into a luggage lift; Afoa subsequently sued the Port of Seattle for failing to maintain a safe workplace. After years of legal battles, a King County jury ruled that the Port of Seattle bore 25 percent of the blame for the accident, with airlines contracted through Afoa's former employer responsible for the remainder of the compensation.

Manchester United manager Louis van Gaal is preparing his team for a crucial match against Aston Villa on Saturday, with a potential victory putting United above Manchester City into second place in the Premier League. Van Gaal has scheduled a lunch appointment with Sir Alex Ferguson to discuss the club's trajectory, drawing confidence from recent victories over Tottenham and Liverpool that vindicate the faith of United legends in the team's renewed purpose. With places two through four increasingly interchangeable and Arsenal also in contention, United are well-positioned to benefit from the scramble for Champions League qualification, though they face stern tests ahead.

A 58-year-old passenger flying from Newcastle Airport to Gatwick on an EasyJet flight was stopped by airport security staff who mistakenly thought his six tubs of pease pudding were Semtex plastic explosive, despite the containers being clearly marked as such. Security told the traveller that the consistency of the traditional North East pudding could "technically" be classed as Semtex, and they also confiscated several Greggs pies alongside the pudding from his hand luggage before ultimately allowing him through with a warning to pack such items in hold luggage in future. The incident reflects the wide range of unusual items that airport security scanners routinely detect, with examples including confiscations at Stansted Airport and a giant squash seized at Birmingham Airport.

West Indies have named a 14-man squad for the first Test against England, with uncapped duo Shai Hope and paceman Carlos Brathwaite retained after the new coaching regime's initial training camp. Denesh Ramdin will continue to skipper the side facing England with Kraigg Brathwaite as deputy, while leg-spinner Devendra Bishoo earns a recall after playing his last Test three years ago. The squad also includes West Indies one-day captain Jason Holder for the opening match of the three-match series starting in Antigua on Monday.

A dog walker named Peter Hamilton used nail clippers to free a kidnapped woman who had been held captive and sexually assaulted by 43-year-old Rejean Hermel Perron in his Toronto home for five days. The 27-year-old victim emerged from the house bound in handcuffs and restrained with duct tape, and police claim Perron subjected her to multiple sexual assaults including ritualistic actions while holding her at gunpoint. Perron has been arrested and charged with sexual assault with a weapon, kidnapping, forcible confinement, and assault, with detectives investigating the possibility of additional unreported victims.

A family of three navigates Route 1 (the Pacific Coast Highway) from San Francisco—cling to its famous cable cars scaling the City's hills and visiting Alcatraz—to Los Angeles in a classic American road trip, spending three weeks exploring iconic destinations including the Golden Gate Bridge, the dramatic Big Sur coastline, and memorable stops like the quirky Madonna Inn and Monterey Peninsula. The journey culminates at Huntington Beach, also known as Surf City, where they stay at the Huntington Beach Hyatt Regency and enjoy three-and-a-half miles of white sand before heading home.

A furious row erupted today between the Lib Dems and Conservatives after Lib Dem minister Danny Alexander released details of what he claimed were secret plans drawn up by the last government to cut Child Benefit and Child Tax Credits, with the Lib Dems hitting back at claims that David Cameron himself had ordered officials to examine ways to curb access to benefits. Prime Minister Cameron flatly rejected the proposals, insisting they were civil servant ideas ordered by Alexander, but the Conservatives remain under pressure to explain how they will make £12 billion in savings from the welfare bill ahead of the election—a clash that threatens to damage both parties' poll standings. The furious exchange marks the worst row between the former coalition partners and raises serious doubts about whether they could work together again if the Tories fall short of a majority, with the Lib Dems vowing to block any welfare cuts as the price of future coalition negotiations.

Sergio Garcia insists he is focused on winning his first PGA Tour title since 2012 at this week's Shell Houston Open in Houston, despite the Masters at Augusta being just around the corner, Rory McIlroy's bid for a third consecutive major victory, and questions surrounding whether Tiger Woods will end his self-imposed exile from the game. The 35-year-old Spaniard, who finished second behind McIlroy at last year's Open at Hoylake, stated that he will concentrate fully on winning this week before shifting his attention to the Masters, refusing to strategize for next week's tournament while on the golf course. Garcia expressed that while winning a major would be nice, it is not the most important thing in his golfing career, and he is comfortable with the possibility of never winning one, even as top-10 players including Henrik Stenson and Jimmy Walker have withdrawn from the competition due to illness.

The Nepali Pranksters, a comedy group featuring Ashish Prasai known for their hidden camera series of pranks that challenge cultural norms—such as pranksters walking up to strangers for awkward handshakes—were filming an episode about Nepal's plastic bag ban in Jawalakhel when the earth began rumbling with a magnitude-7.8 earthquake on Saturday. The team kept their cameras rolling for 18 minutes as they documented the destruction, capturing scenes of collapsed buildings, survivors being pulled from rubble, and damage to historic sites like the UNESCO world heritage Basantapur Durbar Square. Despite making it safely through the first day, the pranksters expressed their fear about ongoing aftershocks and the country's deteriorating safety conditions.

Fashion designer Max Azria's sprawling 30,000 sq ft Los Angeles mansion in the city's 'Platinum Triangle', dubbed Maison du Solei, is now listed for $85 million and features luxurious amenities including a 6,000 sq ft movie theater, 22 bathrooms, 17 bedrooms, and a distinctive floor-to-ceiling chandelier with 150,000 crystals.
