The mansion's backyard boasts a zero-edged swimming pool with a bar, a Moroccan-style bathhouse, a private glass-enclosed tennis court, and five distinctly designed gardens, along with two greenhouses and an outdoor dining room. Originally built in 1939 by architect Paul Williams for Old Hollywood stars, Azria—founder of the popular BCBG Max Azria brand and chairman of the global fashion house BCBG Max Azria group—purchased the home with his wife Lubov for $14.4 million in 2005 and invested an additional $30 million in renovations.

# Summary

In Fuzhou, a city in Fujian province, southeast China, 50-year-old driver Mei Ru accidentally killed her 53-year-old husband Song Ming when she reversed her car over him while parked outside a supermarket. After the couple arrived at the shopping location, Mr. Ming climbed out of the passenger side and stood behind the vehicle, but his wife suddenly reversed without realizing he was there, striking and running over him. Mr. Ming was rushed to the hospital but died from massive internal bleeding and broken ribs caused by the impact.

NBC News has revised its account of the 2012 kidnapping of chief foreign correspondent Richard Engel in Syria, now stating he was taken captive by Sunni rebels rather than forces affiliated with President Bashar al-Assad, as previously reported. Engel and his team were convinced by their captors through an elaborate ruse that they were being held by Shi'ite government militiamen, when they were actually kidnapped by a criminal gang known for taking hostages for ransom and released for propaganda purposes. The New York Times prompted the reassessment by raising questions about the attackers' identities and suggesting the "rescue" that freed Engel may have been staged rather than a genuine firefight.

# Summary

Lidia Quilligana was arrested in Danbury, Connecticut after police reviewed a nanny cam video that allegedly showed her choking, beating, and burning a 3-year-old girl in her care on a hot stove. The child's parents set up the hidden camera after suspecting mistreatment, and the prosecutor described the footage as the worst video she had ever seen in her career. Quilligana faces first-degree assault charges against a child under 10 years old and is being held on $1 million bail.

During a Take Our Daughters and Sons to Work Day event at the White House, a little girl named Anya Brodie asked Michelle Obama her age, and when Obama replied she was 51 years old, Brodie offered the compliment nearly every aging woman wants to hear—that she looked too young for that age. Flattered by the remark, Michelle Obama asked Brodie to repeat it into the microphone and then invited her on stage for a warm hug. The event brought together children of White House employees and kids from the Boys and Girls Club and D.C. Child and Family Services.

# Summary

Philip Garrod, a 43-year-old paramedic, was caught by a speed camera doing 105mph on his powerful Suzuki motorbike in a 60mph zone on the A470 near Dolgellau, Wales. To avoid a driving ban at Dolgellau Magistrates Court, Garrod falsely claimed he was rushing to work after hearing about an injured cyclist, even fabricating a fake patient clinical record to support his lie. When ambulance bosses investigated and found no record of the emergency call, Garrod was struck off the medical register for dishonesty and misconduct.

Vin Los, a 24-year-old inked-up Canadian man with 24 face tattoos and extensive ink covering his neck, chest, and arms, has successfully launched a modeling career despite skepticism about his crude body art. Starting with his first tattoo of the Le Coq Sportif logo at age 16, Vin insisted that his tattoos embody pop culture and create "a myth, a mystery," landing him a campaign for underwear brand Garçon Model that showcases his tattooed-on chest hair and phrases like "scream my name" and "iconic face." The tattoo-covered model has transitioned from supermarket work to becoming a successful entrepreneur with aspirations of bringing paparazzi culture to his hometown of Montreal.

At 85 years old, Sir Stirling Moss—who won 212 races during his legendary 14-year racing career—appears to have lost none of his racing pedigree, sitting behind the wheel of the Austin-Healey Sprite he once drove as a training car before his career-ending crash in his Lotus at Goodwood on Easter Monday 1962, when he was unconscious for six weeks. Now reunited with the refurbished vehicle, Moss attributes his impressive longevity to a disciplined routine of 77 daily press-ups and a daily bottle of Chardonnay, and has notably swapped high-performance engines for an electric car with a 50mph top speed that suits his London-based lifestyle far better than a traditional petrol car would. The racing legend believes staying fit is a "way of life" and maintains that modern professional racing is too limiting compared to his era, when drivers competed in 52 races per year across multiple continents.

# Summary

Taylor Lynn Fast, 21, has been arrested in Festus, Missouri, after her 17-month-old daughter Layla was found beaten to death at their home with severe injuries to her face, neck, and body. Fast allegedly claimed that a spider bite was responsible for the child's fatal injuries, though police said the wounds were consistent with severe child abuse, comparable to injuries from a car crash. A three-year-old boy with severe bruising and a broken leg was also discovered at the home, and authorities are searching for Fast's boyfriend in connection with the case.

# Summary

Officer Jared Forsyth, 33, a member of the Ocala Police Department since 2012, was accidentally shot by a coworker during a firearms training exercise at a gun range at Lowell Correctional Institution on Monday afternoon. Despite wearing a bulletproof vest, a round entered through his arm and penetrated his chest. Forsyth was rushed to Ocala Regional Medical Center in critical condition but died from his injuries following surgery.

Police are investigating suspected arson attacks on three Melbourne Catholic churches linked to paedophile priests, including a blaze at St Mary's Catholic Church in Dandenong on April 1 and the destruction of heritage-listed St James Catholic Church on North Rd Brighton in Melbourne's south-east on March 30. The churches are connected to convicted abusers Father Kevin O'Donnell and Father Ronald Pickering, who abused boys over decades before his crimes were exposed in 2013. The investigation has drawn attention from survivors and former parishioners, including actress Rachel Griffiths, who expressed relief at the building fire that consumed St James Church, which carried traumatic memories for the community.

Shaquille Omar Hallisey, a 20-year-old thug, has been jailed for four years after admitting to wounding with intent to cause grievous bodily harm in Cardiff. Hallisey struck stranger Matthew Leeke, 38, over the head with a bottle in June last year, leaving him with a fractured skull requiring hospital treatment. The judge described the attack as "a savage demonstration of violence in a public place" and told Cardiff Crown that despite Hallisey's guilty plea and supportive family background, the victim has been profoundly affected by his injuries.

# Summary

According to a Virgin Media survey of 1,000 pet owners, the average pet spends approximately 21 hours per week watching TV, with owners admitting they leave the box on both while at home and when away to prevent their animals from getting lonely. The study reveals that pets show a strong preference for animal-focused programming such as "For the Love of Dogs" (17% of pets' favorite), "Natural World" (14%), and "Springwatch" (12%), while shunning reality shows like "Keeping Up With The Kardashians" and "TOWIE." Notably, almost two-thirds of goldfish owners position their tanks in sight of the TV so their fish can watch for over three and a half hours daily, and one micro pig owner allows their pet to watch up to 28 hours of television per week.

Four sisters—Claire, Emily, Christine, and Lily Vincenti—were at the center of an international custody battle that began in 2010 when their mother, Laura Garrett, took them from their Italian home to Australia and refused to return them, claiming their father Tomaso Vincenti was abusive. After two years, the girls were dramatically removed from their mother's Sunshine Coast home and returned to Italy in 2012, where they now appear settled and happy with their father, though they acknowledge they exaggerated their distress and were manipulated during the ordeal. Despite missing Australia and speaking to their mother every night, the eldest daughters—Claire and Emily—now express regret about their public displays of resistance and are leading positive lives in Italy, attending school and planning for university.

Former teacher Mary Kay Letourneau and her student-turned husband Vili Fualaau sat down with Barbara Walters for a 20/20 interview airing tonight on the eve of their 10-year wedding anniversary to discuss their rape trial and controversial relationship, which turned sexual when Fualaau was just 12 years old and Letourneau was his sixth-grade teacher. Letourneau recounted how their affair began during the summer of 1996, leading her to give birth to two daughters—one while on bail and another while serving a seven-and-a-half-year prison sentence. During the interview, Fualaau revealed the emotional toll of being separated from Letourneau while incarcerated, expressing that contact with her would have helped him navigate the struggles of fatherhood at such a young age.

Carl Froch's promoter discusses potential comeback opponents for the champion following Julio Cesar Chavez Jr's unexpected loss to Andrzej Fonfara, which eliminated hopes of Chavez fighting Froch and leaves James DeGale as the most likely next opponent if DeGale wins his IBF world title fight on May 23, with Gennady Golovkin also under consideration. Meanwhile, negotiations to stage a Carl Frampton versus Scott Quigg fight have collapsed after Frampton's team failed to respond to three offers, including a £1.5 million guarantee, prompting Quigg to move forward with an alternative opponent on July 18. Additionally, Anthony Joshua has been added to a May 9 Birmingham show to fight Rafael Zumbano Love before his scheduled bout against Kevin Johnson on May 30.

Nine-year-old Annabel plummeted 30 feet headfirst down a hollow cottonwood tree in Texas but miraculously survived the horrifying accident virtually unscathed, with firefighters harnessing her to safety after she claimed to have sat in Jesus's lap while unconscious. Her mother Christy Wilson Beam believes the experience is genuine, and most remarkably, Annabel was mysteriously cured of a rare incurable condition that had previously restricted her to liquid and soft foods, allowing her to discontinue most medications and eat solid foods for the first time in her life.

Bradford Bulls have launched an investigation after three cars were broken into and damaged in the Richard Dunn Sports Centre car park, situated opposite the Provident Stadium at Odsal, during Sunday's Ladbrokes Challenge Cup fifth-round defeat by Hull KR. The Championship club is working with Bradford Council, the sports centre, and West Yorkshire Police to address the incident and similar recent vandalism cases in local car parks throughout the area. Stadium manager Mark Leadbeater has pledged to implement enhanced security measures to ensure the car parks remain safe for supporters.

A fight broke out on Oman Air flight 102 from London Heathrow to Muscat after one passenger moved seats on the partially full plane, prompting objections from another passenger that escalated into a physical altercation where one man allegedly attacked the other with a shoe. During the brawl, the assailant threw punches while flight crew intervened to contain the situation, and upon landing in Muscat, one man was taken away for questioning by airport security. The incident reflects a broader pattern of mid-air confrontations over passenger space, including a Delta flight that was diverted last year when a row broke out over a reclined seat, underscoring how aircraft legroom disputes continue to pose challenges to airline safety and passenger comfort.

An unarmed Iranian observation aircraft made two passes within 50 yards of a U.S. Navy MH-60R armed helicopter patrolling the Persian Gulf, an incident that troubled U.S. military officials and sparked concerns about command and control of Iranian forces. The encounter, which occurred during nuclear negotiations between the U.S. and Iran, was unusual given that Iranian forces have generally conducted operations professionally in recent months—leading U.S. officials to suspect it may have been ordered by a local commander who was subsequently reprimanded, evidenced by the aircraft's disappearance from its typical monthly pattern over the Gulf. While no shots were fired and the helicopter safely broke away, U.S. military officials worry that such unsafe maneuvers could escalate into a serious military incident, particularly as the Navy continues monitoring the region for Iranian weapons shipments.

British pet owners surveyed 2,000 responses to design an ideal dog by blending features from several breeds—including an English bulldog's coat, King Charles spaniel's ears, Irish setter's tail, border collie's body, labrador's soft snout, and beagle's eyes—creating what looked like an ordinary pet but embodied the perfect dog depending on regional preferences. Londoners preferred small lap dogs (41 percent), while residents of East Anglia chose large Great Danes (19 percent), reflecting how the ideal dog would vary by location and available space. Additional findings showed over a third of owners wanted a short, low-maintenance coat, 50 percent favored a labrador's soft, round snout, and 30 percent preferred a labrador's mid-range bark over a high-pitched yap.

Two soldiers—Scots Guard Shaun Smith, 25, and Welsh Guard Jason Collins, 22, a Buckingham Palace guard—walked free from court despite inflicting a shocking drunken beating on a helpless civilian, with CCTV footage showing Smith stamping and kicking 18 times on the victim's head while Collins rained blows on his body. Judge James Hill made what he called an "extraordinary" decision to show mercy, sentencing Smith to a 12-month suspended jail sentence and 80 hours of unpaid work, while Collins received only a 12-month community order, despite the maximum penalty for their charges being up to 10 years imprisonment. The furious victim, James Wormald, condemned the lenient sentences, stating there was no excuse for the violent attack and that the force used could have killed him.

West Ham manager Sam Allardyce has defended Leicester City boss Nigel Pearson ahead of their Premier League match at the King Power Stadium on Saturday, praising him as "his own man" despite controversial incidents this season, including an altercation with Crystal Palace midfielder James McArthur. Although Leicester sit bottom of the league with speculation surrounding Pearson's future, Allardyce believes his former coaching staff colleague has done an "unbelievable job" and feels the team's performances are letting them down—they haven't converted chances when it mattered most. West Ham will look to repeat their December success when they beat Leicester 2-0 with just two shots on target, while Pearson seeks to end a five-hour-plus goal-drought at home.

Eighteen-month-old twins drowned in an Arizona canal after their mother, Alexis Keslar, attempted to fend off a bee and released the stroller, which rolled into the water on Friday. Though Keslar went into the canal and tried to rescue her sons, she was hindered by the steep canal embankments, deep water, and swift current that washed the stroller away. Emergency responders searched after the irrigation district slowed the water flow and reduced the water level, but the toddlers were pronounced dead at the hospital; authorities warn that paths along Yuma canals, popular with joggers and bicyclists, pose serious dangers due to swift currents and difficult embankments.

Terry McCarty, who suffered third-degree burns to 70 percent of his body at age six when his brothers accidentally knocked a bowl of kerosene set alight onto him, overcame his traumatic childhood accident and the cruel bullying that followed to become a firefighter. After enduring 58 operations and years of emotional struggle, McCarty confronted his deepest fears during his firefighting training in 2012, realizing that fire no longer controlled him. He now dedicates himself to supporting other burn survivors through the Burned Children Recovery Foundation and aims to establish his own foundation to provide firefighting experiences and funding for children with burns.

A fundraising campaign by the armed forces charity Help for Heroes has raised £10,000 in just four days to help Second World War veteran Robert Clark, among the country's oldest surviving prisoners of war, remain in his own home in North London with a live-in carer after Brent Council refused to fund his ongoing care. The 97-year-old, who survived the notorious death march from Campo PG70 and has lived in his Burnt Oak home for over 50 years, faced being moved to a local care home despite having already spent £50,000 of his life savings on care costs. The donations will cover approximately four to five months of the £960-weekly care needed, with Help for Heroes now targeting £15,000 to provide Mr. Clark with continued independence and familiarity in his own home.

Conservative party offices in Aberdeen, Scotland were sprayed with white paint on their blue front door and windows with a swastika, the word "scum," and a "Q" (allegedly meaning quisling), which Scottish Conservative Councillor Ross Thomson posted images of on Twitter and attributed to nationalist vandals. Labour offices nearby were also reportedly targeted with a white "Q" on their door, though it's unclear if the incidents were connected. The vandalism comes six months after similar defacement of buildings and polling stations during the Scottish Referendum campaign.

Lucas Matthysse defeated Ruslan Provodnikov in a majority decision during a 12-round super lightweight bout, dominating the first round and opening a cut near Provodnikov's left eye early in the second, while using his three-inch reach advantage to evade the Russian's powerful left hook and counter Provodnikov's hard right hook-left hook combinations from late in the third round. Though the 24-3 Provodnikov put Matthysse on the ropes and landed several punches throughout, he could only stagger him in the 11th, falling short as judges scored the fight 115-113 for Matthysse (37-3). After securing the win, Matthysse dismissed overtures to see Crawford defend his WBO title against him, instead setting his sights on Floyd Mayweather or Manny Pacquiao.

Ferrari team principal Maurizio Arrivabene employs a carrot-and-stick approach with Kimi Raikkonen to maintain his motivation, as speculation grows that Lewis Hamilton could replace the Finn at Maranello. After urging Raikkonen to raise his game last week relative to team-mate Sebastian Vettel, the charismatic Arrivabene saw the Finn deliver at Sunday's Bahrain Grand Prix, securing a podium finish ahead of Mercedes' Nico Rosberg and splitting the Mercedes duo to finish second behind Hamilton. While Arrivabene praised Raikkonen's demonstration of talent and suggested he deserves to renew his contract option, he emphasized the need to keep the driver mentally sharp, acknowledging that Ferrari still have considerable work ahead to consistently compete with Mercedes.

A study by Expert Market analyzing data from the International Labour Organisation revealed that only three countries in the entire world—Jamaica, Colombia, and Saint Lucia—have more female bosses than male bosses, with the Caribbean island leading at nearly 60 percent of managers being female. Out of 108 countries surveyed, Jamaica was closely followed by the Philippines at 47.6 percent, with Latin American nations like Panama and Eastern European countries dominating the top positions, while the first-world country of Britain ranked poorly at 41st place with only 34.2 percent female managers, and Pakistan ranked worst globally at just 3 percent. Despite the United States reaching the top 20 countries at 15th place and nearly a third of all businesses worldwide now being owned or managed by women, the number of female CEOs in large companies remains disappointingly low.

Australian teenager Violeta Tupuola, who already has naturally beautiful voluptuous lips, joined the #kyliejennerchallenge sweeping social media by placing a shot glass over her mouth and sucking in to create a vacuum that swells the lips dramatically. In her video, after applying lip gloss and removing the glass in an attempt to emulate Kylie Jenner's famous bee-stung pout, Violeta's lips became so protruding that social media users compared her to a duck, exemplifying the often undesirable and painful results of this puffy pout craze. The dangerous trend has spread among countless teens who share their disturbing lip-swelling experiments online, frequently resulting in severe bruising and effects lasting for hours or even days.

Rangers suffered their first defeat under manager Stuart McCall on Thursday night when the Ibrox side crashed 3-0 to Queen of the South at Palmerston in their second Championship loss of the season, prompting McCall to urge his team toward an immediate response. Despite the heavy defeat and goals from Derek Lyle, Gavin Reilly, and a Lee Wallace own goal, McCall remained defiant and insisted Rangers would bounce back with a win against Raith Rovers on Sunday to maintain their promotion push and play-off momentum. Rangers remain third in the table behind Hibernian on goal difference, while Queen of the South's victory boosted their play-off hopes by leapfrogging Falkirk into fourth place.

A police horse named Charly was vandalized with silver spray paint spelling "RBS" and an arrow while patrolling Venice Beach with his handlers on Tuesday in broad daylight, with a witness posting images of the incident to Instagram. The LAPD is searching for the person responsible, hoping to identify the tagger through the cryptic tag "RBS," while locals condemned the cruel and disrespectful act. The paint was cleaned off relatively easily, but authorities are asking the public for help in locating the vandal.

Queens Park Rangers twice threw away a lead in a 3-3 draw against Aston Villa at Villa Park, with Christian Benteke scoring a hat-trick to earn the hosts a point. Watching the game on his iPhone from Malaysia, Rangers chairman Tony Fernandes tweeted about his despair and emotional rollercoaster, while praising manager Chris Ramsey and defender Clint Hill's fighting spirit. Despite the disappointing result, Fernandes cryptically hinted at a decision regarding his future at the club as QPR remained in the relegation zone.

Harry Kane tops this week's EA Sports Performance Index following Tottenham's 3-1 victory over Newcastle United on Sunday, where he netted Spurs' third goal for his 30th of the season and earned a Game Index score of 55.1 as the Premier League's best player. The 21-year-old PFA Young Player of the Year nominee is followed by his Tottenham teammate Christian Eriksen in second place and Chelsea star Eden Hazard in third, with the EA Sports index measuring player contributions through statistics including goals, assists, appearances, and clean sheets. The rankings are heavily populated by Tottenham players following another intriguing round of Premier League action, with Nacer Chadli fourth and Southampton's Morgan Schneiderlin seventh despite his team losing to Stoke.

A protective father created a humorous t-shirt that warns "Stay Clear Boys - This is my dad!" featuring his muscular torso to keep boys away from his young daughter. The image, first shared on Reddit before spreading to Facebook via Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu champion Kit Dale, went viral and received 370,000 likes and over 45,000 shares within days. While some commenters praised the creative parenting approach, others criticized it as a self-promotional stunt and questioned whether it respects the daughter's autonomy.

An unnamed New York woman was found hanging from the third-floor fire escape of her apartment building on Utica Ave. near Prospect Place in Crown Heights on Sunday morning around 8:20 a.m., with her body left dangling for more than an hour—a discovery that traumatized a resident who awoke to find her hanging outside his window. Witnesses reported being deeply shaken by the incident, with one passerby initially mistaking the body for a Halloween prank. According to neighbors, the woman had been struggling with mental health issues, and her identity has not been released pending family notification.

Full back David Raven stabbed home the winning goal to lead Inverness Caledonian Thistle past Celtic to the Scottish Cup final, but a club like Celtic's subsequent complaint letter to the SFA about a missed penalty has overshadowed his achievement and revealed deeper fractures in Scottish football. The article excoriates Celtic's arrogance and poor sportsmanship—evidenced by empty home games across the league—while also exposing broader systemic failures including declining attendance, deteriorating match quality, and financial mismanagement that echoes Rangers' collapse. The author contends that Scottish football requires sweeping structural overhaul, including expanding the top division, redistributing revenue equitably, and establishing stricter standards to restore competitive balance and revitalize the sport.

Real Madrid's last victory against Atletico Madrid came in last season's Champions League final in Lisbon, but since that triumph, Carlo Ancelotti's side have failed to win any of their last seven games against their city rivals, suffering four defeats and three draws while conceding 12 goals to just 4. Atletico Madrid have dominated the fixture over the past eight months with their third win arriving in January's Copa del Rey last 16 via a 2-0 victory courtesy of goals from Raul Garcia and Jose Maria Gimenez, followed by a 4-2 aggregate triumph and a devastating 4-0 rout in February featuring Fernando Torres's goals. With the two teams drawing 0-0 in last week's Champions League quarter-final first leg, Wednesday night's second leg will determine who advances, with Atletico clearly holding the upper hand in recent encounters.

Ile Louët, an impressive 19th-century lighthouse island in Morlaix Bay, Brittany, offers groups of up to 10 guests the ultimate secluded retreat in a historic lighthouse keeper's cottage for just €200 (£145) for two nights. The quaint cottage provides spectacular views of the bay from its patio, though guests must sacrifice modern comforts like hot running water and navigate a separate outdoor bathroom. This perfect peaceful retreat is ideal for adventurers seeking isolation, requiring a €100 (£70) boat transfer from a local sailor.

Boobesh Palani, a 26-year-old Indian student, was rescued from Wellington Harbour by two teenage girls, Kelly McKay and Payge Olds, after he was found drowning in the water on Monday night. Despite their brave rescue efforts and CPR performed by Kelly McKay, Mr. Palani passed away at Wellington Hospital with his friends and family by his side after police established his identity through a public appeal using photos from his hospital bed. The heroic actions of the two teenagers, who risked their lives in shark-infested waters to save the man's life, were praised by New Zealand Police.

Researchers at the University of Bath and Yale University have developed a new molecular catalyst that significantly improves water electrolysis—splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen—while dramatically reducing the energy losses that typically make producing hydrogen so costly and difficult. This breakthrough enables cleaner hydrogen production from renewable sources like solar power, wind, and tidal energy, allowing the storage of renewable energy as storable hydrogen fuel for later use without reliance on fossil fuels. The technology could revolutionize hydrogen fuel cells for cars and other applications by making clean hydrogen production more economical and practical.

Ed Miliband returned to the campaign trail in Bristol on Tuesday after taking the Easter weekend off to spend time with his family in Doncaster, his first public appearance in over 72 hours. While the Labour leader spent the weekend preparing for upcoming TV debates, his political rivals—including Prime Minister David Cameron, Nick Clegg, Nigel Farage, and Nicola Sturgeon—remained active on the campaign trail throughout Easter Sunday and Monday, with Cameron undertaking a high-profile tour across all four nations of the UK. With just one month until polling day, Labour strategists have calculated that the amount of time Mr Miliband devotes to debate preparation is better spent than constant campaigning, though some Labour MPs have expressed concerns about his participation in additional debates without the Prime Minister and Deputy Prime Minister present.

Yemeni officials reported that Saudi airstrikes targeting the Al Hamza military base on Tuesday hit the nearby Al Bastain School in southwestern Yemen, killing at least three students and injuring others, though the school was not the main target. The conflict intensified after Houthi rebels, who are allied with Iran, seized control of Yemen's capital Sanaa, forcing President Hadi to flee Aden to Saudi Arabia in late March, which prompted Saudi Arabia and other Arab nations to begin bombing Yemen with airstrikes. As the violence escalated with hundreds killed in less than two weeks, civilians and foreigners evacuated from port cities like Aden, with an Air India plane coordinating with Saudi forces to safely reach the airport and transport evacuees to safety in Djibouti.

A Colorado mother, Julieane Jablonski, 38, was arrested on suspicion of providing marijuana to a minor and felony witness tampering after allegedly giving her 19-year-old son a pot brownie that sent him jumping out of a third-story window without hesitation in April. Detectives allege that Jablonski attempted to convince her hospitalized son to recant his police statement about where the marijuana came from, while the alleged victim later commented on the Fort Collins police department's Facebook page to describe the strange trip he experienced. The incident reflects a troubling pattern in Colorado where pot users have harmed themselves since recreational marijuana sales began, with critics blaming dangerously high THC levels in edibles.

Divock Origi, who joined Liverpool for £10 million after impressing at the 2014 World Cup and was loaned back to Lille for the 2014-15 season, has expressed his excitement about joining the club after following Liverpool's progress and the development of young players like Raheem Sterling and winger Jordon Ibe. Origi told Liverpool's official website that he believes manager Brendan Rodgers' proven ability to develop Liverpool's youngsters makes it the right club for him, and he is eager to work under his guidance. The Belgian striker added that his challenging season at Lille has made him mentally stronger and a better player, preparing him well for the opportunities that await him at Liverpool.

ESPN sports reporter Britt McHenry, who publicly encourages women to be "the woman with a voice" and lectured against appearance-based commercials like a cheeseburger advert, was caught on camera verbally attacking towing company clerk Gina Michelle, telling her to "lose some weight" and insulting her education and job. Despite being warned she was being filmed, McHenry continued her tirade, prompting a spokesman to confirm her suspension for one week and sparking a petition demanding her termination for "derogatory, classist and body-shaming" comments. Weight discrimination groups and social media users have condemned McHenry's behavior as hypocritical, with critics arguing that retaining McHenry as a prominent public figure sends a message condoning cruelty toward lower-income Americans and threatens the integrity of sports journalism.

Adult colouring books have become a global trend, with Scottish illustrator Johanna Basford's bestselling *Secret Garden*—originally designed as a child's book concept but adapted for grown-ups—selling over 1.4 million copies and spawning colouring-in circles where adults gather like book clubs to colour together. The phenomenon, which started in France, appeals to participants as a therapeutic, screen-free creative activity that offers relief from modern life's stresses, with colouring-in books now outselling cookbooks in some countries, and Basford's follow-up, *Enchanted Forest*, poised to match that success. The practice, which studies show reduces anxiety, represents a broader resistance to constant digital engagement, with people of all ages discovering that picking up crayons to stay within the lines provides genuine peace and creative satisfaction.

The NHS handed out 404,500 prescriptions for suncream costing £13 million and 4.7 million prescriptions for indigestion pills costing £29 million in 2014, prompting criticism that the health service is wasting money on items cheaply available in chemists while denying patients 19 life-extending cancer treatments and rationing routine procedures. Critics argue that patients are abusing the system by arriving at surgeries with "shopping lists" demanding prescriptions for household essentials like toothpaste and Vaseline, with one GP reporting patients come into her surgery every week wanting prescriptions for head lice shampoo. Patient Concern called the practice "ludicrous," stating that while the NHS faces an £8 billion budget shortfall by 2020/21, patients are denied necessary treatments while others receive free prescriptions for over-the-counter items.

Mark Vanhoenacker, a British Airways pilot who admires the Boeing 747 like a sculptor might a perfect human torso, has written "Skyfaring," a book that shares his passion for flying and the secrets of cockpit life, becoming particularly timely following the Germanwings tragedy when co-pilot Andreas Lubitz deliberately crashed an Airbus 320 into the French Alps. Vanhoenacker describes the intricacies of long-haul flight operations—from the practical comfort of foot heaters (unlike Airbus aircraft where pilots wear heavy socks) to the quirky waypoint names pilots navigate—offering readers an insider's reassurance about the dedication of experienced aviators. The book celebrates the enduring magic of flight and the mystique that has captivated Vanhoenacker since his childhood visits to the cockpit, while avoiding dwelling on plane crashes to focus instead on why we should trust the men at the controls.

Crystal Palace manager Alan Pardew believes Manchester City can still win the Barclays Premier League title despite Chelsea currently leading by six points, citing the team's history of dramatic comebacks including their final-game victory over Manchester United on goal difference and their overtaking of Liverpool last season before Sergio Aguero's injury-time winner against QPR in 2012 sealed City's first Premier League title. Pardew argues that the Premier League title race is never truly over and victories cannot be taken for granted, emphasizing how City has previously snatched the championship in the final moments. Ahead of Palace's Monday match against City, Pardew will be without several injured players and suspended captain Mile Jedinak.

Manchester United midfielder Michael Carrick has revealed he wants to take up a career in motorsport, particularly as an F1 driver, once his playing days end, though he remains focused on extending his time at the club by maintaining strict fitness and lifestyle discipline at 34. The former West Ham star is currently enjoying a renaissance under Louis van Gaal and has reflected on moving to London at age 15 from his family home in the north east, describing it as a transformative experience that accelerated his maturation as a young player. Fans can watch Carrick discuss his journey in "Life Before United" on MUTV.

Wood Green animal welfare charity has partnered with cleaning app Handy to offer a cat fostering service in London, where homeowners can temporarily borrow from over 200 cats in need of homes to naturally and humanely scare away rodents—the cats' presence and scent alone deter mice without requiring actual mouse catching or leaving dead mice in the home. The article also highlights Amazon's Goat Grazer Beta service (available in the US), which allows homeowners to rent goats to control lawn overgrowth and invasive vegetation, offering a practical alternative to traditional mowing. Both services represent creative solutions to common household problems, particularly rodent infestations that worsen during winter months.

At the Coachella music and arts festival in the Californian desert, this year festival-goers wore ever more outlandish looks, with celebrities leading the fashion faux pas: Jaden Smith in a floral-print tunic worn over a wrap skirt, pal Kylie Jenner in nude hotpants and later a black body stocking, Paris Hilton sporting bizarre rose-petal cat ears, Singer Katy Perry going for a demure layered rose dress, and Khloe Kardashian showcasing her commitment to fringing in a white skin-tight fringed ensemble. Beyond the famous attendees, revelers embraced extreme looks featuring neon accessories, exposed flesh, tiny gold lamé hot pants, bizarre headgear, and clashing prints—from a man in a full mummy bodysuit to another Coachella sporting bright floral headgear and a child's Wellington boot as a necklace. The festival became a showcase of fashion excess, with attendees competing to wear the most eye-catching and uncoordinated outfits possible in the scorching heat.

The trophy room at Sandringham, the Queen's Norfolk estate, has undergone a major overhaul, with all 62 hunting memorabilia items—including stuffed rhino heads, an Indian tiger, leopard skins, and gleaming white tusks—removed from public display and placed in storage, while the blood-red walls have been repainted white. The Sandringham museum, which opened in 1928 to house George V's collection of "big game trophies," now features a pointed reference acknowledging that "this destructive era is drawing to a close," a change prompted by legal concerns about unworked ivory and animal parts dating before 1947, as well as pressure on animal welfare crusader Prince William, who had recently called for the destruction of the Royal Family's ivory collection. The replaced items now consist of various trinkets and historical artifacts, with palace officials stating the changes were made to refresh displays and make room for an exhibition of royal family paintings.

Prosecco sales in Britain have surged dramatically, overtaking champagne for the first time in 2014 with £181.8 million in sales compared to champagne's £141.3 million, driven by word-of-mouth popularity during celebratory occasions and the once humble sparkling wine's newfound affordability. Unlike champagne, which is made from classic grapes in the French Champagne region and aged at least 18 months—or longer for vintage bottles—prosecco is produced from Glera grapes in northeastern Italy using tank fermentation that keeps production costs down and allows wine to be made in months rather than years, compared to champagne's nine million bottles sold annually in Britain. With expanded production areas established in 2009 and quality controls in place, prosecco ranges from affordable DOC quality wines (£6-£10) to premium DOCG options (£7-£15), offering consumers a fresh, light, and simple alternative to more complex sparkling wines.

Former Adelaide doctor Tareq Kamleh, who fled Australia to join ISIS—a death cult notorious for beheading non-Muslims—remains registered to practice medicine in the country despite appearing in a recent propaganda video calling for foreign medical professionals to join the terrorist organization in Syria, a decision the Medical Board has refused to reverse. Dr. Kamleh, who previously worked at the Royal Adelaide Hospital, featured in the ISIS recruitment video wearing surgical scrubs and handling babies in a maternity ward as he urged medics to help launch an Islamic State Health Service. If he returns to Australia, he could face charges carrying a sentence of up to 25 years in prison.

Two young siblings, Piper Kulk (8) and her brother William (9), were killed in a fatal crash on rain-soaked roads on the NSW Central Coast when their grandmother's car aqua-planed and collided with a ute; Piper died on Sunday morning while William died in hospital that evening, with the devoted siblings dying within hours of each other. The brothers, who attended Budgewoi Public School and played rugby league with the Budgewoi-Buff Point Bulldogs, were traveling home from the Royal Easter Show with their older brother Daniel (12) and mother Chantelle (31)—both now hospitalized—when the accident occurred, while passengers in the ute also suffered severe injuries. The crash follows several other fatal incidents during the Easter weekend, prompting authorities to urge drivers to reduce speed and adjust to wet weather conditions.

Dino Maglio, an Italian former policeman, has been found guilty of raping a 16-year-old Australian girl he lured to his home via Couchsurfing and sentenced to six and a half years in prison after she reported being drugged and raped. Prosecutors proved Maglio spiked her drink with a tranquilliser pill, leaving her unable to resist, despite his claim the sex was consensual. An investigation by the Investigative Reporting Project Italy uncovered testimonies from 14 other women who alleged they were similarly assaulted using the same drugging method.

Several UK politicians participated in a Cosmopolitan magazine quiz about sexting, with Ed Miliband declaring it "not advised for politicians," Nick Clegg calling it "risky" for young people and "dodgy" for older individuals, Education Secretary Nicky Morgan cautioning it's "too risky," and Green Party leader Natalie Bennett saying it's acceptable for other adults but not herself. Their warnings reflect cautionary tales from fellow politicians, including Tory MP Brooks Newmark who resigned after a humiliating sexting scandal involving explicit photographs, and top Tory official Iain Corby whose Mail on Sunday-revealed exchanges on gay dating service Grindr exposed misconduct. The article also references American politician Anthony Weiner's two sexting scandals that damaged his political career.

Ian Walters, a 51-year-old church treasurer and driving test examiner from Swindon, is accused of deliberately crashing his 4x4 vehicle into a tree at 84mph on the M1 motorway, killing his wife Tracy, 48, whom he married in Cyprus two years prior. The prosecution claims Walters purposely crashed to assert control over Tracy after their marriage deteriorated due to conflicting sexual demands and alleged abuse, but he denies the murder charge, claiming he may have fallen asleep at the wheel due to antidepressant medication he was taking at the time of the March crash.

Manchester Airport temporarily suspended flights for approximately 20 minutes after a drone was reported buzzing around the area, raising fears that an unmanned aerial vehicle could collide with a commercial airliner, causing some departing flights to be delayed and incoming flights diverted to alternative airports. A police helicopter investigation found no evidence of the UAV operating in the airspace, and the airport reopened its second runway to clear the backlog and resume normal operations. The incident underscores growing concerns about drones flying near airports, as experts warn these unmanned aerial devices pose a significant threat to aircraft safety and could be sucked into jet engines.

China's cybercensors have developed a new cyberweapon called the "Great Cannon" that attacks servers outside China's borders, including in the United States, using distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks that harness unsuspecting web surfers' browsers to blast targeted sites—a risk Beijing may have deliberately accepted. Researchers from the University of Toronto's Citizen Lab and partner institutions found the Great Cannon wrecked two online services in March—GreatFire.org and GitHub—which both suspected China was behind the attacks because they hosted tools allowing Chinese citizens to bypass the Great Firewall and access censored Western news content like The New York Times. Unlike the Great Firewall, which monitors all traffic entering and leaving China, the Great Cannon operates more selectively by injecting malicious code into users visiting Chinese servers like Baidu to enlist them as unwitting participants in the attacks, a capability experts warn could easily be adapted for launching attacks far beyond what's already been observed.

Ciudad Real airport in Spain, built in 2009 at a cost of over €1 billion during the country's economic boom to absorb overflow from Madrid's Barajas airport via a high-speed train link, closed in 2012 after budget airlines ceased operations and is now being sold for just one pound, though new owners must pay off hundreds of millions in debt. The 28,000 sq ft terminal, where 5 million passengers were projected to pass through annually, now sits eerily empty alongside its 4,000-meter runway—continually painted with yellow crosses to signal pilots that landing is prohibited—making it one of Spain's most notable ghost airports and a cautionary tale of infrastructure investment that spectacularly failed to materialize.

NYPD Detective Patrick Cherry, caught on video screaming at Uber driver Humayun after being honked at for failing to signal while parking his unmarked Hyundai in the West Village, publicly apologized on NBC 4 New York and was stripped of his shield and gun and placed on administrative duty following the three-minute viral clip. Police Chief Bill Bratton condemned the officer's discourteous and obscene behavior—which included mocking the driver's accent, pounding on the car, and throwing items inside—stating that such anger is unacceptable and undermines all officers' work. Cherry's case was transferred to the Civilian Complaint Review Board on a discourtesy complaint after the Internal Affairs Bureau investigated, and he was reassigned from the Joint Terrorism Task Force, though the Detectives' Endowment Association attributed his outburst to stress from a colleague's recent heart attack.

Former Manchester United goalkeeper Edwin van der Sar is convinced that manager Louis van Gaal will bring glory days back to Old Trafford, as United heads to Chelsea on Saturday in top form after winning six successive matches. While Van der Sar admits the Premier League title is probably beyond United's reach this season—with the Blues winning many games with late goals—he believes the club can challenge for the championship next year with proper reinforcements. Van Gaal revealed this week he has already begun planning summer transfers to strengthen the squad, with the club aiming to secure third place and make strategic signings to accelerate the revival back to where it belongs.

Bournemouth trailed after Kieran Lee's 36th-minute header but equalized through Yann Kermorgant before Matt Ritchie's 85th-minute free kick put them ahead, only for Chris Maguire's stoppage-time penalty to give the Owls a 2-2 draw at Dean Court. The result left Bournemouth manager Eddie Howe frustrated, compounded when Simon Francis was sent off in the 78th minute and Howe felt Callum Wilson deserved a penalty in the second half, suggesting referees were deliberately withholding penalties after Bournemouth had received 15 this season.

18-year-old Billy-Anne Huxham from Caboolture, Queensland was kidnapped at knifepoint by her ex-boyfriend Carl Garry Chapman early Thursday morning and was later found by police on Queensland's Sunshine Coast after she escaped and contacted her family. Chapman, 32, who was out on bail, used a firearm and machete during the abduction and has been charged with multiple serious offences including torture, assault, and deprivation of liberty. The silver Mitsubishi SUV used in the abduction was discovered abandoned in Kallangar, and Chapman is scheduled to appear in Maroochydore Magistrates Court.

Southampton manager Ronald Koeman has delivered a clear message to his players: forget about others and start scoring goals to revive their European ambitions. The Saints have managed just seven goals in their last 10 league matches, leaving them seventh in the Premier League with 53 points, and Koeman believes they must win crucial home fixtures like Saturday's match against Hull City to qualify for European football. With striker Graziano Pelle's goal-scoring struggles potentially seeing him benched, Koeman could recall Sadio Mane to strengthen the attacking lineup.

Teller Lake in Boulder, Colorado has been invaded by an estimated 3,000 to 4,000 goldfish that originated from someone dumping four or five pet goldfish into the water two to three years ago. The non-native species now threatens native fish populations by competing for resources and spreading diseases, prompting Colorado Parks and Wildlife officials to consider the same electrofishing method that successfully removed koi goldfish from a nearby Boulder lake in 2012. Wildlife officials are warning the public against dumping pets into local waters, as it can have devastating environmental consequences.

Everton could yet finish in the bottom half of the Premier League for the first time since 2006, what might be their worst season in nine years under Roberto Martinez, sitting 25 points behind their position after 32 games last season when they targeted a Champions League place. Despite playing 10 Europa League games before being knocked out by Dynamo Kiev, goalkeeper Tim Howard insists European football is not to blame, arguing that no-one blames Champions League football despite similar demands on those teams. Everton drew 1-1 with Swansea City on Saturday, with Aaron Lennon's goal canceled out by a Jonjo Shelvey penalty, leaving the club focused on finishing in the top half.

On Saturday night, the Atlanta Hawks crushed the Brooklyn Nets 131-99 to match their franchise record with a 57th win, with Al Horford and DeMarre Carroll each scoring 20 points and Jeff Teague adding 17. However, the Hawks lost forward Paul Millsap to a right shoulder injury in a collision with Brooklyn's Earl Clark during the first half, with the team providing no immediate details on the injury. In other Saturday action, the Boston Celtics defeated the Toronto Raptors 117-116 in overtime to move into playoff contention ahead of the Heat following Miami's loss to the Detroit Pistons 99-98.

Mobile phone companies in the UK boasted that new 4G mobile phone services would be 'five times faster' than existing 3G networks when launched in 2012, but an official Ofcom study reveals the actual speed increase is only 2.5 times faster, averaging 14.7 megabits per second compared to 5.9Mbit/s on 3G. EE's 4G network led the major mobile networks with the fastest average download speed of 18.6Mbit/s, while Three network was the slowest at 8.5Mbit/s. The technology is already slowing as more people take 4G services, with speeds declining from around 19Mbit/s at launch to the current average of 14.7Mbit/s.

Jimmy Anderson was honored with a silver cap after reaching his 100th Test for England, finding himself in good company with cricket's elite fast bowling centurions. The match in Antigua opened with a minute's silence and black armbands worn by both teams to honor the late Richie Benaud, while new safety helmets designed following Phillip Hughes' death were on display. West Indies' Chris Gayle opted to play in the IPL for Royal Challengers Bangalore rather than represent his country, underscoring the tension between lucrative Twenty20 opportunities and Test cricket commitments.

Ellia Beasley, an aspiring 22-year-old model from Hampshire, regretted her 2012 belly tattoo featuring Lost Prophets lyrics "scream your heart out" after the band's lead singer Ian Watkins was imprisoned for 35 years for child sex offences. Multiple tattoo artists initially told her the bold lyrics couldn't be covered, until celebrity tattooist Kevin Paul designed a giant owl to conceal them after ten and a half hours of work. Now satisfied with her new design and having accumulated over 20 inkings, Ellia resolved to choose tattoos with personal significance in the future.

Teng Jiazhi, a taxi driver from Shenyang, North China, has set a record by taking 30,000 selfies with passengers over the past ten years, a unique tipping system he created after picking up tourists in -30C weather who offered tips that he graciously declined. Known affectionately as "Uncle Teng," the 58-year-old driver handpicked 6,402 of his best selfie photos for the 'Shanghai Big World Jinisi Record,' featuring passengers from around the world, and initially challenged himself to collect 10,000 selfies for the 'Smile Shenyang' campaign during the 2013 National Games. His genuine, contagious smile and friendly demeanor—whether chatting, singing folk songs, or taking photos with up to twenty of his eighty daily passengers—have made him one of the most recognized faces in the region and helped brighten countless journeys.

Mindy Kaling has denied awareness of her brother Vijay Chokal-Ingam's controversial claim that he pretended to be African American to gain admission to medical school, with her representative telling US Weekly she has been estranged from him for years and unaware of his decision to apply under a different name and race—though Chokal-Ingam revealed that his sister, the "Mindy Project" star Vera Mindy Chokalingam, told him she is "ashamed" of him for writing his forthcoming book "Almost Black." Chokal-Ingam claims he transformed his appearance and joined black student organizations to become a competitive applicant at eleven prestigious medical schools including Harvard and Columbia in 1998, ultimately attending St. Louis University School of Medicine before dropping out, though critics argue his experiment trivializes the systemic racism facing black applicants and undermines the structural necessity of affirmative action.

Julie McKenzie initially attributed her severe back pain to scoliosis based on her GP's diagnosis, but by seeking a second opinion and undergoing an MRI scan, she discovered she actually had a malignant tumour on her coccyx—a condition that, while rare, affects around 1,000 people annually and can be confused with benign tumours or the chronic pain that affects 8 per cent of back pain sufferers. Her case highlights how back pain is often under-investigated and serious spinal conditions can be missed, with experts recommending that MRI scans should be offered when pain persists for eight weeks or more. After having her coccyx removed along with the 2cm tumour in October 2013, Julie's cancer was found to be contained and she is now considered cured, though she continues to manage arthritis-related back pain.

A French Bulldog in Eureka, California was caught on camera by Ryan Burns continuously honking a car horn while left alone in an SUV, with the video later accumulating over one million views online after he uploaded it. When Burns investigated the persistent beeping, he discovered the disgruntled pooch standing on its hind legs with its front paws pressed firmly on the horn, and the dog's owner subsequently stepped forward to defend the situation. Though some viewers criticized the moment as animal cruelty, the owner claimed the pet was only left for a short time in cool weather with access to food and water, insisting the "absolutely spoiled rotten" dog simply wasn't happy about being left behind.

Anthony Barbour, a 33-year-old photographer from Liverpool, has transformed typical British locations—including Sefton Park and Liverpool One Shopping Centre, Mudeford Beach and Durdle Door in Dorset, and Burley Village in the New Forest—into surreal mind-bending 360-degree photographs by slowly rotating in one spot and capturing 50 pictures that he meticulously stitches together in Photoshop. Finding the right location with interesting landscapes and open spaces, Mr. Barbour spends hours shooting from floor to sky, using these intricate artistic techniques as an escape from a recent illness diagnosis. He says keeping himself artistically occupied through photography has been important for his well-being and has motivated him to develop more complex editing skills.

Waheed Ahmed, a 21-year-old university student and son of Rochdale Labour councillor Shakil Ahmed, was arrested on suspicion of terrorism offences after being stopped at the Turkish border while allegedly attempting to enter Syria with eight relatives, including four young children. Upon landing in the UK earlier this week, he was detained at Birmingham Airport on April 14, along with five other family members held at Manchester Airport and in the Rochdale area. Greater Manchester Police confirmed that all six individuals have been released without charge, with no evidence found to suggest any imminent threat to the community.

A 32-year-old Massachusetts man named Carlos Colina is facing murder charges in connection with another man's remains discovered Saturday in Cambridge—first found in a duffel bag along a walkway, with additional body parts discovered in an apartment building nearby. The remains belonged to 26-year-old Jonathan Camilien, who knew Colina, and Colina was previously arraigned on charges of assault and battery and improper disposal of a body, with bail revoked in an alleged assault case involving a different victim. Colina is scheduled to be arraigned on April 14 for the murder charge.

Huddersfield beat Catalans Dragons 38-14 in a Super League victory, with in-form winger Aaron Murphy scoring twice in a seven-try romp to end their three-match losing run. Former Wakefield Wildcats star Murphy, who has now scored six tries in his last five appearances, opened the scoring from Jake Connor's pass before adding a second try from a Danny Brough kick, while teammate Ukuma Ta'ai also bagged two tries. The Giants took full advantage of a sin-bin against Willie Tong in the second half, running in two quick tries to secure the comfortable win after leading 22-8 at the break.

Early Saturday morning around 12:15am, at least three suspects approached a man pumping gas at a 76 Station in South Los Angeles, attempted to carjack him, and after he refused, poured gasoline on his white Dodge Charger and set it on fire while two passengers were inside. Although no one was injured, the vehicle was completely destroyed in the arson attack. The Los Angeles County Fire Department is investigating the incident, and the suspects remain at large.

Although Hillary Clinton boasts a robust 3.6 million Twitter followers, only 44 percent of her Twitter fan base consists of real people who are active on the social media platform, with at least 15 percent completely fake, according to online measuring tools—a stark contrast to President Barack Obama's Twitter followers, of which just 4 percent are considered fake. Clinton's social media presence has been further undermined by revelations that her Facebook fans may represent purchased 'likes' rather than earned engagement, with over 46,000 fake accounts listing Baghdad as their hometown, prompting Facebook to deploy teams of employees to root out such fraudulent activity. Hillary Clinton's Twitter follower-count and overall digital metrics appear significantly inflated, raising questions about the authenticity of her social media presence during her presidential campaign.

Andrea Dossena, the 33-year-old man Liverpool once paid £7 million for, was arrested on suspicion of shoplifting at Harrods in Knightsbridge on Tuesday after scoring memorable goals against Real Madrid and Manchester United during his time at the club. Dossena, now playing for League One strugglers Leyton Orient, was later bailed pending further enquiries in late April. The Italian international, who earned £40,000 a week on Merseyside, made only 13 Premier League appearances for Liverpool before his career took him to Sunderland and eventually the third tier.

Animal Rescue Corps workers freed nearly 100 dogs from a blacked-out barn in Manchester, Tennessee, where they had been kept in deplorable conditions including complete darkness, filth, and their own waste, with some suffering acid burns and a wheelbarrow containing 10 dead dogs discovered on the property. The 97 dogs, which included various breeds such as Golden Retrievers and Shih Tzus, were severely emaciated and close to death, experiencing heart problems and infections from light deprivation, though rescue workers described a transformative moment when the animals were brought outside and absorbed the sunshine and oxygen for the first time. Property owner Caroline Irby, 64, was charged with 10 counts of animal cruelty, and the rescued dogs were transported to a shelter in Lebanon, Tennessee, where Animal Rescue Corps said they had witnessed the worst conditions in their organizational history.

Michael Easy, 29, from Southampton, sparked a nationwide police manhunt in 2013 after attacking his pregnant former girlfriend at a party and fleeing, taunting officers with Facebook photos of himself in a platinum wig and pink Dame Edna Everage-style glasses posted under a fake profile. Jailed for 15 months, he breached his restraining order upon release by leaving another party with the same woman in December, then assaulted two prison officers at Winchester Prison during his initial incarceration. He now faces four months in jail for these latest offenses.

Meet Harold Ekeh, an 18-year-old Long Islander who has been accepted to all eight Ivy League schools after submitting his college applications, making him only the second Long Island high school senior in a year to achieve this feat. Harold, who moved to Long Island from Nigeria 10 years ago with his parents Paul and Roseline Ekeh, attributes his success to his family's resilience and his determination to seize every opportunity available to him. Though he downplays his achievement and is considering his options among schools where Yale accepts just 6.3 percent of applicants, Harold is currently leaning towards Yale, following in the footsteps of another Long Island student, Kwasi Enin, who chose Yale after being accepted to all eight Ivies.

David Cameron and his wife Samantha revealed in emotional interviews that caring for their son Ivan, who was born with Ohtahara Syndrome—a rare brain disorder requiring round-the-clock care—left them "falling apart" and pushed their marriage close to breaking point. Ivan, who suffered from cerebral palsy and severe epileptic fits and died suddenly at age six in 2009, was described by the Camerons as a blessing despite the immense physical and emotional strain his condition placed on their family. Speaking ahead of what would have been Ivan's 13th birthday, Cameron reflected on how his son's death caused their "whole world to collapse," though he and Samantha remain proud of Ivan and the profound love they shared with him.

The BBC's iPlayer service has been cutting off the vital last minutes of numerous radio dramas such as Dad's Army and Hancock's Half Hour, leaving listeners frustrated and calling the problem 'irritating' and 'annoying'. The BBC attributes the issue to live radio station broadcasts causing programme start and end times to shift, confusing the automatic recording system—a problem that has persisted for at least three years despite being raised on the Radio 4 Feedback programme. In response, the BBC's General Manager has apologized and announced that among the thousands of shows every week made available on demand, they are now adding two minutes to the end of all recorded versions to prevent shows from missing their final moments.

CNN Student News on April 3, 2015, features international headlines alongside California's historic drought and its impact on water usage, with a show transcript available for review. The program includes a Weekly Newsquiz covering current events such as the Ebola virus spread in West Africa, airstrikes in Yemen, and religious holidays being celebrated worldwide. Teachers and students age 13 or older using CNN Student News can comment for a chance to be mentioned on the CNN Student News Roll Call, selected from the previous show's submissions.

Liverpool striker Daniel Sturridge faces an uphill battle with an injury problem for Sunday's FA Cup semi-final against Aston Villa after being excluded from Monday night's Premier League match against Newcastle United following a knock at Blackburn Rovers last week. Manager Brendan Rodgers will monitor Sturridge's condition throughout the week as the FA Cup represents Liverpool's last chance for silverware this season, with the club already without several other injured players but still within striking distance of Manchester City in the league standings. Despite the mounting injury concerns, Liverpool retain hopes of closing the gap on City and securing a top-four finish to qualify for next season's Champions League.

Thomas Buckett, a teenage burglar seriously injured when he fell 15 feet through a glass skylight while breaking into his school's tuck shop during a May 2010 break-in, sustained life-changing head injuries that left him in a coma for two weeks. After the Buckett family sued Staffordshire County Council for failing to secure the premises, a judge ruled the incident was the fault of Thomas and his friends for trespassing and ordered them to pay an interim legal bill of £150,000, potentially climbing to £260,000. Though the court acknowledged the school should have risk-assessed roof access, it determined few reasonably achievable security measures within a typical school budget could have prevented teenagers from climbing on the roof during non-school hours.

Jose Mourinho, the 52-year-old Chelsea manager and former Real Madrid boss, believes he is improving in every aspect of his job—from the way he reads and prepares games to his training methodology and overall tactical evolution—but admits his one weakness is his inability to avoid being truthful when facing the media. Mourinho regularly finds himself in trouble with the Football Association due to his frank comments, including a £25,000 fine earlier this season for claiming there was a campaign against his side following refereeing decisions in their game against Southampton. Despite acknowledging that his honesty with the press is a problem, Mourinho insists he cannot change this aspect of himself, stating that he is "never a hypocrite" when addressing the media.

In a significant election for Ferguson, Missouri, two black candidates were elected to the city council, tripling African-American representation following the August shooting death of 18-year-old Michael Brown by a white police officer. Wesley Bell, a lawyer and criminology professor who serves as municipal judge in neighboring Velda City, defeated Lee Smith in the Ward 3 race, while Ella Jones won in Ward 1, bringing African-American representation on the six-member council to 50%—double the presence of the lone black incumbent councilman. The election, held over a month after a Justice Department report detailed systemic racism and abuse in Ferguson's government and police department, saw voter turnout nearly double to 29.4% from the previous election's 12.3%.

Chelsea will face Paris Saint-Germain, the team that knocked Jose Mourinho's side out of the Champions League, in a pre-season friendly on July 25 in North Carolina as part of their summer tour to the United States. The Blues will play three games across the pond—including matches against the New York Red Bulls and Barcelona—to prepare for defending their probable Premier League title, which they lead by 10 points over Arsenal. After their US tour, Chelsea will host Fiorentina at Stamford Bridge on August 5 before the Premier League campaign kicks off.

Burnley manager Sean Dyche is attempting to protect struggling striker Danny Ings, who hasn't scored since February 11, by insisting the entire team must share the goalscoring burden after their 1-0 defeat to bottom-of-the-table Everton left them needing to score more than one goal in their next seven matches to survive in the Premier League. While Dyche acknowledges that 23-year-old Ings needs to rediscover his form, he emphasizes it's unfair to pin Burnley's survival hopes on one player and stresses the team must capitalize on the chances they are creating. Meanwhile, Everton manager Roberto Martinez remains noncommittal about signing winger Aaron Lennon permanently despite his impressive recent performances on loan from Tottenham, while young England player Ross Barkley's early penalty miss against Burnley underscored the team's inconsistency.

English photographer John Chapple, originally from North Devon, has traveled worldwide capturing stunning panoramic images using a film camera—from grassy English fields to downtown city skyscrapers and Australian landscapes. Chapple uses a Linhof Technorama 617s III camera to create his distinctive wide-format shots and has emphasized the importance of investing in quality equipment like a good tripod and comfortable boots for landscape photography. In a 2012 interview with Landscape Photography Magazine, Chapple expressed his dream of making a living from his landscape work and explained his motivation to document the world's natural beauty for future generations.

Celebrities are increasingly sporting controversial statement bags emblazoned with inappropriate imagery and slogans, exemplified by Madonna's new mock croc handbag featuring the drug-related slogan "dealer" in diamond lettering, which she showcased on Instagram. The trend spans multiple stars and designers, including Rihanna's 2013 cannabis-leaf clutch and gun-themed bags paired with her gun tattoo, Rumer Willis's "Guardian Angel" bag featuring a handgun outline, and designer James Piatt's "Foundling bag" inspired by abandoned babies. Other notable examples include Kanye West's defaced iconic Hermes Birkin bag painted with nude figures, later followed by Kim's daughter North West hand-painting a second defaced Hermes bag, demonstrating how fashion designers continue to push boundaries with provocative accessory designs.

ABC's World News Tonight with David Muir has officially overtaken underfire rival NBC's Nightly News in the ratings war for the first time in over five and a half years, attracting 84,000 more viewers according to Nielsen ratings for the week ending April 3. The ratings shift comes two months after NBC anchor Brian Williams received a six-month suspension for misleading viewers about his Iraq War coverage, ending NBC's 288-week winning streak that had stretched back to September 2009. Veteran Lester Holt has led NBC Nightly News during Williams' absence, though a new ratings calculation method that prevents NBC from including overnight repeat broadcasts also contributed to the competitive shift.

Robert Downey Jr. has been accused of making a racist remark about Oscar-winning Mexican director Alejandro Gonzalez Inarritu during an interview while promoting the new film Avengers: Age of Ultron. When asked about Inarritu's claim that superhero movies constitute "cultural genocide"—poisoning audiences by depicting heroes killing people who don't share their beliefs—Downey Jr. responded by praising the director's intelligence for formulating such a phrase in Spanish, a comment that sparked backlash on social media for being perceived as racist and ignorant. The controversy is the latest in a troubled press tour for the film that also saw co-stars Jeremy Renner and Chris Evans apologizing for calling their colleague Scarlett Johansson's character a "slut" during a TV interview.

On April 12, a 25-year-old Freddie Gray died from a severed spinal cord after being arrested and transported in a police van in Baltimore, though a leaked affidavit claimed Gray may have been intentionally injuring himself inside the van by banging against the walls—a assertion a witness who spoke about Gray's arrest rejected. Gray's family attorney and local reporters disputed the account, questioning the accuracy of police reports and noting inconsistencies with the leaked affidavit. The investigation into his death remains ongoing with six police officers suspended pending the outcome.

Sir Paul McCartney married Heather Mills in 2002 at Castle Leslie in Ireland, and newly surfaced photographs from the wedding reception—the must-have snap for newlyweds—reveal a notable absence of joy, particularly from his daughters Stella and Mary, who were unhappy about their father's choice of partner following their mother Linda's death. The remarkable pictures, which belonged to Sir Paul's late housekeeper Rose Martin and include images of Sir Paul acting out a mock striptease, are the first happy couple celebrating moments to emerge from the reception and have been kept private for over a decade due to a media blackout ordered by the couple. These previously unseen photos serve as a poignant portent of the bitterness that followed when the pair separated in 2006, leading to a bitter divorce in 2008.

Sebastian Vettel secured Ferrari's first victory in 35 races at the Malaysian Grand Prix, ending both the team's 20-race drought and its remarkable dry spell since Fernando Alonso's triumph at the 2013 Spanish Grand Prix. Upon returning to Ferrari's factory in Maranello, Vettel received a hero's welcome where the team celebrated with traditional rituals, including raising a Ferrari flag at the entry gate for the first time in nearly two years. While acknowledging that the upcoming Chinese Grand Prix in Shanghai may prove more challenging for Ferrari, Vettel is determined that this Malaysian victory will not be a one-off and remains committed to building on the team's strong package in races ahead.

A 51-year-old British-born mother, Iona Costello, and her daughter Emily were found safe and well on New York's Upper West Side after going missing on March 30 while visiting Manhattan, where her husband George had died of a heart attack in 2012. The pair had been under stress due to a legal battle over George's will, which Iona inherited, leading to disputes with his adult children from previous marriages, while her brother-in-law John Costello faced separate vehicular manslaughter charges. Police closed the case after finding them at a hotel, with surveillance footage showing them leaving their parked car laughing, though suspicion had briefly fallen on her stepson George Costello Jr., who has a long criminal history.

A Nigerian Christian's prayer aboard a rubber dinghy carrying around 100 African refugees across the Mediterranean sparked violence when Muslim passengers on the rickety craft ordered him to stop, resulting in 12 Christians being thrown overboard and drowning. Italian police arrested 15 people suspected in the killings as part of a broader humanitarian crisis, with over 10,000 refugees rescued by Italian ships in the past week and more than 900 deaths or missing persons reported since the start of the year. In a separate tragedy near Italy's Lampedusa Island, another sea rescue recovered 70 refugees, including a six-month-old baby with severe burns, who had been forced onto a boat by smugglers without receiving medical treatment for injuries sustained in a gas explosion.

Four-month-old baby Evan Summerfield from Devon fell ill with meningoccal septicaemia and died just 12 hours after laughing for the first time, when a distinctive rash appeared that didn't fade under pressure. Despite doctors and nurses at Derriford Hospital battling to save him, Evan died soon after his diagnosis with meningitis. His family and the Meningitis Research Foundation are now raising awareness about how rapidly the disease strikes, noting that it claims approximately one life every day in the UK and Ireland.

# Summary

A 24-year-old Chicago Cubs fan named Krista Dotzenrod from Minnesota caught a foul ball in her full cup of beer during a Cubs vs. San Diego Padres game on Saturday afternoon. After catching the ball, Dotzenrod chugged the beer with the ball still inside to the cheers of surrounding fans. The Cubs won the game 7-6 in 11 innings, and Dotzenrod kept the ball as a souvenir in her apartment.

After Erik Meldik pranked his girlfriend Dominika Petrinova by falsely claiming he had put her beloved dog Sam in the washing machine as a joke, causing her to burst into tears, she decided to seek painful revenge. Petrinova executed her own painful prank by gluing waxing strips to a plastic chair and tricking her naked boyfriend into sitting on it, leaving him screaming in pain as he was stuck by his genitals. The prankster eventually ripped himself free, leaving skin and hair behind, in what Petrinova described as a lesson he would not forget.

Hull City is targeting QPR centre-back Nedum Onuoha, who is also being pursued by Everton, West Ham United, and Stoke manager Mark Hughes, while the Loftus Road club considers streamlining its squad if relegated. The 28-year-old has been a regular starter and could depart alongside other players facing potential departures. Additionally, Hull City is showing interest in West Bromwich Albion striker Victor Anichebe, likely to be sold this summer, and QPR have held talks over a move for Catania defender Nicolas Spolli, currently on loan at Roma.

London is experiencing a trend of single-item menu restaurants, with the latest addition being Oshun Café, which serves £6 bowls of Brazilian superfood acai berries in four variations including Oshun Native made with acaí pulp and bananas, Oshun Kong featuring acaí pulp with lemongrass and ginger, Oshun Tang made with courgette and lime, and Oshun Maya with raw cacao and avocado. This follows other specialized eateries such as the Porridge Cafe and Brick Lane's Cereal Killer Cafe, the UK's first cereal cafe featuring 120 cereals with 20 toppings, as well as the forthcoming Melt Room in Soho, which will serve four breakfast toasties alongside six signature melts. The acai berries, rich in antioxidants with eight times more than blueberries, are considered superfoods renowned for their health benefits and weight loss qualities.

The town of Zapalnya in south-east Bulgaria was submerged 50 years ago when an artificial lake was created for a Communist-era dam, forcing residents to abandon their homes and leave the settlement. The St Ivan Rilski Church, built in 1895 and once the heart of the community known for rose oil production, now stands as the only remaining evidence of the town, its partially-submerged ruins visible in the Zhrebchevo reservoir near Tvardica. Today, the haunting church ruins and surrounding area have become a popular destination for tourists, photographers, and fishermen, with segments of the abandoned cemetery occasionally visible depending on water levels.
