Real Madrid delivered a dominant performance at the Bernabeu, defeating Granada 9-1 in a La Liga clash that showcased their attacking prowess. Cristiano Ronaldo was the star of the show, scoring an impressive five goals including a seven-minute hat-trick—one coming off Modric's cross—while Karim Benzema added two goals and Gareth Bale contributed one, with an own goal from Diego Mainz completing the rout; Robert Ibanez scored Granada's only consolation goal. The huge victory allows Real Madrid to bounce back from their recent Clasico defeat to Barcelona and strengthens their position in the La Liga title race as they look to secure the championship.

Everton will host Southampton at Goodison Park for Saturday's Barclays Premier League clash at 3pm, with both sides facing injury concerns ahead of the match. Everton striker Romelu Lukaku will undergo a late fitness test on a hamstring problem, while Southampton midfielder Steven Davis is also a doubt after suffering a groin injury on international duty. The visitors arrive in strong form with four wins in their last six Premier League away matches, though they have struggled historically at this ground—winning just one of their last 15 games at Goodison Park—where Everton's record Premier League win came in a 7-1 victory over Southampton in November 1996, a scoreline the Toffees hold a commanding 39-24 head-to-head advantage over the Saints.

FC Tokyo forward Yoshinori Muto is considering a move to Premier League leaders Chelsea after the club made a £4 million offer this month, according to FC Tokyo president Naoki Ogane, though he remains uncertain about joining as Chelsea plans to loan him to partner club Vitesse Arnhem rather than guarantee regular playing time. The 22-year-old Japan international, fresh off scoring against Shonan Bellmare last week, has stated he wants to carefully consider the opportunity and only join a club where he can play regularly and develop his potential. Chelsea manager Jose Mourinho acknowledged his awareness of Muto but maintained that while commercial interests play a role in the club's transfer policy, Chelsea would only sign him if he is a good player or prospect.

Jason Brown, a former Wales goalkeeper, was racially abused by John Wild during a football match in October 2014, with Wild found guilty at Bristol Magistrates Court and issued a three-year banning order. Although Brown welcomed the successful prosecution, he criticized Kick It Out, the FA, and the PFA for not doing enough to support him during the incident, despite Kick It Out's Professional Players Engagement Manager Paul Mortimer meeting with him and offering assistance. Kick It Out responded to Brown's concerns by explaining their process and involvement with authorities, stating they actively follow complaints through to conclusion and work collectively to tackle discrimination in English football.

Scott Turner Schofield, a performer and author known for his one-person shows addressing transgender issues, will become the first transgender man to have a recurring role on a daytime soap opera when he makes his television acting debut on *The Bold and the Beautiful* in May, joining another transgender person making a monumental TV appearance by playing Nick, who mentors Maya Avant (played by Karla Mosley) through her gender identity journey. According to the New York Daily News, Schofield's role will highlight the discrimination faced by the transgender community while providing a positive portrayal that transgender people and their loved ones can celebrate. "It's going in the direction that transgender people can be happy about," said Schofield.

Researchers warn that children should not be encouraged to play rugby at school due to high injury rates, with as many as 1 in 8 suffering injuries serious enough to miss at least seven training sessions or matches, ranging from bruises to concussions and spinal damage. Professor Allyson Pollock argues that the Government's plan to increase rugby participation lacks adequate injury surveillance and prevention systems, and calls for rugby to be made safer by converting contact rugby into touch rugby—already required for children under 10—and eliminating scrums and tackles where most injuries occur. While the Rugby Football Union maintains that rugby provides significant physical and social benefits and has safety rules in place, Pollock emphasizes that comprehensive monitoring systems—such as those implemented by Rugby World Cup holders New Zealand—should be established before expanding school rugby programs.

Tiger Woods took time off from his limited tournament schedule to give retired Chinese basketball star Yao Ming golfing tips at Nike's headquarters in Shanghai, where the 7ft 6in former Houston Rockets centre towered over golf's former world No. 1 during their lesson. Woods has confirmed his return to professional competition at the Players Championship on May 7 at TPC Sawgrass, allaying concerns about a wrist injury he sustained at the US Masters, arriving earlier than Jack Nicklaus's prediction that he would feature at the Memorial at Muirfield Village in June. The 14-time major winner's comeback at the prestigious tournament gives him a chance to repeat his 2013 victory at the course.

Andy Murray advanced to the Miami Open semi-finals with a 3-6, 6-4, 6-1 victory over 21-year-old Austrian prospect Dominic Thiem, who nearly upset the world No. 4 by pushing him to 4-4 in the second set before Murray regrouped after dropping the first. With his fiancée watching from the stands and celebration in the air—Murray threw his sweatband to the crowd after the win—the British star dominated the final set, though Thiem, coming off a tournament loss in a Challenger event, stretched Murray throughout with powerful shotmaking and near-misses. Murray subsequently defended the recent naturalization of Slovenian player Aljaz Bedene as a British citizen, encouraging fellow British players to use his arrival as motivation rather than complain about the decision.

Vincent Kompany admits his mother insisted he finish his education before joining Manchester United at 17, when Sir Alex Ferguson inquired about the Anderlecht prospect, and he believes everything worked out perfectly after eventually moving to Manchester City in 2008. The City captain, who has won two Premier League titles with the club, remains passionate about his commitment, noting that United asked about him during his early career but his priorities lay elsewhere. As Old Trafford welcomes City for their upcoming derby, Kompany emphasizes the rivalry between the two big clubs has evolved into a competitive 50-50 matchup, with City aiming to win their fifth consecutive derby.

Margaret Atwood, the 75-year-old author whose latest book Stone Mattress prompted controversy, has criticized the Duchess of Cambridge's fashion sense, describing her as an "uneventful" dresser who is cautious and advised on what to wear, and saying she hasn't lived up to Princess Diana's fashion icon reputation. Though Atwood believes Kate is wise not to follow in Diana's footsteps, she joins other high-profile female writers like Hilary Mantel and Joan Smith in attacking the duchess's public image and personal style. Despite this criticism, the Duchess's fashion choices continue to be highly influential, with items she wears regularly selling out within hours.

# Summary

Martyn Uzzell, 51, an experienced cyclist, died instantly after hitting a four-inch deep pothole on the A65 in North Yorkshire during a charity cycle ride from Land's End to John O'Groats for Macmillan Cancer Support, which threw him into the path of a car. Despite being inspected twice and reported by police a month before the tragedy, the council failed to repair the pothole, which a coroner ruled was "the cause of the incident." His widow Kate Uzzell has won a six-figure payout from North Yorkshire County Council, though the council refused to formally apologize for the state of the road.

Cristiano Ronaldo made headlines across Spanish sports papers after the beaming Portugal international scored five goals in a single game for the first time in his career, as Real Madrid beat struggling Granada 9-1 on Sunday under Carlo Ancelotti's management, with his impressive haul including an eight-minute hat-trick alongside goals from Karim Benzema and Gareth Bale. Meanwhile, league leaders Barcelona maintained their four-point advantage at the top after defeating Celta Vigo 1-0, with French defender Jeremy Mathieu securing the victory with a diving header.

Holland defeated Spain 2-0 in a friendly match on Tuesday night, with goals from Stefan de Vrij and Davy Klaassen, but Holland manager Guus Hiddink strongly criticized his own supporters for booing Andres Iniesta when the Spanish midfielder entered the game in the 76th minute, calling their behavior "a disgrace" toward the world-class player. Hiddink speculated the booing may have stemmed from Iniesta's goal against the Netherlands in 2010, expressing bewilderment that such conduct had no place in sport, while praising his team's performance in this much-needed boost after a difficult start to his second tenure as national coach. Despite the victory, Hiddink remained focused on condemning the disrespectful treatment of his opponent, emphasizing that Iniesta's talent and character deserved better from the home crowd.

Hillary Clinton's trusted aide Huma Abedin was spotted enjoying a family outing in New York with her husband Anthony Weiner and their two-year-old son Jordan at a Mexican restaurant, just days after she and Clinton faced criticism for not tipping at an Iowa Chipotle. This time, Abedin skipped the fast food chain for the more upscale Tortaría, where Weiner wore his New York Mets baseball cap backwards while dining. The outing marked a brief respite for the low-profile staffer before the 2016 presidential campaign intensifies.

Football Shirt Friday, now in its third annual year during Bowel Cancer Awareness Month, encourages supporters to wear football shirts—old or new—to raise money for The Bobby Moore Fund for Cancer Research UK in memory of 1966 World Cup-winning captain Bobby Moore, who died from bowel and liver cancer in 1993 at age 51. Celebrities including Clare Balding in her Pele shirt, Rob Lee, and Bianca Westwood showed their support on Twitter, while personalities like Ian Abrahams and Danny Murphy discussed the campaign on TalkSport, alongside Paul Davis sharing his 1993 Coca Cola Cup shirt. Fans can participate by wearing any football shirt on any Friday in April and donate through footballshirtfriday.org using the hashtag #FootballShirtFriday.

Quinton 'Rampage' Jackson returns to the UFC after two years with Bellator to face Fabio Maldonado at UFC 186 in Montreal on Saturday night, but endured a tough legal fight before being permitted to compete. Despite the stressful preparation, Jackson expressed gratitude to fans and praised the catchweight fight at 215lb, which he believes is fair for both fighters. The former light-heavyweight champion expressed his preference for stand-up fighting over wrestling, eager to deliver an exciting brawl rather than the grinding wrestling matches he says disappoint fans.

A pregnant woman and her partner were rescued from the English Channel near Dungeness Point, Kent, after their trimaran capsized. The man tied a rope around himself and dove under the overturned vessel to retrieve an emergency flare while his partner clung to the hull in the water, alerting rescuers to their position. Thanks to their lifejackets, the AIS system on board, and a nearby rescue boat that arrived within minutes of the vessel capsizing, both were taken to the hospital where they were found to be cold and shaken but unharmed.

A 17-year-old identified as Cassandra C., diagnosed with Hodgkin's lymphoma in September, has completed her chemotherapy treatment and left Connecticut Children's Medical Center on Monday after being held there since December under state custody. Cassandra had initially refused treatment and ran away after two days, objecting to what she viewed as the toxicity of chemotherapy, prompting a judge to order her into custody of the Connecticut Department of Children and Families to ensure she received the lifesaving treatment, which doctors said gave her an 85% chance of survival. Now that her treatment is complete and she is in remission, Cassandra has been released, and state officials have credited the hospital staff and medical team for successfully saving her life during this difficult ordeal.

A 10-year-old Minnesota boy named Barway Edward Collins, whose body was found in the Mississippi River over the weekend, had his father Pierre Collins arrested Monday on suspicion of second-degree murder. According to Crystal Police Chief Stephanie Revering, electronic evidence places Pierre Collins in the area at the time Barway disappeared on March 18, and authorities believe he has not been truthful with them, while his stepmother Yamah Collins was also taken into custody. Barway, who had emigrated from Liberia in 2011 to live with his father, was last seen after school when surveillance footage showed him turning toward the parking lot as if called by someone he knew.

Following a devastating 7.8 magnitude earthquake that struck Nepal on Saturday and killed over 4,000 people, rescue efforts continue amid rubble across Kathmandu while mass cremations have been taking place at the Pashupatinath temple and along the Bagmati river, where hundreds of bodies are being burned on pyres as families perform Hindu death rites. The Pashupatinath temple has been overwhelmed, receiving around 500 bodies per day compared to its usual average of 30, while wood shortages and limited cremation space have forced families to conduct ceremonies wherever space allows. The earthquake also triggered an avalanche on Mount Everest that killed 17 people, and authorities struggle with shortages of water, food, and medical resources.

A horse named Cody wandered into a thick mud bog in Belvedere, south-east London, and became stuck in what was hardly Becher's Brook—one of Aintree's most notorious fences—requiring fire crews to rescue him in a more than hour-long operation. Though covered in mud and shaken by the ordeal, Cody was otherwise unharmed and was taken to the stables to be examined by a vet. The incident meant the horse would definitely not be running in the Grand National.

Karen Bell, 42, from Hull, was jailed for 12 months after blackmailing a man she met on the free dating site Plenty of Fish by threatening to post videos of their sex trysts online. Bell initially demanded £25 and later increased her demands to £55, falsely claiming her son had secretly filmed their encounter and would publish it on Facebook unless she was paid. Hull Crown Court heard that the man was frightened by her threats and eventually reported her to police, leading to her conviction for blackmail.

Didier Drogba, the Chelsea forward who scored against Leicester on Wednesday, has been awarded the Barclays Spirit of the Game trophy for his inspired charity work through the Didier Drogba Foundation in Africa, which aims to inspire the next generation of footballers and support education and health initiatives in his native Ivory Coast. The foundation, bolstered by £400,000 raised at a recent charity ball attended by Chelsea stars including Eden Hazard, Petr Cech, and Branislav Ivanovic, will use the funds to complete a medical clinic in Abidjan and support mobile clinics for those unable to access healthcare. Young people like Zebeto, who dream of following in Drogba's footsteps, have been inspired by his efforts to give African children the opportunity to pursue their dreams.

Mohammad Qamaruzzaman, a senior official of Bangladesh's largest Islamist political party Jamaat-e-Islami, was executed by hanging in Dhaka after being convicted of crimes against humanity for leading a militia group that killed around 120 unarmed farmers during Bangladesh's 1971 independence war. The execution, which took place after Qamaruzzaman refused to seek presidential clemency and the Supreme Court rejected his final appeal, drew international criticism and sparked calls for nationwide protests from his supporters and party. Bangladeshi security personnel escorted an ambulance carrying his body as it left the Central Jail following the Saturday night execution.

This article explores Washington, D.C. through the lens of *House of Cards* – the hit US political TV series starring Kevin Spacey as Francis Underwood alongside Robin Wright – taking readers to iconic locations like the Capital Grille where political power-broking occurs and the U.S. Capitol atop Capitol Hill. The author highlights how real Washington politics mirrors the darker themes of the show, while also showcasing the city's major attractions including Ben's Chili Bowl restaurant (a favorite of President Obama), the Smithsonian Museum, and the National Archives with its foundational American documents. Despite Washington's struggle to compete with New York for tourists, the article demonstrates that the city offers numerous public monuments and free world-class museums that reveal both the idealism and ruthlessness embedded in American political history.

Eight-year-old Leo Bernal was shot in the head by an armed home intruder in Culver City, California, while lying in bed, requiring doctors to open his skull to extract the bullet and leaving him with 23 staples holding his scalp together. After being released from the hospital on Friday, five days following the Sunday night shooting that left 14 bullets fired into his bedroom, Leo expressed hope to return to sports despite his traumatic experience. Leo's mother said her son is now terrified to return home, and the family has set up a GoFundMe account to help cover his medical costs while police search for the gunman's motive.

A suicide bomber detonated explosives near protesters in Khost, eastern Afghanistan, killing 17 people and wounding 64 others, including Afghan lawmaker Humayoon Humayoon. The attack struck around 10 a.m. local time as the group headed to join a larger rally against the provincial governor. According to reports from Kabul, the Taliban denied responsibility, and no other organization has claimed responsibility for the bombing that left dozens wounded.

According to the third World Happiness Report released by the Sustainable Development Solutions Network for the United Nations, Switzerland, Iceland, and Denmark rank as the world's happiest countries, with happiness linked to life expectancy, social support, generosity, freedom, low corruption, and higher GDP per capita—factors that economics professor Jeffrey Sachs of Columbia University emphasizes are collectively essential to well-being. The report was inspired by Bhutan's concept of "Gross National Happiness," and the U.N. designated March 20 as World Happiness Day to encourage governments to measure and improve their people's happiness. The article highlights the top 10 happiest places on Earth, including Finland, Canada, New Zealand, and Australia, each offering unique attractions such as geothermal hot springs in south Iceland, historic sites near Quebec City, and natural wonders like the Great Barrier Reef.

Four-year-old Austin West drowned in an in-ground pool at his family's home in Gastonia, North Carolina after slipping while chasing a bumblebee, with his chronic asthma contributing to the tragedy. When Austin's grandfather Jeff West attempted to set up a GoFundMe campaign for funeral expenses, he discovered scammers had already created multiple fraudulent fundraisers using the boy's name before the real GoFundMe page got established, though these fake accounts were subsequently removed. The West family remains in urgent financial need, having raised only $315 as of Friday evening, and has started an additional campaign selling T-shirts with Austin's picture to cover costs.

France's National Front family rift deepened when 86-year-old founder Jean-Marie Le Pen withdrew from regional elections after angering his daughter Marine, the party's current leader, with racist and anti-Semitic comments she condemned as "political suicide." Le Pen announced he would step aside to prevent damage to the party and suggested his 25-year-old granddaughter Marion Maréchal-Le Pen as the best replacement candidate, who subsequently confirmed she would seek the party's nomination. Opinion polls indicate that Marine Le Pen is likely to reach the second round of the 2017 presidential elections but is not expected to win.

Andrew Getty, the 47-year-old grandson of oil tycoon J. Paul Getty whose family fortune shaped American wealth, died Tuesday afternoon at his Los Angeles home, with authorities indicating his death "appears to be natural or accidental." Getty was found on his side near a bathroom, and some medication was recovered from the residence, though investigators remained uncertain about his medical history or current medication use. His father Gordon Getty, one of J. Paul Getty's three sons, joined his mother Ann in requesting privacy for the family during this difficult time.

The Wisden–MCC Cricket Photograph of the Year 2014 was won by Getty Images photographer Matthew Lewis for his stunning image of West Indies player Dwayne Bravo executing a full-length catch during the ICC World Twenty20 competition in Bangladesh. The award-winning photograph anchors a collection of striking cricket images that showcase the sport's global reach, from professional players competing in Twenty20 International matches to amateur competitors and children playing on a rubbish tip in Dhaka, Bangladesh, even to participants in the world's highest game of cricket on Mount Kilimanjaro's icy summit. These diverse portraits highlight cricket's universal appeal across skill levels and locations worldwide.

A new Reddit thread asking users to submit the most horrendous baby names they have ever encountered attracted over 18,000 comments in just 24 hours, with FEMAIL compiling the worst examples including "Airwrecker" (an alternative spelling of Erica), "Orgasm" (submitted by the daughter of a labor and delivery nurse), "I'munique," "Obamaniqua," "Like," "Boy Boy," and "Hellzel" (a combination of Hazel and Hells Angels). The thread revealed numerous examples of questionable parental judgment, from names inspired by celebrities like "Britney Shakira Beyonce" to names with unfortunate meanings. In contrast, the most popular baby names in the UK for 2014 were traditional choices: Sophia, Emily, and Lily for girls, and Muhammad, Oliver, and Jack for boys.

The number of hayfever sufferers in the UK is predicted to reach 20 million this summer, a third increase from last year, with five million people expected to experience symptoms for the first time as warm weather triggers an invisible pollen descent with high grass pollen counts—the main culprit allergen. Experts attribute this rise to prolonged warm, dry weather combined with increased air pollution and more potent pollen, noting that lengthening spring pollen seasons can trigger hayfever symptoms even in previously unaffected people. To manage symptoms, sufferers are advised to take preventative measures such as stocking up on antihistamines and steroids before the season starts, checking daily pollen counts, and taking protective actions like wearing sunglasses and keeping windows closed on high pollen days.

Raheem Sterling, a 20-year-old Liverpool forward pictured at the club's Melwood training ground, bears a Wembley tattoo symbolizing his childhood dreams on the St Raphael's estate in north London, and has turned down a £100,000-per-week contract offer while postponing negotiations until summer. Sterling's remarkable rise from joining Queens Park Rangers' Centre of Excellence at age 11 to becoming Liverpool's second-youngest debutant contrasts sharply with that of his former rival Christian Nanetti, whom he faced in Soccer AM's iconic Skill Skool challenge before Liverpool signed him for £500,000 at 15, while also scoring for England in their 4-0 win over Lithuania at Wembley last Friday night. With two years remaining on his current £35,000-per-week deal, Sterling is in a strong negotiating position with manager Brendan Rodgers, who seeks to convince him to sign what could be one of Liverpool's biggest contracts in history.

TV star Karren Brady agreed to help Prime Minister David Cameron announce 16,000 new apprenticeships at a National Grid training centre in Newark, where Baroness Brady stepped forward to assist with his jacket before sitting on the sidelines as he outlined the Conservative pledge to create three million more high-quality apprenticeships over five years if re-elected. The announcement featured commitments from major employers including Morrisons (9,000 apprentices), Costa Coffee, and the National Grid (500), with the Apprentice judge remarking that apprenticeships offer young people the chance to master a trade and gain financial security. Though Brady might have hoped for a more prominent role in the high-profile campaign event, she instead settled for supporting duties while Cameron delivered the keynote address on making apprenticeships level-pegging with university degrees.

Oscar Hübinette took extreme skiing to new heights by shredding down the icy slopes of the active Tolbachik volcano on Russia's Kamchatka Peninsula while his friend Fredrik Schenholm photographed the daring feat. The 12,000-foot volcano, which suddenly erupted in November 2012 after lying dormant since 1976, regularly ejected burning hot lava jets up to 656 feet high, creating a stark contrast with the white snow at -25°C. Fredrik's achievement captures a five-year quest to photograph a skier against an erupting volcano on one of the Kamchatka Peninsula's 160 volcanoes—29 still active—a triumph tempered by the reality that lava flow shifted just 100 meters from their tent during the expedition.

John Carver says he will use a whip to motivate Newcastle players ahead of Sunday's Tyne-Wear derby at the Stadium of Light, determined to end their run of four consecutive defeats against Sunderland. Carver admits that in the last four matches Sunderland have out-worked Newcastle and wanted victory more, but he is committed to ensuring his players impose themselves throughout the Premier League campaign's defining North-East derby. Meanwhile, Sunderland boss Dick Advocaat will take charge of his second game looking to maintain the Black Cats' winning record against Newcastle as they fight for Premier League survival.

Rangers goalkeeper Steve Simonsen will not face increased punishment for breaching the SFA's zero-tolerance gambling rules after an appellate tribunal rejected the governing body's appeal to enhance his penalty for betting on 50 games over a year—none relating to his own club's competitions. The one-game ban handed to Simonsen stands in stark contrast to previous betting offences, including teammate Ian Black's 10-game suspension for placing 160 wagers that included bets against Rangers itself. With the SFA's appeal now failed, Simonsen has served his single-match suspension and will not face further discipline.

Tim Krul's instinctive smile and congratulation toward Jermain Defoe after the Sunderland striker's stunning volley during the Wear-Tyne derby has drawn sharp criticism from pundits like Jamie Carragher and Danny Murphy, who argue such friendliness during an intense local match is inappropriate. The article defends Krul's sportsmanship by invoking the football world's enduring applause for Bobby Moore's famous embrace with Pele in 1970, arguing that acknowledging exceptional skill should not conflict with passion or dedication to one's team. The author suggests that the condemnation of Krul's gesture reveals how modern football has become "leeched of humanity" and questions whether such criticism would have been equally swift had the roles been reversed.

President Barack Obama and his wife Michelle paid over $93,000 in federal taxes on an adjusted gross income of more than $477,000 in 2014, with an effective tax rate of 19.6 percent, according to tax returns released Friday by the White House. The Obamas lowered their 2014 tax bill by claiming nearly $160,000 in itemized deductions, including $70,712 in charitable donations, while the couple's Illinois state income tax return and earnings of $94,889 from Obama's best-selling books were also documented. The White House simultaneously released Vice President Joe Biden's 2014 income tax returns, showing he and his wife paid over $90,000 in federal taxes on an adjusted gross income of nearly $389,000 with a 23.3 percent effective tax rate.

# Summary

Argentina centre Marcelo Bosch has signed a contract extension with Saracens following his heroic performance in the European Champions Cup quarter-final, where he kicked a last-minute penalty to secure a 12-11 victory over Racing Metro in Paris. The 33 times-capped international, who joined Saracens from Biarritz in 2013, has impressed rugby director Mark McCall with his dedication and improvement since arriving at the club. Bosch's contract extension follows new deals announced for locks George Kruis and Maro Itoje.

High-powered human rights lawyer Amal Clooney held a press conference at the National Press Club in Washington DC today, calling for the release of imprisoned former Maldives president Mohamed Nasheed, who was convicted under anti-terror laws and sentenced to 13 years in prison. George Clooney's newlywed wife, accompanied by Nasheed's wife Laila Ali and other legal representatives, declared she will not rest until the jailed Maldivian ex-president walks free and appealed to the US government to pressure the Maldives government for his release. The trial has sparked international concern, with human rights organizations like Amnesty International condemning it as a "sham trial" and "travesty of justice."

Paula Radcliffe, the 41-year-old running legend who has won the London Marathon three times and set an unbeaten world record time of 2 hours 15 minutes and 25 seconds in 2003, will compete in this weekend's race as her final marathon before retiring from professional running, saying she'll be happy to simply finish the 26.2-mile course. After battling back from injuries that prevented her from competing in the London 2012 Olympics and decades of winning races across track, road, and cross-country competitions, Radcliffe will run with the masses rather than elite competitors, though she remains likely to post one of the fastest times by a British woman. Despite her phenomenal achievements and Olympic disappointments, she will be honored with the inaugural John Disley Lifetime Achievement award on Sunday for an inspirational career combining elite athletics with motherhood.

Aaron Ramsey scored against Burnley in a goal that meant the world to him, dedicating it to his late grandmother Eileen who passed away weeks earlier. The Welsh midfielder, who had previously played in the 2008 FA Cup final for Cardiff, reflected on his dramatic extra-time winner against Hull City last season—a goal that ended Arsenal's nine-year trophy drought and saw him hugging the FA Cup following the victory at Wembley. With renewed confidence and no longer burdened by the pressure of their long wait for silverware, Ramsey expressed optimism about Arsenal's chances in the upcoming FA Cup semi-final and the Premier League title race, emphasizing that the team must remain focused and not take their opponents lightly.

Teenager Lily Sharp from the UK decided to prank her mother by faking her own kidnapping, meticulously escalating from innocent texts about a decorator to pretend ransom demands of £4000, referencing the famous Liam Neeson film Taken. Lily convinced her panicked mother that an intruder had taken her phone and kidnapped her, deliberately intensifying her mother's fears while she tried to keep calm and called the police, only to attempt one final cheeky text before revealing the hoax with a selfie from her sofa. Although her mother did not find the prank amusing and responded with profanity, the post went viral on Twitter, receiving 18,000 retweets and 25,000 favorites.

More than 10,000 migrants and refugees from North Africa have arrived in Sicily in recent days according to the Italian Coast Guard, fleeing desperate conditions and seeking what they view as a "promised land" in Europe, despite the death and suffering that characterize Mediterranean crossings. The influx has dramatically increased since Gadhafi's overthrow removed controls on migrant ships from Libya, prompting Italy to launch rescue operations like Mare Nostrum to save migrants from deadly crossings, though the program faced criticism and budget constraints. Despite these efforts and agreements to help patrol Tunisia's ports, thousands continue to risk their lives attempting the journey, with many who arrive in Italy eventually dispersing across Europe without proper processing or fingerprinting.

Sam Allardyce responded to West Ham co-owner David Sullivan's criticism that the team has been "exceedingly disappointing" in their last 12 games by urging him not to judge the club's season solely on 2015's results, insisting that winning games remains the bottom line while maintaining overall performances have been strong. Allardyce emphasized the season has been "terrific," with West Ham already beating last year's total by two points with seven games remaining—meeting the objectives set for him at the start of the campaign. Forward Enner Valencia is now available for selection against Stoke City on Saturday after recovering from a bizarre toe injury.

A new study by Lancashire's Edge Hill University has found a direct link between exam anxiety and poor academic performance, showing that students who worry about their grades are more likely to achieve lower results. Michael Ribton, principal at Burlington Danes Academy, offers practical advice for parents and students to manage exam stress, including creating revision timetables, using revision aids like flashcards, and trying techniques such as "Spaced Learning." Burlington Danes Academy student Fatima Sharif, who achieved four A grades at AS Level last summer and holds a conditional offer from Oxford, emphasizes that personal goal-setting and self-motivation—rather than external rewards—serve as key strategies for successful exam preparation.

Edinburgh's European Challenge Cup hopes have been severely damaged after Scotland international centre Matt Scott was ruled out for the rest of the season following shoulder surgery, leaving him a doubt for September's World Cup and potentially leaving Scotland coach Vern Cotter with a significant gap at centre alongside Glasgow's Alex Dunbar. Scott sustained the injury during the Six Nations against Ireland last month and despite initial hopes of natural recovery, further surgical intervention was deemed necessary after three weeks with no improvement, while Edinburgh also face fitness concerns over back-row forward David Denton following a head knock. Despite these setbacks, hooker Ross Ford insists Edinburgh remain confident ahead of their Challenge Cup semi-final against Newport Gwent Dragons at BT Murrayfield on Friday, with home advantage and fan support expected to help the club pursue their bid to reach the final at Twickenham Stoop on May 1.

Chef Bobby Flay's divorce from actress Stephanie March has become increasingly contentious after Page Six alleged Sunday that the 50-year-old chef had been having a three-year affair with his 28-year-old assistant Elyse Tirrell, which allegedly caused the marriage breakdown. Flay's business partner Laurence Kretchmer quickly dismissed the claims, stating that if such an affair had occurred with an employee, he would have known about it. March is now contesting the prenuptial agreement that would limit her alimony to just $5,000 per month from the chef, who is worth approximately $20 million, after Flay filed for divorce earlier this month.

Emma Louise Connolly, girlfriend of Made in Chelsea's Ollie Proudlock, models Ann Summers' first bridal wear lingerie collection, billed as "perfect for every blushing bride" with soft ivory and nude palettes priced between £14 and £85. The mainstream erotic lingerie brand, founded in 1970 and named after secretary Annice Summers, unveiled the loungewear collection featuring curve-skimming dresses, intricate nightgowns, and provocative sets available in sizes 8-12 and 32A-38DD. Emma became the brand's face in February for its Fifty Shades of Grey-inspired Valentine's Day collection before she and Ollie began dating last October.

The UN's World Meteorological Organization (WMO) removed the name Isis from its official rotating list of future Pacific hurricane names, deeming it inappropriate due to its association with the Islamic State militant group (ISIS), despite Isis being the name of an ancient Egyptian goddess that had appeared on the 2016 hurricane roster. The WMO Hurricane Committee replaced Isis with Ivette on the six-year rotating cycle of storm names. The committee also retired the name Odile at Mexico's request following the 2014 hurricane season and replaced it with Odalys for use in 2020.

Game of Thrones fans leaked the first four episodes of season five online before tonight's premiere, with the episodes appearing on torrent sites and downloaded over 550,000 times as of early Sunday, allegedly originating from HBO-approved review copies. Game of Thrones remains the most illegally downloaded program in the world, with episodes from its first four seasons receiving over 7 million illegal downloads between February and April 2015—a 45 percent increase from the previous year that dwarfs The Walking Dead's 5.7 million downloads as the second-most pirated show. HBO implemented simultaneous global premieres across 170 countries and launched HBO Now as a legal streaming option to combat this widespread piracy.

A new ComRes poll for ITV News reveals that 54 per cent of voters do not want to see Nicola Sturgeon in the next UK government, and 59 per cent oppose the SNP calling the shots, despite the party launching its manifesto with demands for supporting a Labour government while offering the rest of Britain a "hand of friendship." Polls in Scotland put the SNP on course to make huge gains and potentially hold the balance of power in a hung parliament, with the party willing to support Labour on a vote-by-vote basis while demanding extra spending, an end to benefits cuts, and the scrapping of Trident. The poll shows the SNP is far less popular than alternative coalition partners, with only 32 per cent backing Nigel Farage's Ukip and 36 per cent supporting the Lib Dems, while older voters prove particularly opposed to SNP influence.

Jeremy Scott's spring/summer 2015 Moschino collection sent human Barbie dolls down the Milan runway to Aqua's iconic 90s hit "I'm A Barbie Girl," prompting journalist Olivia Phillips to test the bold Barbie-inspired dresses in real life by wearing them on Dubai's streets. The experience revealed that the brash, pink collection had surprising mood-enhancing powers, making strangers smile, opening doors, and generating significant social media attention, despite being impractical in a 30-degree desert climate. Ultimately, Moschino taps into universal nostalgia and whimsy, proving the collection successfully embodies the fun and unapologetic nature of contemporary fashion, designed as much for Instagram aesthetics as for actual wearability.

Chelsea are planning a possible victory parade on Monday, May 25, through Fulham if they win the Premier League title this season. Hammersmith and Fulham council has sent letters to residents outlining the planned celebrations and route, which would see team buses depart from Stamford Bridge's west entrance and travel along Fulham Road, Hortensia Road, and King's Road before ending at Eel Brook Common—an event wholly funded by the football club. With Jose Mourinho's side currently nine points clear at the top of the table with six games remaining, the club is preparing these advance celebrations contingent on securing the Premier League title in May.

Jack Henry Doshay, 22, the son of prominent San Diego businessman Glenn Doshay, was arrested last week on charges of kidnapping, false imprisonment with violence, and child cruelty after allegedly attempting to abduct a 7-year-old girl from Skyline Elementary School in Solana Beach. Prosecutors are accusing Doshay of grabbing the girl as she was leaving class on March 23 and attempting to cover her mouth with packing tape, but teachers and parents heard her screams and intervened, causing him to flee the scene. Doshay pleaded not guilty in court Friday with his parents notably absent from the courtroom, and he is currently being held without bail while facing up to 11 years in prison if convicted.

# Summary

Sri Lanka's sports minister Navin Dissanayake has asked Kumar Sangakkara to reconsider his retirement from limited overs cricket and continue playing for another year, following the team's World Cup quarter-final defeat. Sangakkara bid farewell to one-day cricket after the tournament, where he scored four successive centuries and accumulated 541 runs, but Dissanayake believes the newly appointed interim sports body headed by former test cricketer Sidath Wettimuny may convince him to render his service to the country for one more year. The 37-year-old, who will continue playing Tests, has amassed 14,234 one-day runs—second only to Sachin Tendulkar—and will play for English county side Surrey this season.

# Summary

Nicola Sturgeon, the SNP leader, declared that raising the retirement age is unfair on Scottish pensioners because they die younger than those in the rest of the UK, and demanded the retirement age be frozen—a move that would cost billions of pounds. Miss Sturgeon also called for a significant increase to the flat rate pension to at least £160 per week (from the current £113) and insisted that SNP MPs would reject any further increases to the state pension age. The SNP's demands, which would particularly benefit Scotland, are positioned as conditions for supporting Ed Miliband's Labour government in the event of a hung Parliament.

Kentucky Judge Olu Stevens is facing calls to be sacked after he criticized Tommy and Jordan Gray's victim impact statements during the sentencing of Gregory Wallace, who robbed their Louisville home at gunpoint in front of their 3-year-old daughter in 2013. The parents' statements noted their daughter developed a fear of black men following the robbery, but Judge Stevens accused them of fostering racist stereotypes and suggested the mother had attributed her own views to her child. Despite the family's objections, Stevens granted Wallace probation, stating he deserved an opportunity to redeem himself, while his accomplice Marquis McAfee received 10 years in prison.

Mysterious cardboard signs written in black marker pen have appeared all over the affluent Surrey village of Milford near Godalming, sparking speculation among residents about their author's identity. The hand-written messages, including pleas like "please forgive me" and declarations such as "I love you do much please forgive what I said," represent a contrite gesture by a lovelorn writer seeking to win back their partner. This public display of apology echoes a similar incident in Manchester where a man wore a sandwich board asking for forgiveness, showing that some people resort to desperate public measures to prove their love and seek reconciliation.

Five Chinese feminist activists were detained by police for over a month facing charges of "picking quarrels and provoking trouble"—a vague statute increasingly used to jail protesters—after being arrested while preparing to distribute leaflets about sexual harassment around International Women's Day. The women face potential sentences of up to five years if formally charged, prompting international calls for their release from the U.S. and other countries, which prompted China to lodge protests claiming the interference disrespects its judicial sovereignty. The case highlights Beijing's increasingly harsh crackdown on civil society under President Xi Jinping's administration.

More than 10,000 Australians and New Zealanders gathered at Anzac Cove on Turkey's Gallipoli peninsula for a solemn dawn ceremony marking the 100th anniversary of the bloody Gallipoli landings, which began on April 25, 1915 during World War I. Australian Prime Minister Tony Abbott, New Zealand Prime Minister John Key, Prince Charles, and Prince Harry attended the Anzac Day dawn service to honor the thousands of soldiers who fought and died during the eight-month campaign, with leaders later proceeding to the New Zealand service at Chunuk Bair. Speakers paid tribute to the courage and sacrifice of the Anzacs, describing them as the "founding heroes of modern Australia" and emphasizing the unbreakable bond forged between the two nations through their shared sacrifice at Gallipoli.

Atletico Madrid limped to a 2-2 La Liga draw with Malaga on Saturday, with Fernando Torres scoring his first ever league own goal by heading a corner past his own goalkeeper to pull Malaga back into the game before half-time, after the visitors had gifted Atletico the opening goal earlier in the match. Antoine Griezmann scored twice for Diego Simeone's side, including the equalizer in the 77th minute to earn the champions their draw and keep them in third place. Torres has struggled since his January return, managing just four goals for Atletico.

Danny Lennon's interim Scotland Under 21 appointment has officially ended following his successful stewardship of a 2-1 friendly victory against Hungary last month, with Danny leaving to take charge of Scottish Championship side Alloa Athletic until summer 2016. The Scottish FA thanked Lennon for his professionalism and dedication to the role, with a permanent Under 21 coach appointment to be made in due course.

The rusted remains of a bus and train used in the iconic 1993 film *The Fugitive* lie abandoned in the Great Smokey Mountains of North Carolina, where a real train was crashed into a real bus in a $1.5 million action sequence featuring Harrison Ford's character Dr Richard Kimble escaping from a prison transport moments before collision. Using minimal CGI and 13 cameras, director Andrew Davis captured 95 percent real footage of the dramatic scene, which took a 100-person crew two months to plan and resulted in the destruction of three cameras during the 60-second impact. The unremarkable-looking wreckage of the trashed vehicles, still bearing the "Illinois Dept. of Corrections" lettering from the film's plot, has since become a popular tourist attraction over the past two decades.

Greg Gibbins, a 28-year-old rugby league player, was fatally stabbed in an unprovoked knife attack outside a pizza shop in Toukley on Easter Monday morning, with his family now grieving the loss. Police believe the attack may have been related to an earlier incident in which Gibbins helped a woman being harassed; he went into cardiac arrest at the scene and later died in hospital. A 20-year-old man, Bradley James Brooks, was arrested and charged with murder, with detectives continuing to investigate the circumstances surrounding the fatal attack.

A new Reddit thread explored the experiences of men who purchased mail-order brides from Russia and the Philippines, garnering over 10,000 comments as users spilled the beans on what surprised them most when living with their foreign spouses. Many men noted they ordered brides because they struggled socially, reporting that their mail-order spouses treated intimacy and household duties as transactional tasks rather than expressions of genuine affection, with several citing a lack of emotional connection and passion in their marriages. The article also highlights concerning cases where brides experienced depression and abuse, while noting that the mail-order bride industry continues to boom with platforms like Russianbrides.com hosting over one million users.

Joe Hart described Sunday's 4-2 derby defeat to Manchester United—where United's Ashley Young, Fellaini, Mata and Smalling capitalized on their chances to put City to the sword—as one of his worst days in a Manchester City shirt, leaving the club four points behind rivals United and 12 points adrift of Premier League leaders Chelsea and effectively ending their bid to retain the title. The goalkeeper acknowledged City's dramatic loss of form since the turn of the year and emphasized the need to win the massive games ahead to secure third place, while admitting he was at a loss to explain why his side collapsed after dominating the opening 15 minutes. Hart expressed his disappointment at letting down the traveling fans but vowed the team would regroup and go again, declaring he was hurting from the defeat but ready to think it through and return stronger.

A Northern Territory mother and labourer, Noeleen Foster, proved that every cloud has a silver lining—or at least an amusing shape—when she captured a phallic-shaped cloud above Zuccoli, 25km southeast of Darwin, during her work break last week. The mother of four, who frequently photographs clouds, said she had never encountered one with such an X-rated appearance, though she believes others in the area must have noticed the suggestively shaped formation. The discovery follows a similar incident last year when a nature photographer in Derbyshire, central England, captured another famous phallic cloud that gained online attention.

# Summary

Loren Mathieson filmed her pet Boston Terrier, Gizmo, performing a gravity-defying paw-stand walking stunt at home after he tried on a set of new booties. The footage shows Gizmo planting his front feet and launching his back legs vertically in the air, then gracefully walking forward on his front paws before stepping back down to four legs when he reaches Mathieson. According to Mathieson, Gizmo performed this impressive trick because he was unsure how to walk in his new black boots, and the video has since been watched more than 80,000 times.

Porto, the only undefeated side in the Champions League this season, will face Bayern Munich in their quarter-final first leg at the Dragao on Wednesday night, where Porto manager Julen Lopetegui will meet his former Barcelona team-mate Pep Guardiola. However, Lopetegui has warned that despite Porto's impressive record, his side still has room for improvement, insisting the team "hasn't stopped growing" after integrating many new summer arrivals. Both sides are among the tournament's most prolific scorers, with Porto having conceded just five goals while scoring 24, setting up an exciting clash between two of Europe's elite teams.

Nine-year-old Frankie Franz, an academy player with Dagenham and Redbridge Football Club, emulated Barcelona star Martin Montoya's audacious 27-yard trick shot by kicking a ball straight through a basketball hoop placed in his garden on the first attempt, inspired by a viral clip showing the 23-year-old defender lifting the ball into the net at Barcelona's Ciutat Esportiva training ground in Catalonia. Remarkably, Frankie successfully repeated the feat immediately afterward, and he dreams of one day playing for either Real Madrid or Barcelona.

Financially-troubled CSKA Sofia's Latvian goalkeeper Maksims Uvarenko has not been paid for three months since joining the Bulgarian club on an 18-month contract in January, forcing him to ask his parents for money to pay his rent despite expecting a child with his wife. Despite the club's severe financial problems, which have prompted CSKA chairman Alexander Tomov to seek emergency fan donations to avoid expulsion from the Bulgarian top division, Uvarenko remains impressed by the supporters' unique and passionate backing. The debt-ridden club, a 31-time Bulgarian champion that reached major European Cup semi-finals, has struggled financially since tycoon Vasil Bozhkov's departure in 2006, though CSKA visit title-holder Ludogorets on Saturday with local businessmen offering player bonuses for victory.

Pete Bennett, the 2006 Big Brother winner who won £100,000, appeared on the Jeremy Kyle show today to reveal that he squandered his prize money on ketamine and is now homeless. The article traces the varied fates of Big Brother winners from Craig Phillips, the first ever winner from the Channel 4 show, through to escort Helen Wood from series 15, demonstrating how some contestants like Craig have built successful careers while others like Pete have struggled significantly. From Newcastle dancer Anthony, who won Big Brother aged 23 in 2005, to contract manager Aaron from Big Brother's 12th series, the contrasting outcomes show that entering the house aged and young can either make or break contestants' lives and careers.

In a stunning upset, crisis-hit Parma pulled off the biggest surprise of the season by defeating runaway Serie A leaders Juventus 1-0, inflicting only the second league defeat of Massimiliano Allegri's side. Jose Mauri scored the decisive goal on the hour mark after receiving a cross from Ishak Belfodil, while goalkeeper Antonio Mirante made several crucial saves to secure the victory as Parma's team-mates mobbed him at full-time. Despite the impressive win, Parma remains in last place 10 points from safety, while elsewhere in Serie A, Inter Milan beat Verona 3-0 to end their winless run, with Rodrigo Palacio extending their lead after halftime.

Photographer Simon Brann Thorpe created the "Toy Soldiers" project to challenge how people digest images of war through mainstream media by positioning real soldiers from the Polisario Front as green plastic figurines in the Western Sahara desert, a long-disputed region contested between Morocco and an independence movement since the mid-1970s. The project enables viewers to develop their own emotional and political responses upon realizing the images contain real soldiers, not toys, with all locations chosen at historic battle sites. Thorpe hopes his striking five-week production will raise important questions about how images of war will be consumed in the future amid diminishing attention spans; his "Toy Soldiers" book is available through Dewi Lewis Publishing, and you can follow Thorpe on Twitter, Instagram and Facebook.

# Summary

A shocking incident occurred in Pagma Nagar, Bhiwandi City (a suburb of Mumbai), where a seven-year-old boy was raped by two youths and set on fire when he resisted. The boy suffered 30 percent burns to his body but managed to escape and is now being treated at Thane Civil hospital. Police have registered a case under multiple sections of the Indian Penal Code and are working to identify and apprehend the accused.

Jurgen Klopp confirmed on Wednesday that he will leave Borussia Dortmund at the end of the season after seven years in charge, putting England's top Premier League clubs on alert including Liverpool and Arsenal, though Manchester City is unlikely to pursue him as they target Pep Guardiola. Klopp, who led Dortmund to the Champions League final in 2013, stated that he believes the club needs a change of manager and that he is no longer the perfect fit despite having no contact with other clubs and denying plans for a sabbatical. Thomas Tuchel is expected to succeed him at Dortmund, while Klopp remains open to offers from top European clubs before Dortmund's final matches, including a pivotal clash against Bayern Munich on April 28.

Sean McCabe, a 30-year-old security guard from Wales, was diagnosed with stage four Hodgkin Lymphoma in June 2013 and told he had just two months to live, prompting him to plan his own funeral at St David's Church and write a heartfelt goodbye letter to his children. However, in an extraordinary turn of events, his cancer went into remission, and he decided to rebook the same church for his wedding to his long-time fiancee Lisa Williams on July 25, with his children Kian, Summer, and Asia participating in the ceremony. McCabe's remarkable recovery transformed what could have been a funeral into a celebration of his survival and his commitment to the woman he loves.

A family's car hurtled off a parking lot at Berth 73 in San Pedro and plunged into the Los Angeles harbor, landing upside down in 30-foot water. Two boys—one aged eight and the other 13-year-old Abdelkrim Elmezayen—were pulled dead from the vehicle despite immediate resuscitation efforts, while their parents survived and are recovering in the hospital. Firefighter Miguel Meza was hailed a hero by Los Angeles City Councilman Joe Buscaino for diving into the murky water to aid the rescue, as nearly 100 emergency personnel responded to the scene.

Qatar's investment grip on London tightened this week with the purchase of a majority stake in hotel company Coroin, which owns iconic five-star properties including Claridge's, The Berkeley, and The Connaught, while Constellation Hotels also recently acquired London Park Lane Hotel and operates Le Grand in Paris. The Gulf state's expanding portfolio encompasses landmark London buildings such as The Shard, Harrods—purchased by Qatar Holding for a reported £1.4 billion in 2010—Canary Wharf, and Camden Market. Qatar's royal family and state investment arms continue their aggressive expansion into British real estate and major companies, with stakes in institutions like Barclays Bank, Sainsbury's, and British Airways' parent company IAG.

A tragic incident occurred near Melbourne when a grey 2005 Toyota Kluver 4WD driven by a 35-year-old Sudanese mother crashed into a lake at Wyndham Vale in Melbourne's outer west just before 4pm on Wednesday, resulting in the deaths of three children: one-year-old Bol and four-year-old twins Madit and Anger. The children's father visited the crash site to defend the mother, stating she is a "good mother," while a fifth child, five-year-old Awel, survived and remains in serious condition at the Royal Children's Hospital. Police have released the mother after questioning and continue investigating whether the incident was accidental or deliberate, appealing to the community for information about the family's circumstances.

Scientists at MIT in Massachusetts claim that a common computer keyboard could be a powerful new tool in the battle against Parkinson's disease, as a person's keystrokes may reveal important information about their motor skills by analyzing key hold time and typing patterns. Researchers have developed an algorithm that can distinguish between typing affected by sleep deprivation and normal typing, and preliminary results from about two dozen Parkinson's patients suggest it can detect early Parkinson's disease—a significant advance since currently patients are usually diagnosed five to ten years after the disease begins, long after symptoms take hold. The team is now planning larger studies and developing smartphone apps to gather similar data from mobile devices, with potential applications for other diseases affecting motor skills such as rheumatoid arthritis.

# Summary

Liverpool manager Brendan Rodgers held a press conference to preview Saturday's match against Arsenal while addressing Raheem Sterling's recent TV interview, in which the 20-year-old star revealed he is not ready to sign a new £100,000-a-week contract at Anfield. Despite Sterling's admission that he finds interest from Arsenal flattering and his openness to playing abroad, Rodgers insisted that the young England star will not leave Liverpool in the summer and emphasized that the club is a "superpower" that does not need to sell its best players. The manager added that while Sterling's unauthorized BBC interview surprised the club, his focus remains on developing the player's football rather than being concerned about the contract situation.

Stoke boss Mark Hughes has dismissed the latest reports linking Asmir Begovic to Real Madrid, expressing confidence the goalkeeper will sign a new contract after scheduled talks with his representatives this summer. In other transfer business, Hughes hopes to complete the signing of Barcelona winger Moha El Ouriachi, a young talent he plans to develop for future first-team action. With Muniesa and winger Victor Moses sidelined by injury, Hughes remained upbeat about Stoke's momentum following their stoppage-time draw at West Ham, insisting the club still has achievable goals to chase in the remaining matches.

Aston Villa defeated Tottenham Hotspur 1-0 in a Premier League encounter, with Christian Benteke scoring the winning goal in the 35th minute to give manager Tim Sherwood a particularly satisfying victory against the club that sacked him last summer. Villa's intensity and defensive solidity, anchored by the central partnership of Ron Vlaar and Nathan Baker, proved too much for Tottenham as they battled in vain during the second-half fightback, with temporary captain Harry Kane unable to spark a comeback. Sherwood's tactical setup and Benteke's dominance secured three crucial points for Villa's survival bid, sending the Villa faithful into raptures at the final whistle.

Gerard T. "The Frenchman" Ouimette, a former mob enforcer with ties to New England crime boss Raymond L.S. Patriarca, died Sunday at a federal prison in Butner, North Carolina, at age 75 from lung cancer. According to the Federal Bureau of Prison's website and a Rhode Island State Police report, Ouimette was suspected of involvement in up to eight murders and controlled a criminal network responsible for gambling, loansharking, and extortion, though he was never convicted of homicide. In 1996, he became the first New England criminal sentenced to life in prison under the federal "three strikes" provision, and despite his incarceration, he maintained such influence that contraband including alcohol, drugs, and lobster dinners were smuggled in weekly—a reflection of the violent underworld of prison life where assassination squads were assembled over disputes; his body will be returned to Rhode Island for burial.

Danny Cipriani has jetted off to Dubai for a short break after helping Sale Sharks to a 23-6 victory against Gloucester, taking advantage of a rare free weekend before the team's crucial final four rounds of Aviva Premiership action. With Sale out of contention in Europe and currently sitting seventh in the league, Cipriani and Steve Diamond's side are focused on securing a top-six finish to qualify for next season's European Champions Cup. The playmaker has been integral to Sale's improved form this season and will return to action on April 12 when Sale travel to face London Irish.

# Summary

Owen Farrell is hoping to return from a knee ligament injury sustained against Clermont in January to play in Saracens' European Champions Cup semi-final against Clermont Auvergne on April 18. The England fly-half missed the entire RBS 6 Nations due to the injury but reports that his knee is feeling good after completing training exercises including running, kicking, and tackling. Saracens will face Leicester this weekend before taking on Clermont in the semi-final, with a final decision on Farrell's availability to be made after this week's training.

Officer Michael Slager, 33, a North Charleston Police Officer in South Carolina, has been charged with murder after reportedly stopping Walter Scott, 50, over a broken tail light and opening fire on him during the traffic stop. Witness video footage shows Slager shooting Scott in the back as he ran away, contradicting the officer's account, and Slager's patrol car captures the initial interaction before Scott fled and was ultimately shot dead; court records reveal Scott had no active arrest warrant at the time, though he was behind on child support payments, and his family believes this fear prompted him to run, with Scott's family expressing their grief over the tragedy.

Tottenham manager Mauricio Pochettino has expressed confidence that Dutchman Michel Vorm can deliver a solid performance at Burnley on Sunday, despite disappointment at missing the Capital One Cup final. Vorm will deputize for France international Hugo Lloris, who sustained a knee injury in Tottenham's 4-3 victory over Leicester, and Pochettino praised the goalkeeper's professional attitude despite operating behind Lloris in the pecking order. The Spurs boss emphasized that Tottenham's defensive performance must be a collective team effort rather than relying solely on the keeper.

An Uber customer named Jaime Hessel was hit with a shocking $16,000 bill for a 7-mile, 35-minute "trip from hell" across New York after complaining about her unsafe driver who was distracted by his phone, cut across lanes dangerously, and even drove down a bus lane. After Uber initially offered her a $15 refund, the cab-hailing app bizarrely charged her $12,251.49 total, though the company later admitted it was a clerical error and she was never actually charged. Uber has since apologized and provided Hessel with a full refund, though she says she no longer trusts the service with her credit card information.

A cloud formation resembling the global Wi-Fi symbol recently appeared in the sky above Xiangtan city in Hunan Province in central China, creating what could be the world's largest 'Wi-Fi cloud' with coverage that sparked jokes among users. A student from Hunan University of Science and Technology photographed the formation, and the Technology University's Weibo account shared the image with humorous posts about their "giant Wifi Cloud," prompting thousands of shares and comments on Chinese social media, with users suggesting passwords like "moguiyun" (Devil's Cloud) and calling it "God's router." This phenomenon adds to China's history of unusual cloud formations, including UFO-shaped clouds previously spotted in Jilin province and mysterious 'highway' clouds in Hainan province.

Arsenal midfielder Jack Wilshere met Brazil legend Pele on Thursday evening at the 10Ten Talent launch party, tweeting ahead of time that he was "looking forward to meeting @Pele tonight" and would ask fan questions (#JackMeetsPele), then sharing the experience with followers as he interviewed the three-time World Cup winner. Pele reflected on Brazil's disappointing performance at last year's World Cup, describing the 7-1 semi-final defeat to Germany as "a disaster" that nobody could have predicted, though he praised Brazilian Premier League players like Oscar and Ramires for bouncing back this season. The meeting also coincided with the announcement of an "Art, Life, Football" exhibition celebrating Pele's 75th birthday, featuring work from renowned artists including Andy Warhol.

Italy is experiencing a major migration crisis as desperate migrants from Africa and the Middle East attempt to reach Europe by sea, with the Italian coast guard rescuing 8,480 migrants from Friday to Monday and responding to SOS calls from 20 boats in distress on Monday alone. Save the Children fears that approximately 400 migrants may be missing after a ship carrying 550 people allegedly capsized in the Mediterranean near Libya, though the Italian coast guard has not yet confirmed the incident or found evidence of the particular boat sinking. According to the International Organization for Migration, over 10,000 migrants have arrived in Italy in the first three months of 2015, and at least 480 have died crossing the Mediterranean since the beginning of the year due to dangerous conditions and overcrowded vessels operated by smugglers.

Saracens flanker Jacques Burger has been cited for alleged foul play after striking Racing Metro scrum-half Maxime Machenaud during last Sunday's European Champions Cup quarter-final match in Paris, which Saracens won 12-11. The 31-year-old Namibia international faces a disciplinary hearing Thursday before independent judicial officer Simon Thomas, where World Rugby's low-end punishment for such an offence is a two-week ban, escalating to eight to 52 weeks for more serious findings. Saracens return to action against Leicester on Saturday before their Champions Cup semi-final against Clermont Auvergne on April 18.

Chile's Calbuco Volcano erupted for the third time in a week on Thursday, with the explosion producing an extensive plume of ash that spread to Argentina and accumulated nearly 2 feet deep in some areas, prompting the evacuation of approximately 1,500 residents and triggering panic in the tourist towns of Puerto Montt and Puerto Varas. According to eyewitness Gregorio Billikopf, who lives across Lake Llanquihue from the volcano and can see about eight volcanoes on clear days, Thursday's eruption was described as spectacular but less severe than the two previous eruptions on April 22 and 23. The volcanic flows raised water levels in the Río Blanco, and ash contamination forced authorities to evacuate farm animals and salmon from the region, marking the first major eruption since 1962.

Six people, including five men and a 23-year-old woman plus a 26-year-old man, were arrested at Dover port on suspicion of Syria-related terrorism offences, with four of the men from Birmingham in their 20s. The suspects are currently being questioned at a police station in the West Midlands, and West Midlands Police say they do not pose an immediate risk to public safety. The arrests are part of an ongoing investigation, with searches taking place at addresses in Birmingham, and come amid reports that nine Britons were detained in Turkey for allegedly attempting to cross into Syria.

Philadelphia secured their first victory of the season with a 2-1 win over New York City FC, with Vincent Nogueira scoring the dramatic winner in stoppage time after NYC goalkeeper Josh Saunders collided with an attacker in the box; David Villa equalized for City in the 55th minute after starting the move with a back-heel for a classy finish. In other action, Houston ended a lengthy scoring drought to defeat Montreal Impact 3-0 with Giles Barnes opening the scoring from a corner kick, while New York's Lloyd Sam rescued a 2-2 draw for the Red Bulls against DC United with a 90th-minute equalizer, and San Jose beat MLS-leading Vancouver 1-0 in a match that ended with both teams down to 10 men.

A church preacher, Eric Isaiah Adusah, is being held on suspicion of murdering his pregnant British wife, Charmain Adusah, in a hotel room in Ghana, where she was found dead in a bathtub by hotel staff after allegedly lying there for approximately four days. Her husband, a 28-year-old self-proclaimed prophet and evangelical preacher based in London, is said to have left the hotel hurriedly on the day she died and was arrested upon his return to Ghana following the discovery of her body.

Ethan Czahor, a former aide to Jeb Bush fired for offensive tweets, has launched an app called 'Clear' that removes embarrassing social media posts that could damage a user's professional reputation. The app uses IBM's Watson supercomputer and sentiment analysis to scan old tweets for potentially offensive content and rates users on how safe their social media profile is. Research from Stanford and Cornell University shows that algorithms can identify troll-like behavior and antisocial posts in 80% of cases, as trolls tend to use more profanity, make more comments, and post less readable content than regular users.

Justin Rose finished second at the Masters with a 14-under-par total, one of only six times this score has been achieved in Masters history, despite arriving at Augusta National having missed the cut in three of his previous five tournaments. Inspired by his strong finish and eager to follow Rory McIlroy's successful summer of 2014 when McIlroy won major tournaments consecutively, Rose believes the momentum will fuel his pursuit of becoming the first European to win both the US Open and Masters. Although Rose felt he had opportunities to close the gap on winner Jordan Spieth during the final round, he remains confident that his ability to compete in contention at a major championship will drive him forward for the rest of the year.

An unidentified college student at the Texas-based country music festival Chilifest managed to escape punishment for underage drinking after challenging three police officers to a game of Rock Paper Scissors and winning with rock against their scissors. The incident was captured on Vine by user Old Row and quickly went viral with over 500,000 views, as fellow festival goers and Texas A&M students reacted with disbelief on social media to the officers' leniency. The video shows the three officers walking away after their loss, allowing the girl to celebrate her victory without receiving a ticket.

# Missing Teenage Schoolgirl in Melbourne

Sally Gordon-Smith, a 14-year-old schoolgirl, went missing near Melbourne on Friday afternoon after leaving her school in Keilor East around 3pm. Police confirmed she was still missing early Saturday morning and expressed concern for her safety due to her young age. She was last seen wearing blue jeans, a black jumper, and black shoes, and authorities are appealing to the public for any information about her whereabouts.

# Summary

Crystal Palace manager Alan Pardew has dismissed reports linking him with a move to sign goalkeeper Tim Krul from his former club Newcastle, insisting there has been no contact with the player, the club, or his agent. Pardew, who left Newcastle for the south London club in January, maintains he is satisfied with current Eagles goalkeeper Julian Speroni and has no plans to raid his former club. The manager stated that while Palace will be active in the transfer market this summer, they are not currently looking to sign a goalkeeper.

British photographer John Daniels, 61, has captured adorable images of puppies and other young animals posing for picture-perfect photo shoots in his studio, timed to celebrate National Pet Day. Using clean white backgrounds and creative props, Daniels creates heart-warming shots that showcase puppies in charming portraits, often experimenting with unusual animal pairings of kittens, ducklings, and other creatures to produce unique and playful compositions. With decades of experience working in the animal world, Daniels relies on his studio setup, skilled animal handlers, and a philosophy of never forcing animals to perform—instead allowing them to relax naturally—to capture these young subjects at their happiest.

Hilary Border, 54, from Nottinghamshire, stole £20,000 from her dementia-stricken 83-year-old mother Dorothy while neglecting to pay her care home fees as power of attorney. Care home staff attempted to contact Border about an outstanding £16,000 debt over two years before reporting her to police, and she pleaded guilty to fraud. At Nottingham Crown Court yesterday, Border was spared jail with a 16-month suspended prison sentence, along with an eight-month home curfew and 150 hours of unpaid work.

David Suchet, best known for playing Hercule Poirot, is campaigning to improve NHS treatment for rare diseases after his grandson Todd was diagnosed with the incurable genetic illness Tuberous Sclerosis Complex shortly after birth. The condition, which affects Todd—the son of Suchet's fitness coach son Robert—can cause tumours in vital organs, epilepsy, and learning difficulties, but Suchet criticizes "disorganisation" in the NHS for preventing the proud grandfather from securing available medications that could help manage his grandson's symptoms. Through the hashtag #Fight4Treatment, Suchet is urging the public to contact their MPs to pressure the Health Secretary into making these vital drugs accessible through the NHS.

Yorkshire have signed Indian international Cheteshwar Pujara until the end of May, replacing Younis Khan whose contract was cancelled after he expressed his intention to join Pakistan's potential touring party to Bangladesh. Pujara, an outstanding young Test cricketer who made his international debut in 2010 and has six Test centuries, is eager to return to English county cricket after his previous experience with Derbyshire and will join Yorkshire's Division One squad until Aaron Finch becomes available following his Indian Premier League commitments with Mumbai Indians. According to Yorkshire director of cricket Martyn Moxon, Pujara is a highly-rated player looking forward to wearing the white rose and contributing to the club's success.

US Army Staff Sergeant Julian McDonald welcomed 4-year-old Belgian Malinois Layka into his Columbus, Ohio home after fighting for two years to adopt her, fulfilling his promise to the dog who saved his life during a 2012 Afghanistan mission. Shot four times at point-blank range while clearing a Taliban compound, Layka completed the mission with her team before undergoing emergency surgery that resulted in the amputation of her front leg. Despite her injury and military training, the year-old Layka quickly adapted to civilian life and became close friends with McDonald's son, earning her place as a cherished family member and decorated hero.

For decades, medical experts and the federal government have warned Americans about the dangers of excess salt—which contributes to tens of thousands of deaths annually—but new research is challenging this conventional wisdom by questioning whether current salt guidelines have a valid scientific basis and suggesting that consuming too little salt below 3,000 milligrams daily may also pose health risks. While the average American consumes about 3,500 milligrams of salt per day and current dietary guidelines recommend limiting intake to 2,300 milligrams (with an even stricter 1,500-milligram limit proposed for African Americans and people over 50), some researchers argue that healthy people can safely consume up to 6,000 milligrams daily, and that people adhering to current limits may actually experience more heart trouble due to low sodium levels. The debate continues within medical organizations themselves, with the American Heart Association maintaining that current sodium intake is too high, while some former officials contend there is insufficient evidence that reducing salt consumption lowers blood pressure.

A newly released DEA report revealed that several DEA agents took part in alleged sex parties with prostitutes funded by drug cartels in Colombia starting as early as 2001, with lawmakers grilling DEA Administrator Michele Leonhart over the misconduct at a hearing. At least 7 agents admitted to sleeping with prostitutes during overseas assignments, and investigations found repeated allegations of DEA Special Agents patronizing prostitutes while on foreign postings, with seven agents suspended for between two and 10 days. The inspector general's broader investigation uncovered a wide range of sex-related offenses across multiple federal agencies including the FBI, DEA, ATF, and US Marshals Service.

A 36-year-old St. Charles Parish sheriff's deputy named Burt Hazeltine was ambushed and shot three times in the face, elbow, and eye while directing traffic in a school zone on US 90 near Louisiana Street in Paradis, just west of New Orleans, in what authorities described as a road rage incident. The shooting occurred after 56-year-old Paul Devillier, a former TSA worker, allegedly became angry when Hazeltine refused to stop traffic to allow him to make a left turn, and later confronted the deputy at a convenience store where he opened fire.
